Tags: Granddaughter, Daughter, Wedding, Wife, Marriage, Code, Family, Communication
Story Summary: Miles shares a story about his granddaughter "Boppy" who has a secret family code. If he were in ICU and she whispered "Boppy wo", his eyes would light up and he'd say "bring her in". This was a secret family code.
Spiritual Metaphor: Jesus used first-century cultural "codes" that the religious leaders immediately recognized as claims of divinity.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles McPherson shared a personal family story about his daughter's wedding and his 3-year-old granddaughter. She named him "Boppy," and she has a series of unique "Boppy-isms" that only the immediate family understands. One of them is her phrase "Boppy wo," which she uses whenever something exciting or sudden happens.

Pastor Miles explained this as a private, intimate family code. If he were in the hospital on his deathbed in intensive care, and the hospital staff were blocking visitors at the door, but his granddaughter came up and whispered "Boppy wo" at the door, his eyes would immediately light up and he would say, "Bring her in." It is a secret code of immediate recognition.

He explained that Jesus has His own "Boppy code" in scripture. When Jesus said certain things, He was speaking in a first-century cultural code that the people of His day immediately recognized as claiming to be God. Even if a modern reader misses the nuance, the high priest and religious leaders knew exactly what He was saying, which is why they tore their clothes and accused Him of blasphemy.

Illustration Name: The Secret Event at Sports Arena
Tags: Sports Arena, Tony Robbins, The Secret, Criticism, Pharisees, Outreach, San Diego
Story Summary: Miles speaks about when he was invited to speak at "The Secret" (a secular motivational event at San Diego Sports Arena) and preached the gospel. Religious "Pharisees" criticized him on social media for being on the poster.
Spiritual Metaphor: Like Jesus, we must bring our light into dark, secular spaces rather than retreating out of fear of criticism.
Full Story Text:
About 15 years ago, Pastor Miles was invited to speak at a massive secular motivational event called "The Secret" held at the local San Diego Sports Arena, which drew about 12,000 people. Prominent secular and motivational figures like Tony Robbins and Ken Blanchard were also on the speaker lineup, and Pastor Miles was featured on the event poster.

Before agreeing to speak, Pastor Miles verified that he would be allowed to preach the actual gospel, tell people that Jesus died and rose from the dead, and give an invitation to accept Christ as Savior. They agreed, and he spoke.

However, because he was associated with a secular motivational poster, several religious critics and "Pharisees" began criticizing him online, claiming he was turning away from God or associating with a cult. Pastor Miles responded to this by pointing out the purpose of the gospel: we must go into the darkness to bring Christ's light, rather than isolating ourselves to maintain a performance of holiness.

Illustration Name: His Wife's COVID & The White Dove
Tags: Wife, Marriage, COVID, Sickness, Hospital, Dove, Prayer, Holy Spirit, Healing
Story Summary: During COVID, Miles' wife got sick for 12 days and feared she'd die. He yelled his credentials at the curb doctor to get her help. During her sickness and recovery, a white dove landed on his prayer window every single day at dusk on clockwork, bringing peace.
Spiritual Metaphor: The Holy Spirit brings comfort and presence during seasons of total crisis.
Full Story Text:
During the height of the COVID-19 pandemic in August 2021, Pastor Miles and his wife contracted the virus. His wife’s condition was particularly dire due to underlying health complications; she remained bedridden for 12 days and eventually reached a point of despair where she prayed for death. When the time came to admit her to the hospital, the fear that she might not survive was overwhelming.

Overcome with desperation, Pastor Miles found himself crying and shouting at the admitting doctor on the hospital curb. He attempted to leverage every credential he possessed, identifying himself as a former player for the Chargers and a local pastor in a plea for immediate help. Through family connections, he eventually secured access to her room. She began a steady recovery and was discharged after eight days.

Throughout this period of intense stress, Pastor Miles frequently heard a loud fluttering sound outside his prayer window, reminiscent of a mighty rushing wind. He discovered a white dove—a traditional symbol of the Holy Spirit—perched on his roof directly above his place of prayer. The bird arrived every evening at dusk, remained through the night, and departed in the morning. To Pastor Miles, this felt like a divine whisper: "I got you." Once his wife recovered and returned home, the dove stopped appearing.

Tags: Trinity, Father, Son, Holy Spirit, Speech, Breath, Words
Story Summary: Human speech is a metaphor for the Trinity: the Will to speak is the Father, the spoken words are the Son, and the Breath required to carry the words is the Holy Spirit. Spacing and breathing must agree.
Spiritual Metaphor: The Father, Son, and Holy Spirit work in absolute unity and agreement.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles uses the mechanics of human speech as a natural metaphor to explain the functional unity of the Trinity:

The Father represents the will or the thought formed in the mind before anything is said.

The Son represents the physical word that is spoken—the outward expression of the thought, mirroring Jesus as the "Word made flesh."

The Holy Spirit represents the breath required to carry the word out of the mouth and into the world.

This illustration highlights that speech is impossible without breath; one cannot speak and inhale simultaneously. Just as words do not contradict the thoughts that formed them, the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are three distinct persons of one nature, existing in perfect agreement.

Tags: Football, Weight Room, Boise State, Dumbbells, Curls, Resistance, Strength
Story Summary: Miles visited BSU weight room and saw 150-pound dumbbells. He notes that to get physically stronger, you need weights and physical resistance.
Spiritual Metaphor: When we pray for spiritual strength, God provides trials and difficult situations to serve as the "resistance" to build our faith.
Full Story Text:
During a visit to Boise State University in Idaho to support the football team, Pastor Miles entered the athletic weight room. He was struck by the sight of massive 150-pound dumbbells. Joking about the impossibility of curling them, he noted that such weights are far beyond the capacity of an average person.

He observed that an athlete cannot train for that level of strength by simply curling the air. Physical growth requires actual weights and resistance; the muscle fibers must be torn under tension so they can be rebuilt stronger than before.

Tags: Deer, Interstate 15, Freeway, San Diego, Car pool, Roadkill, Running over
Story Summary: Miles saw a deer trying to cross the 14-lane I-15 freeway in San Diego. It had no traffic light or crossing guard and had to jump multiple high concrete walls in active traffic.
Spiritual Metaphor: Trying to navigate life without God is like that deer trying to run across 14 lanes of highway traffic. It leads to spiritual roadkill.
Full Story Text:
While driving on the Interstate 15 freeway in San Diego—a massive 14-lane highway complete with carpool lanes and concrete dividers—Pastor Miles spotted a full-grown deer standing at the edge of the asphalt. It appeared to be preparing to cross.

The situation was impossible; there were no traffic signals or crossings to protect the animal. To reach the other side, the deer would have to survive five lanes of high-speed traffic, scale a concrete wall, navigate carpool lanes, and scale another wall. From his car, Pastor Miles watched the deer in his rearview mirror, thinking, "Deer, don't do it! This is a bad idea, you are going to get run over."

He compared this scene to the way many people attempt to navigate life. They try to cross the high-speed obstacles of life using only their own strength and intuition, without divine guidance.

Illustration Name: The Lion Feeding in the Pen
Tags: Lion, Feeding, Pen, Persecution, Roman, Compromise, Commitment
Story Summary: Miles describes feeding six full-grown lions custard from his hands in a six-acre pen. He contrasts this with first-century Christians being fed to lions in Rome, refusing to deny Christ even unto death, while modern believers compromise over a simple Instagram comment.
Spiritual Metaphor: True commitment to Christ persists under trial. We must find the courage to stand for our faith.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shared an experience where he visited a six-acre animal enclosure containing six full-grown, majestic lions. He and his companions stood in the pen and fed the lions custard from their hands, separated only by a protective screen barrier. He noted that while he was excited to do this, he could never imagine the authorities putting him in that pen not to feed the lions, but to be fed to them as prey.

He compared this to first-century Roman Christians. During the Roman Empire, Christians were regularly fed to wild lions in arenas and Coloseums as public spectacles simply for refusing to deny Jesus Christ. Yet, they refused to compromise their faith, while modern Christians sometimes deny or hide their faith over a minor comment on their Instagram feed.

Tags: Cocaine, Addiction, Birthday, Surrender, Wife, Marriage, Cheating, Opportunity, Healing, Drugs
Story Summary: Miles recounts his drug addiction, smoking marijuana daily since age 16, and permanent deliverance 42 years ago on April 12, 1984. He shares a conversation explaining that living in sin isn't "cheating God"—it's cheating yourself out of the best life He has for you.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's grace is ready to "reverse the curse" of our addictions and mistakes whenever we surrender.
Full Story Text:
Prior to April 1984, Pastor Miles' life was spiraling out of control. He was heavily addicted to cocaine, smoking marijuana daily—a habit he had since age 16—sleeping around, and ruining his relationship with his girlfriend, who is now his wife of 42 years. Despite his outward success as a Chargers football player, he felt hollow inside.

He felt God standing right next to him daily, whispering: "Whenever you're ready... whenever you're ready." Miles kept deflecting, telling God: "Not yet, I need to have more fun," while in reality destroying his own life. Finally, 42 years ago today on April 12, 1984, at 5:00 in the morning, he surrendered his life to Christ and walked away from cocaine and drugs permanently.

He later recounted a conversation with a young man who asked: "If I do drugs my whole life, sleep around, and right before I die, ask Jesus to forgive me, will He?" Miles answered yes. The young man then asked: "Why would God let me cheat Him like that?" Miles responded: "You didn't cheat God. You cheated yourself. Your life was jacked up, and you missed out on an amazing life."

Tags: Spanish, Second-guessing, Truth, Understanding, Mindset, Doubts, Accusations
Story Summary: While learning Spanish, Miles realized he often second-guesses himself, doubting his ability to understand what is being said, though he got it right the first time.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil whispers doubts to make us second-guess God's voice, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy (as a man thinks, so is he). We must trust God's truth.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shared that he is currently learning to speak Spanish because he loves the language. However, he noticed that when listening to native speakers, he often second-guesses himself, wondering if he actually understood what was said. He will hear a sentence, doubt his understanding, and then have to verify, only to realize he actually got it right the first time.

Illustration Name: Doing the Weather on KUSI News
Tags: Weatherman, Green screen, KUSI, Weather, Authority, Sovereignty, Rent, Worry
Story Summary: Miles shares a lighthearted dream of being a weatherman on local San Diego TV, pointing out how simple it is in 70-degree weather. He contrasts human attempts to control the weather with God's effortless, sovereign authority.
Spiritual Metaphor: If God holds the vast universe, we can trust Him to handle our temporal worries (like rent).
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles once went on KUSI, a local San Diego news station. He had always harbored a small, lighthearted dream of being a weatherman—standing in front of the green screen, pointing to the maps, and predicting the forecast. He asked if he could do it, joking: "It's San Diego, how hard can it be? It's 70 degrees outside!"

Tags: Birth, Grandmother, Father, Puppy, Childbirth, Pain, Suffering
Story Summary: Miles' grandmother described giving birth to his father as simple as "boop" with no labor pain.
Spiritual Metaphor: Childbirth pain and physical suffering entered the world as a direct result of the Fall (Genesis 3).
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles' grandmother used to describe how his father, whom they nicknamed "Puppy," was born. She said she was simply standing around, and suddenly, "boop," the baby came out with no labor pain.

Illustration Name: The Stick Figure & The Alien Cartoon (Reverse the Curse)
Tags: Cartoon, Alien, Human, Pride, Submission, Crucifixion, Rejection
Story Summary: Miles shares a cartoon depicting a human and an alien. The alien says they love Jesus and give him cookies when he visits, then asks what the human did when Jesus visited. The human answers: "We killed him."
Spiritual Metaphor: We must stop "killing" Jesus' influence in our lives through our pride, and instead submit to Him.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shared a cartoon he saw on Instagram depicting a stick-figure human talking to a stick-figure alien. The human says: "Oh, you're an alien! Jesus made you guys, too."

The alien responds: "Yeah, we love Jesus! We give him cookies every year when he visits us, we have so much fun." Then the alien asks: "What did you guys do when he came to visit you?"

The human awkwardly replies: "We killed him."

Tags: Conference, Prayer, Sleep-in, Texting, Discipline, Community
Story Summary: Miles met a guy at a hotel conference and agreed to pray. They texted back and forth about sleep schedules, eventually praying at 5:00 AM after the guy asked to "sleep in."
Spiritual Metaphor: Highlights the active discipline of seeking God with your whole heart and making time for connection.
Full Story Text: While attending a conference at a hotel, Pastor Miles met an individual for the first time with whom he had previously only interacted online. Miles proposed they meet to pray the following morning. During their text exchange regarding wake-up times, the individual noted a 4:00 AM rising time, while Miles typically rose at 4:30 AM. However, the individual followed up by stating, "But I want to sleep in tomorrow." Accommodating this request, Miles suggested they meet at 5:00 AM. They eventually met, engaged in conversation for three and a half hours, and spent time in prayer.

Tags: Height, Chargers, Lakers, El Centro, Mission Valley, Reality, Boundaries
Story Summary: Miles notes that he is actually 5'11 and 7/8, though he was listed as 6 feet during football. He uses this to show that reality has distinct boundaries.
Spiritual Metaphor: All physical reality has absolute, defined boundaries and hierarchies established by God.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles explored the concept of "reality" by providing examples of statements that contradict factual truth: suggesting the Chargers still reside in Mission Valley, claiming the Lakers play in El Centro, or asserting that he is exactly 6 feet tall. He offered a personal clarification: "I am 5'11 and 7/8. When I played, they told me I was 6 feet, but I'm 5'11 and 7/8. That is reality."

Illustration Name: Home Depot Tall Ladder & Cold Weather
Tags: Home Depot, Ladder, Tall, Cold, Temperature, Hierarchy, Boundaries
Story Summary: To buy a "tall ladder" or define "cold weather," we must operate on a comparative hierarchy starting from a baseline (the floor or freezing).
Spiritual Metaphor: All reality operates in a structured hierarchy with a beginning, middle, and end.
Full Story Text: If asked to go to Home Depot to purchase a "tall ladder," a person intuitively understands that "tall" is measured from the floor—the beginning—and ascends to a specific height. This understanding requires a hierarchy of measurement. Similarly, the description of weather as "cold" is processed on a temperature hierarchy ranging from freezing to hot.

Illustration Name: The Lifespan of a Man & John Wayne
Tags: John Wayne, Lifespan, Richard Pryor, Cancer, Age, Mortality, Boundaries
Story Summary: The average lifespan of a man is 76.4 years, meaning Miles has exactly nine years left. He references Richard Pryor's story of John Wayne yelling at cancer to "get the heck out of here."
Spiritual Metaphor: Our physical bodies have finite timelines, but God exists on both sides of our lifespan boundaries.
Full Story Text: Observing an elderly man naturally brings to mind the hierarchy of age, spanning from birth to old age. With the current average lifespan of a man being 76.4 years, Pastor Miles noted that he statistically has nine years remaining. He humorously remarked, "I ain't going out like that!" He recalled a story from comedian Richard Pryor about John Wayne's reaction to a cancer diagnosis, where Wayne reportedly told his doctor, "Get the heck out of here. I ain't going out like that."

Illustration Name: Moving Boston Snow vs. San Diego Weather
Tags: Boston, Snow, San Diego, Weather, Seasons, Flip-flops, Shorts
Story Summary: A pastor friend in Boston spent $150,000 just clearing snow out of his parking lot, while Miles was driving in San Diego in 90-degree weather wearing shorts and flip-flops.
Spiritual Metaphor: Environmental seasons operate under a hierarchy easily sustained and balanced by God.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounted a conversation with a fellow pastor in Boston who shared that his church had spent $150,000 during a single winter just to remove heavy snowfall from their parking lot. In contrast, at that same time, Miles was driving in San Diego in 90-degree weather wearing shorts and flip-flops, thinking to himself, "My bad, my bad. Sorry."

Tags: Granddaughter, Boo boo, Band-aid, Tears, Play, Pantry, Attention, Medical kit, Doctor, Family
Story Summary: Miles' 2-year-old granddaughter slipped and cried, claiming her knee was bleeding though nothing was there. She led him directly to the pantry where the band-aids are kept, doing a happy dance once she got what she wanted.
Spiritual Metaphor: God wants us to bring our emotional and spiritual "boo boos" to Him. He is a patient Father ready to hold us and provide comfort.
Full Story Text: While visiting his house, Pastor Miles' two-year-old granddaughter, wearing tap shoes, slipped on the floor and began to cry. Obsessed with medical kits, she often spends her time checking his heart and ears with toy instruments. Following her fall, she insisted her knee was bleeding, pointing at the spot and saying, "It's right here! Right here!" Although there was no visible injury, Miles offered a napkin. She refused, demanding a band-aid. After asking to be held, she led Miles directly to the pantry where the band-aids were stored. As soon as they reached the pantry, her tears vanished and she performed a happy dance, having successfully persuaded him to give her both a band-aid and his undivided attention.

Illustration Name: The Navy Seal on the Plane (Sean)
Tags: Plane, Oklahoma City, Navy Seal, Beard, Grandson, Texting, Prayer, Relationship, Communication
Story Summary: Miles sat next to a tough, quiet Navy Seal on a flight. After starting a conversation, they exchanged numbers, and the Seal ended up texting him weekly, researching his sick grandson's condition, and praying for him.
Spiritual Metaphor: Communication is what transforms a silent encounter into a relationship. Prayer is our active communication with God.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles was on a flight returning from Oklahoma City to San Diego when he found himself seated next to a man named Sean. Sean fit the description of a Navy SEAL: bearded, wearing dark glasses and a baseball cap, and sporting a tight t-shirt. Pastor Miles, always eager to engage with those around him, struck up a conversation that lasted several hours. They shared stories about family, hunting, and shooting, and Miles spoke specifically about his grandson, who was in the hospital at the time. Before landing, they took a photo together and exchanged phone numbers.

A week later, Miles received a surprising text from Sean stating, "I went to church and prayed for your grandson." Miles was stunned because Sean did not initially present as a sensitive or "warm and fuzzy" person. Since that encounter, Sean has continued to text Miles weekly, researching the specific medical condition of Miles' grandson and actively praying for his son.

Tags: Leadership, 360 evaluation, Evaluation, Criticism, Sins, Mirror, Laver, Confession, Humility
Story Summary: Miles describes the brutality of reading anonymous 360-degree leadership evaluations, and the urge to deny flaws. He connects this to the bronze laver in the tabernacle, which was made of mirrors to help the priest see his reflection as he washed.
Spiritual Metaphor: Confession requires us to look into God's mirror and be completely honest about our own unorganized hearts, receiving His cleansing instead of defending our pride.
Full Story Text:
In the corporate world, leaders often undergo a "360-degree evaluation." This process involves employees, peers, and supervisors providing anonymous feedback and ratings. Participants are often given a blank box with the instruction: "Write whatever you want to help them be better." Pastor Miles notes that reading these comments can be a brutal experience, as people often write blunt assessments such as, "He is arrogant, loud, unorganized, and thinks he knows everything."

The natural human instinct when faced with such criticism is denial—a defensive "That’s not me!" However, Miles compares this to the Tabernacle in the Old Testament. The bronze washbasin, known as the laver, was constructed from the polished bronze mirrors of the women. As the priest approached the basin to wash himself, he was forced to see his own reflection. Confession in prayer requires this same level of high self-awareness.

Tags: Childbirth, Labor, Wife, Sickness, Three children, Sins, Pain, Women, Strength, Marriage
Story Summary: Miles' wife's labor times got progressively longer for their three children (12, 24, and 49 hours), and he joked that she had sin in her life. In reality, he realized he could never endure that pain and has immense respect for the strength of women.
Spiritual Metaphor: We must look beyond our own complaints to see the massive burdens others carry, actively praying for them and bearing their loads to fulfill the law of Christ.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles reflects on the births of his three children, noting a peculiar and difficult trend: his wife’s labor times became progressively longer with each child. The first labor lasted 12 hours, the second 24 hours, and the third reached a grueling 49 hours. During the process, he jokingly teased her, saying, "You got sin in your life!"

Through that experience, however, Miles came to a profound realization. He recognized that he could never endure that level of physical pain himself, which instilled in him an immense respect for the strength of women. He realized that many people around us are carrying burdens far heavier than our own, often hidden from view.

Tags: Childbirth, Labor, Sickness, Three children, Sins, Pain, Women, Strength, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Jabez prayed to be blessed and have his territory expanded, not to show off, but so he would not cause pain to anyone, and God blessed him.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our prayers for blessing must be rooted in a pure desire to glorify God and protect others from pain, rather than serving our own ego.
Full Story Text: In 1 Chronicles 4:10, a man named Jabez cries out to God: "Oh, that you would bless me indeed, and enlarge my territory..." Jabez was not asking for an increase in status or wealth for the sake of vanity or to show off to others. His heart was revealed in the second half of his request: he asked to be blessed specifically so that he would not cause pain to anyone. Because his motivation was pure and directed toward the well-being of others and the honor of God, the scripture records that God granted his request.

Tags: Mother, Sickness, Death, Mortician, Wrapping, Linen, Face, Folded, Grieving, Hope
Story Summary: Miles watched a mortician respectfully wrap his deceased mother's body in a linen sheet, asking permission for each part until he reached her face, which Miles realized was the final pronouncement of death. He contrasts this with Jesus folding His headpiece separately, declaring He is not finished.
Spiritual Metaphor: The folded headpiece declares that Jesus' work is not finished. His face is not covered in death; He is alive, active, and continues to speak and minister to us.
Full Story Text:
In 2017, Pastor Miles' mother passed away from cancer. He and his sister took care of her, and he was there in the room when she took her last breath. After her passing, the mortician arrived to prepare her body, and Miles asked if he could go into the room with him.

Miles stood by as the mortician meticulously and respectfully wrapped his mother's body in a linen sheet, asking permission for each part: "Can I wrap her legs? Can I wrap her arms? Can I wrap her torso?"

But then, the mortician paused before her head. He asked: "Can I cover her head and put this over her face?" At that moment, Miles felt a deep chill and thought: "This is the final step. When he does this, it is over." He realized covering the face is the ultimate pronouncement of death.

When Jesus rose from the dead, he left the linen grave clothes behind, but he folded the head/face handkerchief in a separate place by itself. By removing the face cloth, Jesus was declaring: "I am not done. My face is not covered. I still have business to do, people to speak to, and glory to emanate."

Tags: Coma, Easter, Miracle, Roman, Guards, Stone, Resurrected, Coma theory, Cray-cray
Story Summary: Miles mocks the theory that Jesus just fell into a coma. Waking up in a freezing tomb, wrapped in heavy linen sheets, pushing a 2,000-pound stone away, and overcoming Roman guards alone while recovering is "cray-cray" (crazy).
Spiritual Metaphor: Jesus' death was verified by Roman experts, and His resurrection was a genuine, undeniable supernatural miracle, not a medical fluke.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles mocks the secular theory that Jesus didn't actually die on the cross but just fell into a "deep coma" or fainted.

Imagine someone waking up from a deep coma inside a dark, freezing tomb, completely wrapped tight in heavy linen sheets and spices, pushing a 2,000-pound stone away from the entrance, overcoming a guard of Roman soldiers on his own, and walking around saying, "Hey, I'm okay." It's completely "cray-cray" (crazy). Jesus truly died, and His resurrection was a genuine, supernatural victory over death.

Tags: Lazarus, Resurrection, Martha, Grave clothes, Bound, Unraveling, Mortality, Death
Story Summary: When Jesus raised Lazarus, Lazarus came out bound hand and foot and had to be unraveled by others. Jesus, however, did not need anyone to loose Him; He walked out of His grave clothes on His own power.
Spiritual Metaphor: While other biblical resurrections were temporary resuscitations where the person remained bound by mortality, Jesus' resurrection was a permanent, glorious victory over death where He walked out of His grave clothes on His own power.
Full Story Text:
In John 11, Lazarus dies, and Jesus waits four days before arriving. Martha is angry: "If you would have come on time, he wouldn't have died!" Jesus commands them to move the stone and shouts: "Lazarus, come forth!" Lazarus comes out completely bound hand and foot in grave clothes with his face wrapped. He has to be unraveled by others (like toilet paper).

Lazarus was raised but was still bound and needed others to "loose him" because he would eventually die again. Jesus, however, did not need anyone to loose Him. He stepped out of His grave clothes on His own power, defeating death permanently.

Tags: TV news, Wrinkles, Getting old, Decaying, Resurrected body, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: After Miles was featured on the local news, his wife remarked that he looked old and she saw his wrinkles. He notes that our natural bodies are decaying, but our resurrected bodies will be eternal and glorious.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our physical, natural bodies are decaying and getting old, but when we are resurrected in Christ, we will receive an eternal, glorious spiritual body that never decays.
Full Story Text: The day before Easter, Pastor Miles was on the local TV news. When his wife saw him, she remarked: "Oh, you look so old on TV, I saw your wrinkles." He asked her what he should do, and she replied: "Nothing, just let it go."

Tags: Owl, Hooting, Calling out, Neighborhood, Wild owl
Story Summary: Miles hears a wild owl hooting every morning and tries to call back to it, but he never gets any response.
Spiritual Metaphor: We often try to find our own ways to call out to the world, but God is the only one who answers our deepest spiritual calls.
Full Story Text: There is a wild owl in Pastor Miles' neighborhood, and every morning he hears it hooting. He tries to call back to the owl, but he never gets any response.

Tags: Cartoon, Alien, Human, Pride, Submission, Crucifixion, Rejection
Story Summary: Miles shares a cartoon depicting a human and an alien. The alien says they love Jesus and give him cookies when he visits, then asks what the human did when Jesus visited. The human answers: "We killed him."
Spiritual Metaphor: We must stop "killing" Jesus' influence in our lives through our pride, and instead submit to Him.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shared a cartoon he saw on Instagram depicting a stick-figure human talking to a stick-figure alien.The human says: "Oh, you're an alien! Jesus made you guys, too."The alien responds: "Yeah, we love Jesus! We give him cookies every year when he visits us, we have so much fun."Then the alien asks: "What did you guys do when he came to visit you?"The human awkwardly replies: "We killed him."

Tags: Proposal, Ring, Cubic Zirconia, Bling, Marriage, Wife, Promiser, Trust, Family
Story Summary: Miles describes proposing with a tiny 0.00001 carat cubic zirconia ring because he was broke. His wife fainted in excitement. The promise of marriage was only as good as the character of the one making it.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's promises are perfect because He is a perfect Promiser who cannot lie.
Full Story Text:
Over four decades ago, Pastor Miles McPherson prepared to propose to his wife, but as a young man with very limited means, he could not afford an authentic diamond. Instead, he purchased a minuscule cubic zirconia ring, which he playfully described as "0.00001 carats" and "bling upon bling"—long before that term had entered the common vernacular.

When he presented the ring, his future wife had to squint just to locate the tiny stone. Despite the modest nature of the gift, she was so overwhelmed with joy and anticipation that she fainted, requiring Miles to gently wake her.

Pastor Miles used this moment to illustrate that the physical ring was merely a symbol of a deeper commitment. The value of any promise is not found in the token itself, but in the character of the one making it. A promise from a dishonest person is worthless, regardless of its outward appearance. In contrast, Jesus is the perfect promise because He is sent by God—the perfect Promiser—who is holy, immutable, and incapable of lying.

Tags: Proposal, Ring, Cubic Zirconia, Bling, Promiser, Trust, Family, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Miles visited Pelican Bay Supermax prison where guards wear bulletproof vests because isolated inmates make zip-guns out of paper clips and rubber bands. Despite high security, 53% return within three years.
Spiritual Metaphor: Human punishment or education cannot fix a corrupt heart. Only the Gospel can truly change our hearts.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles has visited numerous correctional facilities throughout his ministry, but few left an impression as sobering as Pelican Bay State Prison in Northern California. As a "supermax" facility, it is designed to house the most dangerous and high-risk inmates. The environment is one of total isolation: prisoners live in single-cell units with reinforced steel doors and concrete walls, spending 22 to 23 hours a day in confinement. Even their meals are delivered through a narrow slot in the door.

During a visit to the facility, Miles noticed that the internal guards wore heavy bulletproof vests. He questioned the necessity of such gear given that the inmates had virtually no possessions in their cells. The guards explained that prisoners are capable of constructing improvised "zip-guns" using nothing more than paper clips and rubber bands, which are powerful enough to fire a projectile through a human heart.

Despite this unparalleled level of security, surveillance, and control, the three-year recidivism rate for supermax prisoners stands at 53%. More than half of these individuals return to prison within three years of their release.

Illustration Name: Developing a Friendship with a Liar
Tags: Friend, Liar, Truth, Trust, Integrity, Prophecy
Story Summary: Miles describes starting a friendship with a man who misrepresented the truth twice, making it impossible to continue the relationship.
Spiritual Metaphor: We need a God who is 100% reliable. God is holy, perfect, and keeps every promise with verified precision.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles once recounted an experience where he began to build a close friendship with a new acquaintance. Initially, the relationship was progressing well until the man "misrepresented the truth." Though Miles was confused by the initial lie, he chose to let it slide. However, when the dishonesty occurred a second time, the foundation of the friendship was shattered. The lack of integrity created an insurmountable distance, and the relationship eventually ended.

Tags: Bruce Lee, Death, Rumor, Resurrected, Witnesses, Truth
Story Summary: In 1975, when martial arts star Bruce Lee died, a rumor circulated that he would rise from the dead. It never happened, unlike Jesus' resurrection which is verified.
Spiritual Metaphor: Humans create hopeful rumors that fail, but Jesus' resurrection was a verified reality of fulfilled prophecy.
Full Story Text: In 1975, the death of martial arts icon Bruce Lee left a 15-year-old Miles McPherson heartbroken. In the wake of his passing, a rumor began to spread among fans that Lee would rise from the dead after ten years. Because Miles and his peers so desperately wanted to believe the legend was true, they accepted the rumor as fact. However, as the years passed, the promised resurrection never occurred, and the legend remained just that—a rumor.

Illustration Name: Dan and Ann at Youth Group
Tags: Dan, Ann, Youth Group, Encounter, Marriage, Relationship, Greetings
Story Summary: When Miles was a youth pastor, a simple greeting at youth group between Dan and Ann led to a 40-year marriage.
Spiritual Metaphor: Simple encounters can change your life. We must never take brief, friendly greetings for granted.
Full Story Text: During a Wednesday night youth service at Horizon, Pastor Miles asked the students to turn around and introduce themselves to someone nearby. A young man turned, shook a girl's hand, and introduced himself: "My name is Dan." She responded: "My name is Ann." This brief, simple encounter sparked a connection that eventually led to marriage. They have now been together for 40 years.

Tags: Charlie Kirk, Van Jones, CNN, Text, Civil Conversation, Peace, Unity, Conflict
Story Summary: The day before Charlie Kirk died suddenly, he texted his opponent Van Jones to meet, speak, and disagree gentlemanlike. Van Jones saw the text after Charlie's death and was deeply moved.
Spiritual Metaphor: We are called to rise above cultural divisions, have civil conversations, and love those we disagree with.
Full Story Text: The day before conservative commentator Charlie Kirk passed away suddenly, he sent a text message to his progressive political opponent, Van Jones. The two had been in constant public tension and ideological combat for years. The text read: "Hey, why don't you come on my show and we can talk and just be gentlemen and disagree gentlemanlike." Van Jones did not see the text until after Kirk had passed. He later shared this story on CNN with Anderson Cooper, expressing how deeply he was moved by the gesture of reaching out for a civil conversation despite their differences.

Tags: Hunting, Woods, Monster, Brother, Advesary, Unity, Deception, Perspective
Story Summary: A hunter saw a terrifying "monster" in the woods, dodging behind trees. When the monster finally stepped out right in front of him, he realized it was his own brother.
Spiritual Metaphor: The enemy deceives us into seeing those who look or think differently as "monsters," when they are actually brothers and sisters in Christ.
Full Story Text: A man hunting in the woods spotted what appeared to be a terrifying "monster" approximately 100 yards away. He aimed his gun, but the figure jumped behind a tree. As the figure moved closer, dodging behind rocks and trees, the man's fear grew. However, when the figure finally stepped out directly in front of him, the hunter realized it was not a monster at all—it was his own brother.

Tags: Grim Reaper, Sickle, Death, Reaping, Sowing, Harvest, Consequence
Story Summary: The term "reap" is connected to the Grim Reaper, who carries a large sickle to cut down and harvest souls.
Spiritual Metaphor: Reaping is a natural and spiritual law of harvesting what you have planted. We always reap what we sow.
Full Story Text: The term "reap" in the biblical concept of reaping and sowing is frequently associated in modern culture with the "Grim Reaper." This figure is traditionally depicted in dark, flowing black robes with a hidden face, wielding a large, curved sickle used to cut down and harvest souls at the end of their lives.

Tags: Son, Navy Seal, Pool, Hands tied, Deep end, Push, Safety, Trial, Strength, Father
Story Summary: Miles played a game where he tied his young son's hands, threw him in the pool, and pushed him away from the side to build his strength, ready to save him if needed.
Spiritual Metaphor: A loving father pushes his child past their comfort zone to build strength. God does the same to build our faith.
Full Story Text: When Pastor Miles' son was a young child, they engaged in a "Navy Seal" training game in their backyard swimming pool. Miles would tie his son's hands behind his back and place him in the water. Using a long pool-cleaning pole turned backward, Miles would gently push his son back toward the deep end whenever he attempted to reach the safety of the side. This forced the boy to survive and tread water using only the strength of his legs. While Miles stood on the deck ready to jump in and save him at a moment's notice, his son thrived under the challenge, often shouting, "Let's do it again, I want to be a Navy Seal!"

Tags: Apple, Seed, Multiplication, Tree, Harvest, Generosity, Math
Story Summary: An apple seed grows into a tree producing 300-600 apples annually, yielding over 567 million apples after 30 years of planting seeds.
Spiritual Metaphor: We can count the seeds in an apple, but we cannot count the apples in a seed. Every act of generosity has an infinite, multiplicative harvest.
Full Story Text: Consider the mathematical potential contained within a single apple seed. A typical apple contains approximately six or seven seeds. If planted, a single seed can grow into an apple tree that yields between 300 and 600 apples every year. Over a productive thirty-year lifespan, that one tree will produce roughly 9,000 apples, which collectively contain over 63,000 seeds. Eventually, the lineage of that one original seed has the potential to multiply into 567 million apples.

Tags: Ice Cube, Grind, 5 Years, Discipline, Entitlement, Success, Work
Story Summary: Miles quotes rapper Ice Cube: "If you're not willing to work five years and grind it out... you don't deserve it."
Spiritual Metaphor: We expect overnight success, but receiving God's blessings and fulfilling your purpose requires long-term, disciplined planting.
Full Story Text: The term "reap" in the biblical concept of reaping and sowing is frequently associated in modern culture with the "Grim Reaper." This figure is traditionally depicted in dark, flowing black robes with a hidden face, wielding a large, curved sickle used to cut down and harvest souls at the end of their lives.

Tags: Abraham, Melchizedek, Sodom, Tenth, Tithe, Grace, Honor, Victory
Story Summary: Abraham voluntarily gave Melchizedek (priest-king of Salem) a tenth of his victory spoils out of gratitude, while refusing to take anything from the corrupt King of Sodom.
Spiritual Metaphor: Tithing was established by grace and gratitude long before the law was written, as an act of honor to God as our source.
Full Story Text: After Abraham rescued his nephew Lot by defeating four kings with his personal army of 318 soldiers, he was greeted on his return by Melchizedek (the priest-king of Salem), who brought out bread and wine and blessed him. In response, Abraham voluntarily gave Melchizedek a tenth (tithe) of everything, recognizing that his victory came from God. Meanwhile, the corrupt King of Sodom came out to meet him, but Abraham refused to take anything from him.

Tags: Stolen, Car, Church, Thief, Police, Restored, Security, Trust
Story Summary: Miles' car was stolen from the church parking lot during a service, but returned within an hour after the thief ran a stoplight right in front of a police officer.
Spiritual Metaphor: When we surrender everything to God, telling Him: "All I have is yours," He protects and manages our security according to His sovereign plan.
Full Story Text: Years ago, while Pastor Miles was preaching a service, someone stole his car from the church parking lot. He walked outside and realized it was gone, thinking: "Maybe God wants me to have a new car." However, by the end of the service, his car was back in the parking lot! A thief had driven it out of the lot, run a stoplight right in front of a police officer, and was immediately caught, returning the car to Miles within an hour.

Tags: Children, Pantry, Cookies, Chocolate, Drool, Stealing, Robbing God, Integrity
Story Summary: A pastor friend caught his children in the pantry with cheeks stuffed and chocolate drool on their faces, while they denied eating cookies.
Spiritual Metaphor: Many Christians claim they aren't robbing God, yet they walk around with the "chocolate drool" of withheld tithes and uncommitted resources in their pockets.
Full Story Text: A pastor friend told his children not to eat any cookies. A few minutes later, he heard noises in the pantry. He snuck in to find his children standing there with their cheeks stuffed like chipmunks and brown chocolate drool spilling out the corners of their mouths, while insisting they hadn't eaten any cookies.

Illustration Name: Taking a Youth Pastor Job for $500/Month
Tags: Chargers, Paycheck, Youth Pastor, Mortgage, Bills, Trust, Faithfulness, Money
Story Summary: After retiring from the Chargers making $9,000/game, God called Miles to be a youth pastor for $500/month, though his bills were $1,500. He chose to trust God, and spent three years seeing Him faithfully provide.
Spiritual Metaphor: God wants to make us holy and teach us to trust Him. If He can trust us to be faithful with our money, He can trust us with His kingdom's power.
Full Story Text: When Pastor Miles retired from the Chargers, he was making $9,000 per game. God called him to go into youth ministry. His senior pastor offered him a job paying only $500 per month. Miles' mortgage was $1,500 per month, he had two car payments, three kids, and a wife. God asked him: "Are you going to trust me or count the pennies?" Miles accepted the job within two seconds, and spent three years seeing God faithfully provide for his bills before his paycheck ever matched his expenses.

Tags: Brain, Neurons, Synapses, AMCC, Willpower, Discipline, Sickness, Training, Suffer, Mindset
Story Summary: The Anterior Mid-Cingulate Cortex (AMCC) is a two-and-a-half-ounce brain structure responsible for willpower that physically grows *only* when you do things you do not want to do.
Spiritual Metaphor: Obeying God and pushing through spiritual resistance (like fasting or prayer) physically builds our spiritual willpower and longsuffering (James 1:2).
Full Story Text:
The human brain is a marvel of biological engineering, containing approximately 86 billion neurons and 100 trillion connections. Within this complex network exists a specific two-and-a-half-ounce structure known as the Anterior Mid-Cingulate Cortex (AMCC). This region serves as the seat of willpower and discipline.

Neuroscientific research has revealed a unique characteristic of the AMCC: it grows larger and more robust exclusively when an individual engages in activities they find undesirable or difficult. Whether it is choosing to run in freezing temperatures, studying through exhaustion, or adhering to a fast, the act of pushing through resistance physically expands this part of the brain. Conversely, in individuals characterized by laziness or a lack of discipline, the AMCC is notably smaller.

Tags: Scrips Ranch, Jacuzzi, Millionaire, Homeless, Fear, Lies, ANTs, Mindset
Story Summary: Miles sat in a jacuzzi with a wealthy man who owned a 3,200-sq-ft home but confessed a constant fear of ending up homeless, despite having friends who would easily help him.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil is a master liar who whispers ridiculous, unfounded "automatic negative thoughts" (ANTs) into our heads to paralyze us with fear.
Full Story Text:
While visiting Scrips Ranch, Pastor Miles sat in a jacuzzi with a man of significant worldly success. The man owned a 3,200-square-foot home and earned a high six-figure income. Despite these clear markers of security, the man confessed that he lived in constant, paralyzing fear of becoming homeless.

Miles highlighted the irrationality of this thought by noting that the man possessed multiple wealthy friends who would immediately provide him with a home should he ever face a true crisis. His immense wealth and social support system stood in stark contrast to the persistent, unfounded terror in his mind.

Tags: REM sleep, Dreams, Paralysis, Half-awake, Fear, Captive, Thoughts, Mindset
Story Summary: During REM sleep, our brains paralyze our physical muscles so we don't act out our dreams. Sometimes, waking up half-awake in this state causes a terrifying feeling of paralysis.
Spiritual Metaphor: We must take active command of our thoughts. When unbiblical thoughts enter our minds, we must "take every thought captive" and tell our minds to align with God's truth.
Full Story Text:
During the REM (Rapid Eye Movement) cycle of sleep, the body enters a state known as REM paralysis. This is a biological safeguard designed by God to keep our physical muscles immobile while we dream, preventing us from physically acting out our internal narratives. Without this paralysis, the consequences of acting out dreams could be disastrous or even fatal.

Occasionally, an individual may regain partial consciousness while this paralysis is still active. This results in a terrifying sensation of being wide awake yet unable to move, speak, or cry out, even when sensing a perceived danger in the room.

Tags: The Third Option, Race, Book, Intervies, CNN, TMZ, Fear, Peace, Trust
Story Summary: When writing his book on race, the devil whispered to Miles weekly that he would destroy his church. By preparing his heart and trusting God, his heart was at peace during 100 intense interviews.
Spiritual Metaphor: Trusting God does not mean difficult circumstances will disappear, but it changes your heart so you are prepared to stand strong in the fire.
Full Story Text:
Leading up to the release of his book The Third Option—a work focused on racial unity—Pastor Miles faced intense mental opposition. For a year and a half, he heard a persistent internal whisper: "You are going to destroy your church with this book." The timing of the release coincided with the national unrest of 2020, heightening his anxiety.

However, once the book was released, Miles entered a season of high-pressure engagement, including over 100 interviews with major outlets such as TMZ and CNN. Despite the intensity of the questions and the global scrutiny, the "destruction" he feared never materialized. Because he had prepared his heart and surrendered his fear to God, he experienced a profound internal peace. The external circumstances were still "fire," but his internal condition had changed.

Illustration Name: The Shadow of the Big Truck
Tags: Truck, Shadow, Worry, Fear, Testimony, Control, Trust, Mindset
Story Summary: Miles describes seeing a giant shadow of a truck sweep over his car, making him think he was going to get hit, until a quiet voice told him it was just a shadow.
Spiritual Metaphor: We are often terrified of the *shadow* of death (financial worry, relationship conflict, health struggles) rather than death itself.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles was driving to church on a sunny morning. Because the sun was low, it cast long shadows. As he slowed down to take his exit, he heard a massive truck rolling up behind him very loudly. The giant shadow of the truck swept completely over his car. In that split second, his mind panicked, telling him the truck was hitting him. But then, a quiet voice whispered: "Be calm, it's just the shadow."

Tags: Dishwasher, Dishes, Grease, Cleaning, Surrender, Pride, Grace, Family, Wife
Story Summary: Miles was confused when his wife told him to rinse dishes before putting them in the dishwasher.
Spiritual Metaphor: We think we need to clean ourselves up before coming to God. But He is the dishwasher—He cleans us, we just need to surrender.
Full Story Text: When Pastor Miles and his wife first got a home dishwasher, Miles put the dirty dishes straight in. His wife told him he needed to wash the dishes off first. Miles was confused, thinking that was the dishwasher's job. He felt that since they had paid money for the appliance, it should do the work itself.

Tags: Dishwasher, Dishes, Grease, Cleaning, Surrender, Pride, Grace, Family, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: A little boy tried desperately to reach a book on the very top of a bookshelf but was too short. His father walked in and reached it effortlessly.
Spiritual Metaphor: The standards of Heaven require absolute perfection, and no matter how hard we try, we cannot reach it. So God the Father came down to us in Jesus Christ.
Full Story Text: A little boy was trying desperately to reach a book on the very top of a high bookshelf. He jumped, climbed on the shelves, and tried everything he could, but he was simply too short to reach his goal. Then, his tall father walked into the room, reached up effortlessly, and brought the book down to him.

Tags: Daniel, Nebuchadnezzar, Dream, Astrologers, Magicians, Plan, Purpose
Story Summary: Nebuchadnezzar demanded his astrologers tell him both what he dreamed and what it meant. Only Daniel, trusting God, prayed and received the answer.
Spiritual Metaphor: Unlike false gods, our God is a personal, active, and conversational helper who has a specific, structured plan for our lives.
Full Story Text: In the biblical narrative found in the Book of Daniel, King Nebuchadnezzar was plagued by a terrifying dream that he could not comprehend. In a display of absolute power and desperation, he demanded that his astrologers and magicians not only interpret the dream but first describe the dream itself to prove their power. Failure to do so would result in their execution. These advisors protested, claiming that such knowledge was impossible for men and resided only with the gods. However, Daniel, a servant of the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, turned to prayer. God revealed both the dream and its intricate meaning to Daniel, who then shared it with the King, ultimately sparing the lives of the advisors.

Tags: Frog, Kettle, Boiling, Temperature, Deception, Mindset, Culture
Story Summary: A frog in cold water on a stove will stay and boil to death because the temperature increases so gradually it doesn't recognize the danger.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil masterfully, gradually deceptions our cultural leaders, normalizing moral decay so slowly that we don't realize the spiritual danger until it is too late.
Full Story Text: A biological observation often cited is that if a frog is placed directly into a pot of boiling water, it will immediately jump out to save itself. However, if the frog is placed in a pot of cold water and the heat is increased at a very gradual pace, the frog will remain in the water. Because the change in temperature is so incremental, the frog fails to recognize the mortal danger until it is eventually boiled to death.

Illustration Name: The Dallas Airport & School Board Meeting
Tags: Dallas, School board, Activist, Pornography, Kids, Ministry, Outreach
Story Summary: An activist showed Miles a children's cartoon book from a school library containing explicit pornography, highlighting the battle for the next generation.
Spiritual Metaphor: Represents the urgent spiritual battle facing the next generation. Our children are targeted, and only the Gospel can truly change their hearts.
Full Story Text: While traveling to the airport in Dallas alongside other conference speakers, Pastor Miles McPherson encountered a stark reality of modern cultural shifts. One of his companions, an activist who frequently attends school board meetings to challenge curriculum content, passed a book from the front seat to the back for Miles to see. The book, a cartoon intended for children and found within a school library, contained graphic and explicit pornography. The discovery was deeply shocking and served as a firsthand look at the materials currently accessible to youth.

Tags: College, Chargers, Rams, NFL, Draft, Flyers, Scouts, Trust, Plan, Career
Story Summary: Miles, from a small school in Connecticut, was stressed for months before being drafted, cut, and eventually landing with the San Diego Chargers.
Spiritual Metaphor: We believe a specific person or circumstance holds our future, but our hope and future are held solely by God.
Full Story Text: In 1982, Pastor Miles was finishing his senior year at the University of New Haven, a small division-two school in Connecticut. He harbored a deep desire to play in the NFL, yet his school was virtually invisible to professional scouts. To bridge this gap, his coach resorted to sending physical paper flyers by mail to various teams. Through this persistence, five scouts eventually arrived to watch film and conduct agility tests. One scout provided a spark of hope, stating, "Based on our information, your times match up with the top guys. Your future is bright." Despite the initial encouragement, Miles endured months of intense stress before being drafted by the LA Rams. After being cut, he eventually found his place with the San Diego Chargers.

Tags: Youth Group, Pregnant, Teenager, Confusion, Virgin Birth, Miracle, Holy Spirit
Story Summary: A pregnant high school girl in Miles' church claimed she didn't know how it happened, which Miles laughed at.
Spiritual Metaphor: Highlights the contrast of Mary's virgin birth, which was a genuine, supernatural impossibility achieved solely by the Holy Spirit.
Full Story Text: During his time as a youth pastor, Miles encountered a high school girl from his church who came to him with the news that she was pregnant. During their conversation, she claimed, "I don't know what happened!" Miles reacted with laughter, responding, "I do, for sure! I know exactly what happened."

Tags: Golf, North County, 16th Hole, Warm-up, Driving Range, Swing, Rhythm, Discipline, Consistency
Story Summary: Running late for a golf game, Miles played the final three holes without a warm-up, hitting the ball horribly, before going to the driving range to get his swing back.
Spiritual Metaphor: You cannot just "warm-up" with God on Sundays and expect a powerful spiritual life; walking by faith requires daily, consistent rhythm.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles was running late for a golf game in North County due to a series of church meetings. By the time he arrived, his friends had already reached the 16th hole. Rather than missing out entirely, he decided to jump in for the final three holes—16, 17, and 18—without any opportunity to warm up. The result was a series of terrible shots that traveled only 3, 4, or 10 feet. As his friends teased him for his lack of preparation, Miles felt the frustration of his poor performance. Immediately after the game, instead of joining his friends for a meal, he went straight to the driving range. He spent twenty minutes working out the "swing demon" to regain his rhythm and feel for the game.

Tags: Montana, Whitefish, Cold Plunge, Freezing Lake, Key card, Community, Endurance
Story Summary: In Whitefish, Montana, Miles stood in a freezing 39-degree natural lake for 15 minutes because he was with a group of 15 other trainers doing it together.
Spiritual Metaphor: Doing things in community distracts us from the physical pain of trials, encouraging us to go much further than we ever would on our own.
Full Story Text:
In his personal routine, Pastor Miles regularly engages in cold plunges at home, typically immersing himself in 47-degree water for three minutes. However, a significant challenge arose during a conference in Whitefish, Montana. The environmental conditions were extreme: the air temperature was 32 degrees and snowing, and the agenda called for a plunge into a natural lake where the water temperature was a freezing 39 degrees.

Feeling terrified by the prospect, Pastor Miles admitted to hoping his room key would fail, providing a convenient excuse to avoid the ordeal. Despite his hesitation, he retrieved his suit and joined the group at the water's edge. He ultimately managed to stand in the freezing lake for 15 minutes.

Tags: Conference, sdpd, Faith community, Gravity, Oxygen, Love, Trust, Perspective
Story Summary: Miles challenged a moderator's label of "faith community" by pointing out that everyone has faith—in gravity, oxygen, money, or love.
Spiritual Metaphor: We are all designed to live by faith; the only question is where you put your trust.
Full Story Text: During a civic gang conference in San Diego, a moderator introduced Pastor Miles as a representative of "the faith community." Upon taking the stage, Miles offered a correction to the moderator’s terminology. He stated, "We all belong to the faith community. You have faith in law enforcement; others have faith in education or money. We are all creatures of faith."

Illustration Name: Abraham and Sarah "Good as Dead" Childbirth
Tags: Abraham, Sarah, Laughed, Baby, Childbirth, Age, Mortality, Miracle, Covenant, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: God promised Abraham (100) and Sarah (90) a baby. Though Abraham was "as good as dead" physically, they trusted God and a massive lineage was born.
Spiritual Metaphor: Out of biological impossibility and mortal limits, God brings infinite life and legacy to those who trust His covenant.
Full Story Text:
Faith is defined as the substance of things hoped for. It is a universal human experience rather than a niche religious category. We demonstrate faith in oxygen every time we take a breath, faith in gravity whenever we walk, and faith when we give our hearts to another person with the hope that they will not break it. Every person is designed to live by faith; the fundamental question is not whether we have faith, but where we choose to place our trust.

Illustration 3: Abraham and Sarah "Good as Dead" Childbirth

Tags: Richard Pryor, Late Show, Club, Entitlement, Effort, Expectation, Fasting, Discipline, Pride
Story Summary: Miles references Richard Pryor's comedy skit about people who show up late but expect to get in, comparing it to Christians who expect blessings without spiritual effort.
Spiritual Metaphor: Blessings are not about entitlement; we must actively seek and pursue them through prayer and fasting.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles referenced a famous comedy skit by Richard Pryor about people who show up late to a club or a show but still arrogantly expect to get in just because of who they are, saying: "I'm here, dressed up, so let me in."

Tags: NFL, Workshop, Evangelist, Calling, Empty Room, Preparation, Attention, Career
Story Summary: Over 30 years ago, Miles was the only player who showed up for an NFL conference workshop on evangelism, gaining the speaker's full attention and clarifying his calling.
Spiritual Metaphor: What seems like an insignificant, empty room can be the exact place where God sharpens your primary spiritual calling.
Full Story Text: Over 30 years ago, Pastor Miles attended an NFL conference. He walked into a workshop on evangelism and found that he was the only player who showed up. The speaker was discouraged, but Miles was thrilled because he received the speaker’s full, undivided attention. That single, private workshop ignited his calling as an evangelist.

Sermon Title: We Need To Talk: The Holy Spirit
Tags: Texas, Partners, Construction, Vision, Negotiator, Project Manager, Father, Son, Holy Spirit, Trinity
Story Summary: A Texas construction company had three equal partners: the visionary, the negotiator, and the project manager who did the physical building.
Spiritual Metaphor: This models the Trinity: the Father plans, the Son negotiates/signs the contract, and the Holy Spirit does the work of internal transformation.
Full Story Text: In Texas, a highly successful Land Development and Construction company operated through the synergy of three equal partners. Each partner held a distinct, essential role in the execution of their projects. The first partner served as the visionary, the one who conceptualized the master plan for large tracts of land, such as 300 or 500-acre plots. The second partner acted as the negotiator, handling the legalities, drafting the contracts, securing the financing, and officially signing the agreements. The third partner functioned as the hands-on project manager. This individual worked directly in the field, overseeing the physical construction to ensure every detail was built according to the original design.

Sermon Title: We Need To Talk: The Holy Spirit
Tags: NYPD, Detective, Decoy, Surveillance, Father, Ride-along, Child, Protection, Battle, Security, Family
Story Summary: Miles' father, an NYPD detective, took him as a decoy on ride-alongs and had him walk down alleys to check suspects, promising he was right behind him.
Spiritual Metaphor: When the Holy Spirit leads us into difficult assignments or spiritual battles, our loving Heavenly Father is always positioned right behind us, securing our victory.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles' father served as an NYPD patrol officer and detective, specifically tasked with arresting corrupt police officers. During Miles’ youth, his father would occasionally take him on "ride-alongs," utilizing him as a decoy for surveillance operations. His father would instruct him to walk down a street and look into a specific doorway to confirm if a suspect was present. Miles recalled feeling terrified by these assignments, questioning if he was being put in harm's way and asking his father, "Are you coming with me?" His father would always offer the same assurance: "I'll be right here behind you." While Miles was the one walking toward the potential danger, his father was positioned nearby, watching and ready to intervene.

Tags: Football, Grandson, Routes, Out of Breath, Reality, Illusion, Pride, Family
Story Summary: Miles tried to run routes with his 9-year-old grandson, believing he still had his NFL-level speed, but was completely out of breath in five routes.
Spiritual Metaphor: We frequently hold a false, inflated perception of our own goodness or strength. We need God's presence to show us our true reality.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson recalls a moment when he went out to play football with his 9-year-old grandson. In Miles' mind, he still held the self-image of a professional athlete, believing he could run routes and throw with the same proficiency as he did during his NFL days. However, the physical reality was quite different. After attempting to run just five short routes, he found himself completely out of breath. Furthermore, the athletic gap was made clear when his grandson was able to throw the ball a greater distance than Miles could throw it back to him.

Tags: Rats, Water, Swimming, Hope, Survival, Rescue, Fainting, Lifegiving
Story Summary: Rats treading water fainted and began sinking at 15 minutes. But after being rescued, dried, and rested, they swam for 60 hours in a second test because they held hope of a rescue.
Spiritual Metaphor: Hope is a powerful, lifegiving force. Jesus is our eternal hope born to rescue us from our spiritual drowning.
Full Story Text: In a scientific study, researchers placed rats in a tub of water to observe how long they could tread water before they succumbed to exhaustion. On average, the rats fainted and began to sink at exactly 15 minutes. At that critical moment, the scientists intervened: they pulled the rats out, dried them off, and allowed them to rest for 10 minutes. When the rats were placed back into the water for a second time, their performance changed dramatically. Instead of lasting another 15 minutes, the rats continued to swim for 60 hours—a total of two and a half days—before they finally tired.

Tags: Boston, Pizza, Flight, Sickness, Wife, COVID, Prayer, Friendship, Faithfulness, Marriage
Story Summary: While Miles' wife was hospitalized with severe COVID, his close friend Matthew flew from Boston to San Diego just to deliver pizza and pray with him every three hours.
Spiritual Metaphor: God expresses His absolute faithfulness and love through the sacrificial actions of His people. A true friend is willing to go to extreme lengths to carry your burden.
Full Story Text:
During a period when Pastor Miles' wife was hospitalized with a severe case of COVID, a close friend and fellow pastor named Matthew called from Boston to offer his support. When asked how he could help, Miles responded with a joke, noting his East Coast roots and requesting "some good pizza."

Unexpectedly, three days later at 9:00 PM, Matthew appeared at Miles' door in San Diego. He had traveled across the country specifically to deliver the pizza and offer spiritual support in person. Matthew remained for two days, during which time the two men maintained a dedicated prayer vigil, praying together every three hours according to the clock.

Sermon Title: Smell The Roses – How To Love Yourself!
Tags: Boston, Pizza, Flight, Sickness, COVID, Prayer, Friendship, Faithfulness, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Sitting in a restaurant, Miles began studying his own hands and birthmark, realizing we get so busy in our routines that we forget to appreciate how God uniquely made us.
Spiritual Metaphor: Loving yourself is a holy, biblical act of appreciating God's design in you and realizing you are fearfully and wonderfully made in His image.
Full Story Text: During a quiet moment of reflection at a restaurant, Pastor Miles McPherson began to observe the details of his own physical being. He spent time studying his hands, noting that they had been with him through every experience for sixty years. He specifically observed a unique birthmark on his arm, a feature he vividly remembered seeing as a young child. This moment of pause led to the realization that many people become so consumed by the cycle of "working, working, working" that they neglect to stop and truly appreciate the intentionality of how God created them.

Sermon Title: Smell The Roses – How To Love Yourself!
Tags: Talents, Money, Parable, Burying, Fear, Faith, Abundance, Stewardship
Story Summary: In the Parable of the Talents, two servants invested their talents and doubled their return, while the third buried his talent in the ground out of fear and lost everything.
Spiritual Metaphor: God has given each of us distinct natural and spiritual talents. Burying them out of fear of failure brings spiritual torment, but investing them out of faith opens the door to infinite joy.
Full Story Text: The Parable of the Talents, found in Matthew 25, describes a master who entrusts his servants with various resources before departing on a journey. Upon his return, the master evaluates how each servant managed what they were given. The narrative demonstrates that the master has a clear expectation for his servants to actively invest and grow the resources placed in their care, rather than simply preserving them.

Tags: Hotel, Rock Climbing, Crevices, Strongholds, Lies, Claws, Mindset, Handhold
Story Summary: Miles describes climbing an artificial wall and realizing he must grab and step on tiny crevices designed to hold him. He compares this to the devil finding half-truths to latch lies onto.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil latching lies to half-truths to build strongholds, which we must take captive.
Full Story Text: During a stay at a hotel, Pastor Miles McPherson attempted to climb an artificial rock climbing wall for the first time. Under the guidance of an instructor, he learned the importance of weight distribution and specific body techniques. He observed that progress toward the summit was only possible by identifying and utilizing tiny artificial crevices. These small grips, though seemingly insignificant, are specifically designed and securely fastened to support a person's entire weight.

Illustration Name: The Scripps Ranch Millionaire's Homeless Fear
Tags: Scripps Ranch, Jacuzzi, Millionaire, Homeless, Fear, Lies, ANTs, Mindset
Story Summary: Miles sat in a jacuzzi with a wealthy man who owned a 3,200-sq-ft home but confessed a constant fear of ending up homeless, despite having friends who would easily help him.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil is a master liar who whispers ridiculous, unfounded "automatic negative thoughts" (ANTs) to paralyze us with fear.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts a conversation held in a jacuzzi with a wealthy friend living in the Scripps Ranch community. This individual possessed significant financial security, earning a high six-figure salary and owning a 3,000-square-foot home complete with a swimming pool. Despite this outward prosperity, the friend confessed to a persistent and deep-seated anxiety: the fear of becoming homeless. Pastor Miles countered this irrationality by noting that the friend had dozens of wealthy associates who would personally ensure he had a home if he ever faced hardship, illustrating the total lack of factual basis for the fear.

Tags: Reading, Dark Room, Light Switch, Blindness, Revelation, Wife, Sin, Marriage
Story Summary: Miles was reading in a dark room and insisted he could see fine. His wife turned on the light, and he was shocked to see how dark it actually was.
Spiritual Metaphor: We think we are good people whose lives aren't that bad, staying blind until God's light shines on our need for a Savior.
Full Story Text: Miles was reading in a room for several hours as the sun set. His wife walked in and told him it was pitch black and he needed to turn on the light. Miles insisted he could see fine and didn't need the light. She kept asking until he gave in. She turned the light on, and Miles was shocked to realize just how dark the room actually was.

Illustration Name: The Demonic Goo Monster on the Front Pew
Tags: Reading, Dark Room, Light Switch, Blindness, Revelation, Sin, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: A demonic creature covered in green goo slammed into a church. The pastor calmly said he wasn't scared because he had been married to the demon's sister for 20 years.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil and his demons try to terrorize us, but we do not need to fear because Jesus conquered them on the cross.
Full Story Text: A pastor was preaching in a small church when a massive, terrifying demonic creature (covered in green goo and dripping wet) slammed open the back doors. People panicked, screamed, and jumped out of windows. The creature stomped down the center aisle, sat on the very front pew, and roared at the pastor: "Are you not scared of me?" The pastor looked at him calmly and replied: "No, I'm not. I've been married to your sister for 20 years, what can you possibly do to me?"

Illustration Name: Marshawn Lynch "I'm Just About That Action"
Tags: Seattle Seahawks, Super Bowl, Marshawn Lynch, Beast Mode, Deion Sanders, Action, Obedience, Baptism
Story Summary: Star running back Marshawn Lynch refused to talk to the press, telling Deion Sanders: "I'm just about that action."
Spiritual Metaphor: There is a massive difference between just talking about Jesus and actually living for Him out of action and obedience.
Full Story Text: After winning the Super Bowl in 2014, Seattle Seahawks star running back Marshawn Lynch (nicknamed "Beast Mode") sat at the interview podium and refused to answer any questions. Commentator Deion Sanders ("Prime Time") pulled him aside and asked why he didn't like to talk to the press. Lynch gave a legendary one-sentence answer: "I'm just about that action."

Illustration Name: "Because I Said So" & The Coffee Table
Tags: Levi, Coffee Table, Feet, Kids, Obedience, Role Model, Parenting, Family
Story Summary: Miles' son Levi put his feet on the table. Miles told him not to, and Levi pointed out Miles doing it. Miles said "Do what I say, not what I do!"
Spiritual Metaphor: Unlike hypocritical parents, Jesus never just says "because I said so." He went first and modeled everything He asks of us.
Full Story Text: Miles' four-year-old son Levi likes to put his feet on the coffee table. Miles tells him: "Levi, don't put your feet on the table." Levi notices Miles immediately putting his own feet up on the table, and points it out. Miles tells him: "Do what I say, not what I do! Because I said so!"

Tags: Ice Bath, Freezing, Athletes, Inflammation, Anxiety, Hesitation, Commitment, Submerge, Baptizo
Story Summary: Athletes use cold ice baths for physical benefits, but many stand at the edge hesitating because it is too cold.
Spiritual Metaphor: We want spiritual benefits (peace, joy, healing), but refuse to fully submerge ourselves (tithe, serve, share faith) into Christ's call.
Full Story Text: Professional athletes use cold ice baths (submerging their bodies in freezing water) because it has immense physiological benefits: reducing inflammation, lowering heart rate, relieving anxiety, and improving sleep. People know the benefits, but they stand at the edge of the tub hesitating: "It's too cold! I don't want to get in!"

Illustration Name: Grandson's "Elf on the Shelf" and Birth
Tags: Grandson, Elf on the Shelf, Reindeer, Santa, Myths, Incarnation, Eternity, Family
Story Summary: Miles' grandson asked about his elf on the shelf and if his pregnant mother could "pop out" a baby, leading to a discussion about Sarah having a baby at 90.
Spiritual Metaphor: Christmas myths distract us from the real historical truth that Jesus is an eternal God who existed before the world's foundation.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shares a personal story regarding his eight-year-old grandson, who recently acquired an "Elf on the Shelf." This toy is a popular contemporary tradition where an elf is placed around the home to observe if children are behaving for Santa Claus. During a visit, the grandson asked Miles if he had an elf at his own house. Miles explained that he did not, as there were no small children living there.

The conversation took an inquisitive turn when the grandson noted his mother’s pregnancy and asked Miles, "But my mommy is pregnant, can you just pop out a kid?" His grandmother had to explain the biological realities of age, though Miles humorously noted that in the biblical account, Sarah bore a child at the age of 90.

Illustration Name: Abraham's Mistress Hagar in the Desert
Tags: Abraham, Hagar, Desert, Ishmael, Barren, El Roi, God Who Sees, Mistress, Sickness, Family, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Abraham had a child with Hagar, but jealousy arose and Hagar fled to the desert to die. The Angel of the Lord met her there, revealing Himself as El Roi (The God Who Sees Me).
Spiritual Metaphor: God sees you and knows your deepest crisis. He is a personal, active provider who meets us in our desert when we surrender.
Full Story Text:
The story of Hagar originates from a time of deep personal crisis. When Sarah found herself unable to conceive, she instructed Abraham to have a child with her servant, Hagar. However, once Hagar became pregnant, Sarah was overcome with intense jealousy. This conflict became so severe that Hagar fled into the harsh conditions of the desert, essentially waiting to die.

In that place of isolation and despair, she was met by the Angel of the Lord—identified as a pre-incarnate appearance of Jesus. In this encounter, Hagar gave God a specific name: El Roi, which translates to "The God Who Sees Me."

Tags: Fasting, Physical vs Spiritual, Self-Control, Detox
Story Summary: In football, a first down is exactly 10 yards, which is 30 feet. This is the exact length of the human digestive tract from mouth to exit.
Spiritual Metaphor: Fasting stops physical consumption in this 30-foot tube, creating a space to detoxify our bodies and spirits to focus completely on face-to-face intimacy with God.
Full Story Text: In this illustration, Pastor Miles uses the mechanics of American football to explain the necessity of reaching a goal in its entirety. In football, a team is required to move the ball exactly ten yards to earn a first down and keep possession. If a player runs for nine yards and eleven inches, they have technically failed to achieve the objective. Despite the effort exerted, they remain short of the requirement.

Tags: Morning Prayer, Integrity, Seeking God, Protection
Story Summary: The tomato frog in Costa Rica leaks a white toxic sweat to defend itself after being bitten, but it is already chewed up and injured.
Spiritual Metaphor: Many Christians live like the tomato frog, seeking God only at night after being chewed up by the day's stress, instead of seeking Him first in the morning for protection.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes a specific creature found in the wild: the Costa Rican Tomato Frog. To a predator, this frog looks remarkably like a ripe, delicious tomato. It is bright red, plump, and appears to be a source of nourishment. However, the frog is actually toxic.
