If an animal attempts to eat it, the frog releases a thick, glue-like mucus that can cause allergic reactions or even choke the predator. What appeared to be life-giving food is actually a threat to life.

Illustration Name: Preaching on the Road to Cincinnati
Tags: Divine Detours, Evangelism, Ministry, Orchestration
Story Summary: When a flight to Cincinnati was diverted, Pastor Miles and a friend rented a car and offered a ride to three college students. Miles turned around in the passenger seat and preached the Gospel, leading two of them to Christ. His friend realized he was called to ministry because of this divine detour.
Spiritual Metaphor: God orchestrates seemingly disruptive, chaotic detours in our lives to position us to fulfill His dream, make a difference, and step into our calling.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts an experience early in his journey while driving on the road to Cincinnati. During the drive, he felt a powerful divine urge to preach. Despite being alone in his vehicle, he began to preach fervently to the empty seats and the open road as if he were standing before a congregation of thousands. He used this time of solitude to develop his voice and respond to the internal call long before the physical platform existed.

Tags: End in Mind, Preparation, Self-Control, Excellence
Story Summary: A friend taught Miles to park by backing into spaces. Police cars always do this because when an emergency call comes, they don't have time to back out. It deals with hassle on the front end so the back end is effortless.
Spiritual Metaphor: We must live our lives with the end in mind. Dealing with baggage and seeking God on the front end allows us to effortlessly step forward when God calls us.
Full Story Text: Miles discusses the intentional habit of backing into a parking space rather than pulling in head-first. He explains that while it takes more effort and focus to back in initially, it positions the driver for an immediate and efficient exit. It is a tactical decision made upon arrival to ensure readiness for whatever comes next.

Tags: Persistent Prayer, Anniversary, Intercession, San Diego
Story Summary: When the church acquired Liberty Station, Miles found an old chair, took it to the roof, and sat in it daily for years weeping and praying for San Diego.
Spiritual Metaphor: The old roof chair represents the immense power of persistent, tear-filled, and humble prayer on the front end of a long-term ministry legacy.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles presents a physical chair that has survived the 20-year history of the church. He describes how this chair was used during the early days when they would sit on the roof of their building, looking out over the city of San Diego. From that vantage point, they would dream, strategize, and pray for the people of the city. The chair serves as a tangible connection to the humble, visionary beginnings of the Rock Church.

Tags: Evangelism, Altar Call, Personal Story, Simplicity
Story Summary: Miles spent years witnessing to his dad with no success. While walking Miramar Lake, he asked his dad when he would get saved, and his dad replied that he was waiting for Miles to do an altar call and ask him. They sat on a bench and did it right there.
Spiritual Metaphor: The Miramar Lake encounter serves as a metaphor for the "Bridge of Shared Experience." It teaches that believers do not need to be theological scholars to witness; they simply need to be honest about who they were before Christ. By meeting people where they are—literally and figuratively—the "I was" portion of a testimony creates a point of contact that allows others to see a path toward change.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares a personal experience regarding an encounter at Miramar Lake. During a run, he engaged in a conversation with a stranger about life and spiritual transformation. He describes how most people are searching for something to fill a void in their lives and how he used his own history to connect with this individual. The story highlights the simplicity of engaging someone in their current environment and the power of being available to share a word of hope.

Tags: Happiness, Materialism, Idolatry, False Comforts
Story Summary: Miles' daughter saw a dress she just had to have and started hyperventilating from excitement, forcing Miles to physically pull her out of the store.
Spiritual Metaphor: We get desperately hyped over temporary material possessions, believing they are our source of happiness, when permanent joy is only found in Christ.
Full Story Text:
Summary / Story DetailsMiles describes a humorous but poignant scene of a woman shopping for a dress who becomes so overwhelmed with excitement or stress over the purchase that she begins to hyperventilate. The illustration paints a vivid picture of the intense physiological and emotional responses humans have toward material acquisitions and the pursuit of "the perfect look" or the perfect deal.

Spiritual Metaphor / Preaching ApplicationThis illustration is a metaphor for "Misplaced Worship" and "Materialistic Idolatry." It contrasts the passion we often display for temporary, worldly goods with the frequent lack of passion we show for the things of God. If we can become so physically overwhelmed by a piece of clothing, how much more should our hearts be moved by the eternal gift of salvation? It challenges the congregation to evaluate what truly takes their breath away and where they are investing their highest emotions.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Why Christmas? - Part 1, Walking in God's Favor
Tags: Divine Purpose, Miracle of Life, Pregnancy, Value
Story Summary: Pregnancy starts with a single cell called a zygote, which multiplies exponentially (1 to 2 to 4 to 8, etc.) to eventually form billions of cells.
Spiritual Metaphor: Life in the womb is a divine partnership. God designed your frame and wrote your purpose before any of your days even began (Psalm 139).
Full Story Text: This illustration highlights the biological miracle of human life. It begins with the meeting of a sperm and an egg to form a single zygote cell. This single cell contains the entire blueprint for a human being. Through a process of rapid multiplication, that one cell becomes 100 trillion cells, eventually forming a complex person with a heart, brain, and personality.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Why Christmas? - Part 1, Walking in God's Favor
Tags: Obedience, Rationalization, Peter, Overthinking
Story Summary: Peter walked on water without understanding the physics of it. But when he looked at the wind and began to rationalize, he talked himself out of the miracle.
Spiritual Metaphor: Humility means obeying God even when we don't understand the "how." Overthinking and rationalization will talk us out of the miracles God has for us.
Full Story Text: This section addresses the human tendency to use logic to explain away the supernatural. Pastor Miles references the account of Jesus walking on water and notes how people often attempt to rationalize the event—for example, by suggesting that Jesus was actually walking on a hidden sandbar rather than performing a miracle.

Sermon Title: When Bad Things Happen - Part 1, Dwell On The Past
Illustration Name: High School & College Regrets vs. Faithful Remembrance
Tags: Remembrance, Faith, Trials, Testimony
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares about old friends dwelling on high school girlfriends, missed pro careers, and potential riches.
Spiritual Metaphor: He explains that while dwelling on regrets is useless, we must 'dwell on the past' in a spiritual sense to remember how God got us through previous impossible situations.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts his experiences returning to his home in New York or his former college in Connecticut to visit old friends. During these gatherings, the conversation often turns to the past, but in an unproductive way. His friends sit around dwelling on "what-ifs"—ruminating on girlfriends they wish they had stayed with, imagining they had gone pro in sports even if they didn't actually play well, or dreaming of wealth they never possessed. In these social settings, the common refrain is always: "Stop dwelling on the past!"

Sermon Title: When Bad Things Happen - Part 1, Dwell On The Past
Illustration Name: The $125/Week Youth Pastor Step of Faith
Tags: Trust, Finance, Leap of Faith, Provision, Career, Testimony
Story Summary: After retiring from the Chargers, Miles accepted a youth pastor job paying only $125/week ($500/month) with a family, mortgage, and cars in San Diego.
Spiritual Metaphor: He chose to trust God, who faithfully provided for three years before his paycheck caught up. This became his 'stone of remembrance' for future major projects like Liberty Station.
Full Story Text: After retiring from his career with the San Diego Chargers, Miles faced a significant life transition. At the time, he had a wife, three children, a mortgage, and two cars in San Diego. Despite these heavy financial responsibilities, he felt called to ministry. He was offered a position as a youth pastor that paid only $125 a week, totaling $500 a month. Knowing his mortgage alone was three times that amount, he took a "two-second leap of faith" and accepted the role. It took three full years before his salary finally matched his monthly expenses. Throughout that period, God provided supernaturally, teaching him the reality of divine trust.

Sermon Title: When Bad Things Happen - Part 2, Mother's Day
Illustration Name: Saving Sister Margaret in the Pool
Tags: Family, Humorous, Sister, Support
Story Summary: Pastor Miles jokes on stage with his sister Margaret about how he 'saved her life' when she was drowning in three feet of water in their pool, walking on water to yank her out when he was six and she was three.
Spiritual Metaphor: In our own strength, we often exaggerate our ability to save ourselves or others, but true salvation and rescue are solely the work of Jesus Christ, who walked on water and reached down to save us when we were genuinely drowning in sin.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles brings his sister Margaret on stage to share a lighthearted memory from their childhood. He humorously recounts a time when he saved her life from drowning in only three feet of water in their family pool. Miles dramatizes the event, claiming he "walked on water" to yank her to safety when he was only six years old and she was three.

Sermon Title: When Bad Things Happen - Part 2, Mother's Day
Illustration Name: Laurie's Son Michael and the Foot of the Cross
Tags: Addiction, Surrender, Mother, Son, Restoration, Testimony, Family
Story Summary: Laurie shares how her son Michael was a heroin addict for 8 years, facing 5 felony counts. Angry at God, Laurie met Michael at the foot of a large cross and surrendered him, laying him at Jesus' feet.
Spiritual Metaphor: Michael got saved, attended ministry school, married, and entered full-time missions.
Full Story Text: Laurie shares the painful reality of her son Michael’s eight-year struggle with heroin addiction. The situation escalated to five felony charges and the prospect of prison. Exhausted and angry at God, Laurie eventually reached a breaking point. She met her son at the bottom of a large cross, told him she was finally letting him go, and symbolically laid him at the feet of Jesus. Following this act of surrender, Michael was saved, attended the Rock School of Ministry, married a Christian woman, had children, and entered full-time missions.

Sermon Title: When Bad Things Happen - Part 2, Mother's Day
Illustration Name: Teresa's Midnight Vigil and the Prison of Worry
Tags: Worry, Peace, Surrender, Mother, Son, Addiction, Health, Testimony, Family
Story Summary: Teresa shares about her son's meth addiction from age 14 to 18. Obsessed with trying to fix him, she stayed up late every night, losing weight and getting physically sick from worry.
Spiritual Metaphor: When she finally surrendered him to God, she found complete supernatural freedom and peace. Her son got clean and built a beautiful family.
Full Story Text: Laurie shares the painful reality of her son Michael’s eight-year struggle with heroin addiction. The situation escalated to five felony charges and the prospect of prison. Exhausted and angry at God, Laurie eventually reached a breaking point. She met her son at the bottom of a large cross, told him she was finally letting him go, and symbolically laid him at the feet of Jesus. Following this act of surrender, Michael was saved, attended the Rock School of Ministry, married a Christian woman, had children, and entered full-time missions.

Sermon Title: When Bad Things Happen - Part 5, Get Involved!
Illustration Name: The Madea Prompt and Breaking the Redline
Tags: Outreach, Comfort Zone, Redlining, Compassion, Brokenness, Community
Story Summary: Watching Tyler Perry's 'Madea Goes to Prison' where a man helps his childhood friend who became a prostitute, God challenged Miles about helping prostitutes in San Diego.
Spiritual Metaphor: Miles shares his vision to 'hire' prostitutes all at once to offer them housing, jobs, and Jesus, and compares avoidance of brokenness to historical banking 'redlining'.
Full Story Text:
While watching the Tyler Perry film Madea Goes to Prison, Pastor Miles was struck by a scene where a character attempts to rescue a childhood friend from prostitution. During this moment, he felt God speak to him directly, asking: "What are you doing for the prostitutes in San Diego?"

This prompt brought to mind a risky vision Pastor Miles had conceived 15 years earlier. The vision involved a massive collaboration with local police to "hire" a large group of local prostitutes all at once. The goal was not traditional law enforcement, but rather to bring these women to a safe location where the church could offer them:

Sermon Title: Worthy - Part 1, What Is God Worthy Of?
Illustration Name: Chasing the Tight End and the Excuse of Injury
Tags: Effort, Excellence, Excuses, Football, Sports, Worthiness, Accountability
Story Summary: During a Chargers defensive meeting, a coach repeatedly played a clip of a heavy tight end outrunning a fast cornerback. Asked if he was running as fast as he could, the cornerback made the excuse 'I'm hurt' to avoid being benched or admitting defeat.
Spiritual Metaphor: Miles uses this to show how we give God mediocre effort and then make excuses.
Full Story Text:
In the NFL, there is a distinct physical hierarchy: cornerbacks are typically the smallest and fastest players on the field, while offensive tight ends are significantly larger and slower. During a defensive film review meeting following a game against the Oakland Raiders, a coach repeatedly played a specific clip for the entire team.

The footage showed a tight end running down the field to catch a pass while a cornerback trailed far behind him, unable to close the distance. The coach paused the film and asked the cornerback a pointed question: "Are you running as fast as you can?"

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Dialed In - Part 1, Prayer
Theme/Category: In football, a first down is exactly 10 yards (30 feet), measured on the sideline with red poles. This is the exact length of the human digestive tract from mouth to exit.
Tags: Fasting, Physical vs Spiritual, Self-Control, Detox
Story Summary: Pastor Miles uses the mechanics of a first down in American football to explain why we must go the full distance in our spiritual commitments. A team must gain exactly ten yards (30 feet) to earn a first down; gaining nine yards and eleven inches is still a failure. This 30-foot distance is also the exact length of the human digestive tract, which is temporarily shut down during fasting.
Spiritual Metaphor: Just as a first down requires the full ten yards, spiritual breakthrough requires us to fully commit to our spiritual disciplines without compromise. Fasting tests our internal integrity; cutting our commitments short by "an inch" (breaking the fast early or making compromises) prevents us from reaching the spiritual first down and breakthrough that God has prepared for us.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson uses the rules of American football and human biology to illustrate the importance of complete commitment to spiritual disciplines. In football, a team is required to advance the ball exactly ten yards—which is 30 feet—to earn a first down and keep possession. If a player runs for nine yards and eleven inches, they remain short of the goal and technically fail to achieve the objective, regardless of the effort exerted. Miles explains that 30 feet is also the exact biological length of the human digestive tract from mouth to exit. When we engage in the spiritual discipline of fasting, we stop physical consumption through this 30-foot tube. This physical pause creates a powerful spiritual space to detoxify our bodies and minds, allowing us to align our hearts with God's voice. This illustration highlights that spiritual breakthrough requires us to go the full distance of our commitments. Fasting is a test of secret, internal integrity, and compromising or shortening our commitments by 'an inch' prevents us from reaching the spiritual first down that God has positioned for us.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Dialed In - Part 1, Prayer
Theme/Category: The tomato frog in Costa Rica leaks a white toxic sweat to defend itself after being bitten, but it is already chewed up and injured by the time it uses its defense.
Tags: Morning Prayer, Integrity, Seeking God, Protection
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes the Costa Rican Tomato Frog, which looks like a ripe, delicious tomato to predators but is actually highly toxic. When bitten, it releases a thick, white, glue-like poison that forces the predator to spit it out, leaving the predator dazed and choked. This illustrates how we often seek God only at night after being "chewed up" by worldly compromises.
Spiritual Metaphor: Many worldly desires look red, ripe, and satisfying like a tomato, but they are actually deceptive and toxic "tomato frogs" that can paralyze our faith. Fasting and daily prayer retrain our spiritual palate so we can discern between true spiritual food and deceptive compromises, seeking God first in the morning for protection rather than crying out only after we are already chewed up.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson describes a specific creature featured on Animal Planet: the Costa Rican Tomato Frog. To an unsuspecting predator, this frog looks remarkably like a ripe, plump, and delicious tomato, appearing to be a perfect source of nourishment. However, when a predator bites into it, the tomato frog instantly secretes a thick, white, toxic, glue-like mucus all over its skin. This poison causes a severe allergic reaction, choking the predator and forcing it to spit the frog out. Though the frog survives, the predator is left dazed, choked, and heavily injured. Pastor Miles uses this as a vivid metaphor for the deceptive nature of sin and worldly compromises. Many habits, relationships, and worldly temptations look 'red and ripe' like a tomato, seeming like they will easily satisfy our appetites, but they are actually deceptive 'tomato frogs.' Under the surface, they carry spiritual toxins that can paralyze our faith and choke our relationship with God. Fasting and quiet prayer help clear our spiritual eyes and retrain our taste buds so we can discern between true spiritual food and the deceptive tomato frogs of the world.

Theme/Category: Pastor Miles describes an imaginary spiritual swap meet at Veterans Stadium where the devil gives away free items designed to destroy lives (drugs, porn, division). In the center, the locked stadium labeled 'Satan Only' contains his number-one weapon: deception.
Tags: Deception, Sin, Devil's Tactics
Story Summary: Pastor Miles recounts a story from his first month of full-time ministry in 1986, when he attended a youth camp in Philadelphia. He saw a newspaper advertisement for a swap meet at Veterans Stadium and imagined a spiritual flea market where the devil was giving away free items like drugs, pornography, and division. When Miles asked Satan what was locked inside the stadium, the devil replied that it was his number-one weapon: deception.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil cannot force anyone to sin; he can only deceive us into believing that sinning is the best option for us. The free 'gifts' at his swap meet look appealing but are designed to destroy our marriages, relationships, and lives. We must stay alert to his deceptive tactics and seek God's truth to guard our minds.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson recounts a story from 1986 during his first month of full-time youth ministry. While speaking at a youth camp in Philadelphia, he saw a newspaper advertisement about a local flea market and was inspired to describe an imaginary spiritual swap meet held at Veterans Stadium (where the Philadelphia Eagles used to play). In this illustration, traffic from New York, New Jersey, and Pennsylvania is backed up as people flock to the stadium parking lot. There, tables are lined with demons giving away free items designed to destroy human lives: drugs, pornography, guns, and various ways to disobey God. People are eagerly loading their cars and trucks with these free 'gifts.' In the center of the parking lot stands the locked stadium, with every door labeled 'No Admittance - Satan Only.' When Miles asks the devil what is locked inside, Satan replies that it is his number-one weapon. He explains that since he lacks the authority to force anyone to sin, he must rely on this weapon to deceive people into believing that disobeying God is their best option. This story highlights that the devil's primary tactic is deception. He entice us with seemingly free, satisfying choices that are actually designed to bring spiritual addiction, broken relationships, and death. To counter his number-one weapon, we must immerse ourselves in the Word of God and pray daily for spiritual discernment.

Illustration Name: Translucent Kid Danny & The Youth Group
Theme/Category: Miles recalls Danny, a passionate 12-year-old in his youth group who later fell into gang life. While preaching in Juvenile Hall years later, Miles found Danny locked up down the hall and asked him: 'Are you better off now in gang life, or were you better off when you were following God?' Danny stood silent.
Tags: Rebellion, Deliverance, Choice, Consequences
Story Summary: Pastor Miles recalls a 12-year-old boy named Danny in his early youth group who was so fair-skinned he seemed translucent. Danny was incredibly passionate about God, attended Bible studies, and served on missions to Mexico. However, as Danny entered high school, he drifted into gang life and they lost touch. Years later, while Miles was preaching to juvenile hall inmates, they pointed out that Danny was locked up literally down the hall.
Spiritual Metaphor: Danny's tragic drift from a passionate youth group volunteer to a locked-up gang member illustrates the devastating, progressive nature of deception. It serves as a reminder that we can never be more fulfilled by what the world or gang life offers than by what God has planned for us, and that our choices carry inescapable, painful consequences.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson shares a heartbreaking, true story about a young boy named Danny from his early days of full-time youth ministry. Danny was an 11- or 12-year-old boy who was so fair-skinned and fair-haired that Miles affectionately referred to him as 'translucent' or 'clear,' joking that you could see his veins. Danny loved God deeply, hung out with the high school youth group, attended Bible studies, and even went on mission trips to Mexico to serve orphans. However, as Danny entered high school, he fell in with a Mexican gang, got involved in gang culture, and eventually cut off contact with the church. Years later, while Pastor Miles was preaching to a group of about 30 youth inmates in a San Diego juvenile hall, he was sharing Danny's story as an illustration of how easily young people can be led astray. To his shock, the inmates interrupted him and said, 'Hold up, he's down the hall.' Danny was literally locked up in that very facility. Miles walked down the hallway, and Danny came out and sat right in front of him. Miles reminded him of their times in church, the Bible studies, and their trips to Mexico, and then asked him directly: 'Danny, are you better off now, or were you better off when you were following God?' Danny sat in dazed, heartbroken silence, unable to claim that his new life in the gang was better than his walk with God. This illustration highlights that the devil's deception leads to progressive captivity, and our horizontal choices have very real, vertical consequences before God.

Sermon Title: Rock Church -  The Third Option - Part 1, The Third Option
Theme/Category: Kintsugi is an ancient art form where broken pottery is repaired by joining the cracks together with gold, making the repaired vessel more beautiful and valuable than the original.
Tags: Kintsugi, Brokenness, Unity, Gold, Restoration
Story Summary: Pastor Miles introduces Kintsugi, an ancient Japanese art form where broken ceramic vessels are repaired by joining the broken pieces together with a lacquer mixed with powdered gold, silver, or platinum. Rather than trying to hide the fractures, the gold makes the cracks a beautiful, celebrated, and prominent part of the pottery, making the repaired piece more valuable than the original unbroken vessel.
Spiritual Metaphor: Kintsugi is a powerful metaphor for God's redemptive restoration. The devil uses racism, prejudice, and division to shatter our communities and hearts, but Jesus does not merely sweep away our broken pieces. He joins us back together, filling our cracks with His precious grace and making our healed, united relationships more beautiful, resilient, and valuable than they were before the fracture.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson introduces his series on racial unity by presenting the concept of Kintsugi (or Kintsukuroi), an ancient Japanese art form of repairing broken pottery. In traditional Kintsugi, when a ceramic bowl, vase, or plate is shattered, the artisan does not discard the broken pieces or attempt to hide the cracks with clear glue. Instead, they meticulously join the fragments back together using a special lacquer dusted with powdered gold, silver, or platinum. The resulting gold seams run prominently throughout the vessel, highlighting and celebrating the fractures rather than concealing them. This philosophy treats the breakage and repair as a vital part of the object's history, and the Japanese believe that the repaired pottery is actually more beautiful, unique, and valuable than the original unbroken vessel. Pastor Miles explains that Kintsugi is the ultimate metaphor for God's redemptive power and racial reconciliation. The devil has used the sin of racism, prejudice, and division to shatter human relationships and split communities into fragmented 'us versus them' groups. However, we serve a God who specializing in putting broken things back together. Jesus' prayer is that we would all be one, and when we submit to Him, He repairs our racial and relational fractures with the 'gold' of His grace, creating a unified, diverse church that is far more beautiful and valuable to His Kingdom than a community that was never broken.

Sermon Title: Rock Church -  The Third Option - Part 1, The Third Option
Theme/Category: Society is designed primarily by right-handed people for right-handed people. Right-handed people have a 'privilege' they never think about because they never experience the hassle of left-handed tools.
Tags: Privilege, Left-Handed, In-Group Bias, Empathy
Story Summary: Pastor Miles uses the concept of 'right-handed privilege' to explain cultural bias. He asks the right-handed people in the congregation to raise their hands, showing they represent the vast majority, while only a few left-handed people raise theirs. He explains how our world is designed by right-handed people for right-handed people—from school desks and golf clubs to catchers' mitts and how we shake hands, forcing left-handed people to constantly adapt and deny their natural hands.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is a powerful illustration of in-group bias and cultural privilege. When you belong to the dominant, comfortable 'in-group' (like right-handers), you enjoy a lack of friction and automatic advantages that you never have to think about, often assuming that out-group disadvantages do not exist. As believers, we must recognize these invisible 'left-handed disadvantages' and actively extend in-group grace, patience, and favor to those who are different from us.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson uses a highly relatable, physical demonstration to explain the sociology of bias and privilege. He asks all the right-handed people in the congregation to raise their hands, revealing that they make up the overwhelming majority of the room. He then asks the left-handed people, including himself, to raise their hands, showing they are a small minority. Miles explains that society is designed almost entirely by right-handed people for right-handed people. From the specialized desks in school classrooms and catchers' mitts in sports to golf clubs and daily tools, right-handed individuals enjoy a seamless, friction-free environment. In contrast, left-handed people face constant, minor daily disadvantages and must constantly strain, adapt, or 'deny' their natural hands to navigate a right-handed world—even having to learn to shake hands right-handed to avoid awkwardness. Miles points out that right-handed people do not hate left-handed people, but they possess a 'privilege' they never have to think about because they never experience the hassle. This serves as a powerful metaphor for in-group bias and cultural privilege. When we are part of a dominant 'in-group,' we enjoy automatic comfort and advantages, making it easy to assume that out-group disadvantages do not exist. Miles challenges the congregation to acknowledge their blind spots, recognize the 'left-handed' hurdles that other ethnicities or cultures face daily, and actively extend the same favor, patience, and benefit of the doubt that they naturally give to their own in-group.

Sermon Title: Rock Church  - The Third Option - Part 2, Unalienable Hope
Theme/Category: In 2010, 16-year-old Kalief Browder was falsely accused of stealing a backpack and spent over 1,000 days in Rikers Island (700 in solitary) without a trial, before charges were dropped. He later committed suicide.
Tags: Kalief Browder, Injustice, Rikers Island, Dehumanization, Labels
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares the tragic story of Kalief Browder, a 16-year-old African American boy from the Bronx who was falsely accused of stealing a backpack in 2010. Unable to afford the $3,000 bail, he spent over 1,000 days in Rikers Island, including 700 days in solitary confinement where he was beaten by inmates and guards. Refusing multiple plea deals because of his innocence, he was eventually released when charges were dropped, but tragically committed suicide shortly after.
Spiritual Metaphor: This heartbreaking story represents the devastating impact of dehumanization, systemic injustice, and labels. When we label a group of people as 'them' or view them with disdain, we find it easier to tolerate their mistreatment or turn a blind eye to their lack of justice. We have a non-negotiable, biblical responsibility to seek justice, stand against unfairness, and love our neighbors as ourselves.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson shares the heartbreaking, true story of Kalief Browder to illustrate the devastating, real-world consequences of systemic injustice and dehumanization. In 2010, Kalief, a 16-year-old African American youth living in the Bronx, New York, was walking home from a party when police officers pulled up and arrested him after a bystander accused him of stealing a backpack containing an iPad. Because his family could not afford the $3,000 bail, Kalief was sent to Rikers Island—one of the country's most violent correctional facilities—to await trial. He spent over 1,000 days (more than three years) in Rikers Island without ever being convicted of a crime, including an agonizing 700 days in solitary confinement. During his stay, he was repeatedly and severely beaten by both guards and other inmates, much of which was captured on surveillance video. Despite the physical and psychological trauma, Kalief refused multiple plea deals that would have allowed him to go home if he simply admitted guilt, maintaining his absolute innocence. Eventually, the prosecution dropped all charges and released him because they had no evidence and the accuser had fled the country. Tragically, the psychological trauma of his confinement was too severe, and shortly after his release, Kalief committed suicide. Pastor Miles uses this tragedy to illustrate the unalienable responsibility of the church to stand for justice. When we categorize people into 'us versus them' and use dehumanizing labels, we find it easier to tolerate or ignore their mistreatment. Miles challenges believers to recognize that God is a God of justice, and we must fight to ensure that our neighbors—regardless of their color or background—are treated with the same dignity and fairness we desire for ourselves.

Sermon Title: Rock Church  - The Third Option - Part 2, Unalienable Hope
Theme/Category: Hall of Fame baseball player Rod Carew (Panamanian and black) received a heart transplant from 27-year-old white NFL player Konrad Reuland. Reuland's mother visited Carew and wept as she heard her white son's heart beating inside a black man's chest.
Tags: Rod Carew, Heart Transplant, Reuland, Unity, One Race
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares the story of Hall of Fame baseball player Rod Carew, a dark-skinned Panamanian who suffered a severe heart condition at age 71 and needed a transplant. He received the heart and kidney of 27-year-old white NFL player Konrad Reuland, who had tragically died of a brain aneurysm. Konrad's mother later visited Rod and wept as she listened to her white son's heart beating inside a black man's chest.
Spiritual Metaphor: This touching story demonstrates that beneath our outward colors, we are biologically and spiritually one human race made in the image of God. If a white football player's heart can beat perfectly inside a black baseball player's chest to keep him alive, it proves that the devil's attempts to divide us by race are a complete lie. We must give our hearts to our out-groups and celebrate our common humanity.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson shares the powerful, moving story of Hall of Fame baseball player Rod Carew and NFL player Konrad Reuland to demonstrate our fundamental biological and spiritual unity. Rod Carew, a dark-skinned Panamanian-American baseball legend, suffered a massive heart attack at age 71 and desperately needed a heart and kidney transplant to survive. Around that same time, Konrad Reuland, a 27-year-old white tight end who played for Stanford and in the NFL, tragically suffered a brain aneurysm, fell into a coma, and passed away. Konrad had registered as an organ donor, and his heart and kidney were matched and successfully transplanted into Rod Carew. Months later, Konrad's mother, Mary, contacted Rod, asking if she could meet him and listen to her son's heart. In an incredibly emotional encounter, she placed a stethoscope against Rod Carew's chest and wept as she heard her white son's heart beating steadily inside the chest of a black Panamanian man. Remarkably, they discovered that when Konrad was 11 years old, he had actually met Rod Carew and told his mother that Carew was his hero and that he wanted to be a professional athlete just like him. Pastor Miles uses this story to completely dismantle the concept of separate human races. He points out that the medical compatibility of a white athlete's heart keeping a black athlete alive proves that we are biologically identical—99.5% genetically the same. The devil has duped our culture into focusing on outward shades and divisions, but God created only one human race in His image. Miles challenges believers to physically and spiritually 'give their hearts' to those who are different from them, actively breaking down the barriers of out-group discrimination.

Sermon Title: Relationship Goals - Lessons from the Trinity
Tags: Trinity, Design, Holy Spirit, Creation, Image of God
Story Summary: Pastor Miles uses a physical glove shaped like a hand with five fingers to illustrate human design. Left empty, a glove is flat, lifeless, and powerless, completely dependent on a real hand sliding inside of it to fill it out and give it movement and strength.
Spiritual Metaphor: We are made in the image of God so that His Holy Spirit can live inside us. Apart from Him, we are empty and unable to fulfill our true purpose. We must let Him animate and guide us.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles uses the physical structure of a glove to illustrate human design. A glove is fashioned with five fingers, made in the exact image of a hand. However, when left to itself, the glove lies empty, lifeless, and completely unable to perform any work or fulfill its purpose. It relies entirely on a real hand sliding inside of it to fill it out and provide movement, strength, and utility.\n\nGod created human beings in His own image - Genesis 1:26 - much like a glove is created in the image of a hand. We are designed specifically so that His Holy Spirit can dwell within us. When we attempt to live apart from Him, we remain empty, powerless, and unable to fulfill our true purpose. To be truly animated and directed, we must allow His hand to slide inside our lives.

Sermon Title: Relationship Goals - Lessons from the Trinity
Tags: Potential, Design, Worth, Identity, Calling
Story Summary: As an engineering student, Pastor Miles took thermodynamics under Dr. Saras. The professor pulled Miles aside and told him he had more natural talent and intelligence than the top student in the class, which Miles was shocked to hear and struggled to believe at the time.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our heavenly Father sees a design and potential in us that far exceeds our limited self-perspective. We must align our self-worth with our Creator's vision rather than living in survival mode.
Full Story Text:
Recalling his days as an engineering student, Pastor Miles describes his experience taking thermodynamics and dynamics under a demanding professor named Dr. Saras. One day, while discussing a straight-A student, Dr. Saras pulled Miles aside and told him, "You are smarter and have more natural talent than him." Though Miles was shocked and did not believe the assessment at the time—as he was focused simply on surviving the class—the professor recognized a capability in him that Miles was completely blind to.

Just as the college professor saw immense potential and talent in Miles that he could not see in himself, our heavenly Father has a design and high calling for our lives that often far exceeds our limited self-perspective. We are called to trust what our Creator says about our identity and potential rather than living beneath our true purpose in "survival mode."

Sermon Title: Kingdom Warrior - Part 2, A Warrior's Identity
Tags: Authority, Access, Identity, Co-heirs, Leverage, Petco Park
Story Summary: The former owner of the San Diego Padres gifted Miles tickets to his private box at Petco Park. The single ticket granted Miles immense authority and access, allowing him to pass through secret doors, private elevators, a massive complimentary buffet, and sit in premium seats right behind home plate.
Spiritual Metaphor: The former owner of the San Diego Padres gifted Miles tickets to his private box at Petco Park. The single ticket granted Miles immense authority and access, allowing him to pass through secret doors, private elevators, a massive complimentary buffet, and sit in premium seats right behind home plate.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles recalls an experience involving the former owner of the San Diego Padres, who was a member of the Rock Church. The owner provided Miles with a ticket to his private box at Petco Park. Upon arrival, Miles realized that the ticket represented far more than a simple seat assignment; it carried significant authority and exclusive access.

The ticket allowed him to bypass standard protocols, moving through secret security doors and utilizing private elevators. Once inside, he was granted access to a massive complimentary steak sandwich buffet and premium seating located directly behind home plate. Throughout the game, he was attended to by waiters. Miles reflected on the immense leverage and access he held simply by possessing that specific ticket.

This story serves as a metaphor for the believer's life in Christ. When an individual accepts Jesus Christ as their Savior, they receive a spiritual "ticket" that defines a new kingdom identity. While the enemy seeks to keep believers in a state of fear and perceived powerlessness, their true kingdom identity provides: Immense Authority, Spiritual Leverage, and Direct Access. As co-heirs with Christ, believers are spiritually seated with Him in heavenly places. The illustration emphasizes that spiritual battles should be fought from a position of the authority and power found in one's true identity as a member of God's kingdom.

Illustration Name: Healing the Atheist's Leg at the Porn Convention
Tags: Evangelism, Outreach, Healing, Faith, Demonstration, Miracle
Story Summary: At an evangelistic outreach at a pornography convention in Las Vegas, Miles met a man walking with a cane and received a word of knowledge that one of his legs was shorter than the other. Miles invited a nearby atheist volunteer to watch, prayed for the man, and they watched his shorter leg physically grow out, healing his joint pains instantly.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's kingdom is meant to be actively demonstrated, not just declared inside a church building. When we step out of our comfort zones, God will perform the unexplainable to challenge unbelief.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles recounts an evangelistic outreach at a pornography convention held in Las Vegas. During the event, Miles encountered a man utilizing a cane for mobility. He received a supernatural 'word of knowledge' indicating that one of the man's legs was physically shorter than the other. Noticing an atheist volunteer standing nearby, Miles invited him to witness the moment, stating, "You have to watch this, it is about to challenge your beliefs."

As Miles prayed for the man, the group witnessed his shorter leg physically grow out to match the length of the other leg. This resulted in an instant healing of the man's knees, hips, and back. The man was able to walk away without the use of his cane, leaving the atheist observer with a powerful demonstration of God's physical power to consider.

The kingdom of God is designed to be actively demonstrated rather than simply discussed within the confines of a church building. By stepping outside of comfortable environments and engaging with those who are hurting, believers allow God to show Himself strong. He performs the unexplainable to touch lives directly and challenge the foundations of unbelief.

Tags: Redemption, Prison, Service, Purpose, Restoration, Transformation
Story Summary: Miles recalls meeting a massive, rugged, intimidating man at a church event who proudly shared that he was part of the church's crochet/knitting ministry. When asked where he learned to crochet, the man laughed and replied that he learned it while in prison.
Spiritual Metaphor: God is able to redeem any past, no matter how rugged or broken, and repurpose anyone's hands for beautiful, unexpected works of service to build up His kingdom.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles recalls meeting a large, burly, and rugged individual during a church event. To Miles' surprise, the man proudly identified himself as a member of the church's crochet and knitting ministry. Struck by the contrast between the man’s intimidating appearance and his hobby, Miles asked, "Where did a guy like you learn how to crochet?" The man laughed and shared his history, replying, "I learned in prison!"

This encounter illustrates that God is capable of redeeming any past history, regardless of how rugged, broken, or unconventional it may appear. He has the power to take a rough personal history and repurpose a person's hands for creative, gentle, and beautiful works of service intended to build up His kingdom.

Tags: Battle, Spiritual Warfare, Race, Division
Story Summary: Miles describes a hypothetical fight between an alligator and a bear, noting that the winner completely depends on the terrain. If they fight in deep water, the alligator wins easily; if on dry land, the bear wins.
Spiritual Metaphor: Trying to fight racial division in the natural realm (politics, media) is like fighting on the wrong turf where we will lose; we must fight spiritually using God's tools where His victory is assured.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles poses a riddle to the congregation: "If an alligator and a bear had a fight, who would win?" He notes that the correct answer completely depends on the terrain where the battle is fought. If they fight in the deep water, the alligator wins easily. If they fight on dry land, the bear wins.

This represents the spiritual nature of racial tension and division. If we fight this battle in the natural realm (using politics, social media arguments, or our own physical strength), we are fighting on the wrong turf and we will lose. We must fight this battle on the spiritual turf using prayer, love, and forgiveness where God's victory is assured.

Tags: Race, Diversity, Humorous, Skin Color
Story Summary: Pastor Miles humorously talks about how people pay massive amounts of money to travel to Hawaii and lay on the beach for hours just to get a beautiful caramel tan to show off, yet politically correct culture claims to be 'colorblind.' He celebrates the diverse 'Skittles church' of colors and cultures.
Spiritual Metaphor: Instead of pretending to be colorblind, we should see and celebrate each other's colors and cultures, appreciating diversity as a beautiful mosaic designed by a creative God.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles humorously talks about how people pay massive amounts of money to travel to Hawaii and lay on the beach for hours just to get a beautiful, rich caramel tan, and they want everyone to see it. However, in our politically correct culture, people often say "I don't see color!" Miles jokes that if people don't see color, they won't appreciate his beautiful skin tone that God gave him in the womb. He calls the Rock Church a "Skittles church" full of diverse colors.

Metaphor: God is highly creative and intentionally created humans in all different shades and hair textures. Instead of pretending to be colorblind, we should see and celebrate each other's colors and cultures, viewing diversity as a reflection of God's creativity in the body of Christ.

Tags: Family, Tradition, Humorous, Pets
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares a lighthearted family tradition where their dog Lucy, a dachshund whom Miles made 'psycho' using a laser pointer, would go wild every Christmas chewing up all the wrapping paper after presents were opened.
Spiritual Metaphor: Humorous family tradition illustrating the joy and playfulness of raising children and finding joy in chaotic domestic moments.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shares a lighthearted family Christmas tradition from when his kids were young. They had a dachshund named Lucy whom Miles admitted to making "psycho" by playing with her using a laser pointer, causing her to chase the red dot until her brain went wild. Every Christmas after the kids opened their presents, they would let Lucy loose, and she would go absolutely crazy chewing up all the wrapping paper, which served as their primary family entertainment for half an hour.

Metaphor: A humorous story illustrating family bonding, traditions, and finding joy in the simple, chaotic moments of life with family and pets.

Tags: Incarnation, Obedience, Sacrifice, Humorous
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes Jesus' incarnation as leaving a beautiful, plush golden throne in heaven to move into a smelly, stinky ghetto stable or the 'hood' out of absolute obedience to the Father.
Spiritual Metaphor: Jesus voluntarily gave up His cosmic rights and heaven's luxury to move into the mess and stinky situations of our human lives to save us.
Full Story Text:
Story: Pastor Miles describes the incarnation of Jesus Christ as leaving a glorious, plush, golden throne in heaven and choosing to move into the "hood" or a smelly, stinky ghetto stable. He notes that if God asked him to leave heaven's luxury to move into a stable where people would spit on and murder him, he would stay on the throne. But Jesus did it out of complete obedience.

Metaphor: Jesus' incarnation was an act of supreme, incomprehensible obedience. He voluntarily gave up His cosmic, majestic right to stay in heaven's luxury to step into the brokenness, mess, and stinky situations of our human lives to rescue us.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Lord of the Things - Part 1, Getting in the Fast Lane
Tags: Fasting, Discipline
Story Summary: Fasting pauses consumption in this 30-foot tube, detoxifying the body and mind to create a vast space for face-to-face intimacy with God.
Spiritual Metaphor: Fasting is not about starvation but about clearing out our physical distractions (pausing our 30-foot digestive tract) to create a focused spiritual space. By temporarily shutting down physical consumption, we eliminate toxic clutter and position ourselves to receive God's supernatural power and intimate presence.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson draws a physical comparison between the game of football and the human body. He notes that a first down in football requires moving the ball 10 yards. Coincidentally, the human digestive tract is approximately 30 feet long—which is exactly 10 yards. He describes how the things we put into our mouths must travel this entire "10-yard" distance through our system.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Lord of the Things - Part 1, Getting in the Fast Lane
Tags: Detox, Spiritual Life
Story Summary: An owl swallows prey whole, digesting the meat and spitting out compressed bones and fur as a pellet. Fasting and prayer detoxify and cast out the toxic, hard-to-digest waste in our spiritual lives.
Spiritual Metaphor: Just as an owl's digestive system naturally isolates and ejects what is useless and toxic, fasting and prayer are spiritual mechanisms that separate us from the heavy, ungodly waste we have consumed from the world, allowing us to spit out spiritual impurities and live in holiness.
Full Story Text: In this illustration, Pastor Miles discusses the biological process of an owl's digestion. Because owls swallow their prey whole, they often consume materials they cannot digest, such as fur and bones. To deal with this, the owl’s stomach compresses these indigestible bits into small "pellets," which the owl eventually regurgitates (coughs up) to rid its body of the waste.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - My Story - Part 1, The Power of Your Story
Tags: Temptation, Danger
Story Summary: Entering a cage containing six full-grown lions to feed them directly. The warning that they are still wild, dangerous predators mirrors how we think we can safely "tame" or play with secret sins.
Spiritual Metaphor: Many Christians believe they can safely "tame" or play with their secret sins, treating them like household pets. But sin is like a wild, ravenous lion; the moment you lower your guard and enter its territory, it will turn on you and destroy you. We must flee from temptation, not try to domesticate it.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts an experience in South Africa where he had the opportunity to feed lions. He describes the visceral reality of being in close proximity to such powerful predators. The core of the story revolves around the specific instructions required to feed them safely: you have to throw the meat properly and stay behind the protection, recognizing that while the lions are magnificent, they are also dangerous and demand respect. He emphasizes the focus required during the exchange between the provider and the lion.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - My Story - Part 1, The Power of Your Story
Tags: Resistance, Fighting
Story Summary: The intense, focused battle stance of UFC champion Ronda Rousey when stepping into the ring, illustrating the active, aggressive stance required to fight spiritual battles and resist the devil.
Spiritual Metaphor: Resisting the devil requires more than a passive, defensive attitude; it requires an active, focused, and aggressive spiritual stance. Like a world-class fighter stepping into the ring, we must stand firm in God's authority and actively fight against the enemy's schemes.
Full Story Text: In this brief but punchy illustration, Pastor Miles references professional fighter Ronda Rousey. He focuses specifically on her "stance"—the way she carries herself and prepares for a physical engagement. He notes that her effectiveness in the octagon is a direct result of her posture and readiness. The illustration highlights the discipline of the fighter’s stance as a precursor to any actual movement or strike.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Superman - Part 1, A Prophetic Challenge
Tags: Presence, Stillness, Supernatural
Story Summary: Standing perfectly still holding a coat hanger antenna to get black-and-white TV reception, symbolizing the stillness and alignment needed to receive God's supernatural power.
Spiritual Metaphor: To receive a clear signal and supernatural power from God, we must stand still and align our lives with His frequency. When we are distracted or constantly moving in our own strength, we experience spiritual "static"; stillness and obedience restore the connection.
Full Story Text: In this illustration, Pastor Miles McPherson uses the visual of an antenna to describe the necessity of spiritual positioning. He emphasizes that just as an antenna must be raised and properly oriented to receive a signal from a transmitter, a believer must "stand up" spiritually to receive what God is broadcasting. If an antenna is down or broken, it cannot catch the frequency, regardless of how strong the signal is.

Tags: Surrender, Trust
Story Summary: Tandem jumping from 12,000 feet and letting go of the bar, illustrating the complete surrender and letting go of our human safety nets to walk by faith.
Spiritual Metaphor: True faith requires completely letting go of our human safety nets and control mechanisms, trusting that we are securely bound to the Father. When we jump into the unknown of His calling, we must release our grip on the bar and let Him carry us.
Full Story Text: In this illustration, Pastor Miles McPherson uses the visual of an antenna to describe the necessity of spiritual positioning. He emphasizes that just as an antenna must be raised and properly oriented to receive a signal from a transmitter, a believer must "stand up" spiritually to receive what God is broadcasting. If an antenna is down or broken, it cannot catch the frequency, regardless of how strong the signal is.

Illustration Name: David's Goliath "I don't do that anymore"
Tags: Calling, Courage, Victory
Story Summary: Teenage David tells King Saul that yesterday he kept sheep, but today he is a giant killer and doesn't do sheep anymore, modeling how we must declare our new identity in Christ.
Spiritual Metaphor: Accepting Christ means declaring a decisive shift in our identity. We are no longer defined by our past limitations, failures, or quiet sheep-tending days; we must step boldly into our new calling as overcomers and giant-killers.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson recount his personal experience of going tandem skydiving in Camloops. He describes the physical and emotional tension of being inside the plane, the sensory overload of the wind and height, and the moment of absolute commitment required to jump out of the aircraft. He emphasizes that in a tandem jump, you are physically strapped to an instructor who manages the technical aspects of the descent, requiring the jumper to relinquish control.

Sermon Title: On Earth As It Is In Heaven - Part 1, Supernatural Expectation
Tags: Design, Holy Spirit, Creation, Image of God, Powerless
Story Summary: Pastor Miles uses a physical glove shaped like a hand with five fingers to illustrate human design. Left empty, a glove is flat, lifeless, and powerless, completely dependent on a real hand sliding inside of it to fill it out and give it movement and strength.
Spiritual Metaphor: We are made in the image of God so that His Holy Spirit can live inside us. Apart from Him, we are empty and unable to fulfill our true purpose. We must let Him animate and guide us.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles uses the physical structure of a glove to illustrate human design. A glove is fashioned with five fingers, made in the exact image of a hand. Yet, left to itself, the glove lies empty, lifeless, and completely unable to perform any work or fulfill its purpose. It relies entirely on a real hand sliding inside of it to fill it out and give it movement, strength, and utility.

Sermon Title: On Earth As It Is In Heaven - Part 1, Supernatural Expectation
Tags: Potential, Design, Worth, Identity, Calling
Story Summary: As an engineering student, Pastor Miles took thermodynamics under Dr. Saras. The professor pulled Miles aside and told him he had more natural talent and intelligence than the top student in the class, which Miles was shocked to hear and struggled to believe at the time.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our heavenly Father sees a design and potential in us that far exceeds our limited self-perspective. We must align our self-worth with our Creator's vision rather than living in survival mode.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls his days as an engineering student in college. He took thermodynamics and dynamics under a demanding professor named Dr. Saras. One day, while discussing a straight-A student, Dr. Saras pulled Miles aside and told him, "You are smarter and have more natural talent than him." Miles was shocked and did not believe it at the time, as he was just trying to survive the class, but the professor saw a capability in him that Miles was completely blind to.

Tags: Flesh, Compromise, Self-Control, Discipline, Boxing
Story Summary: Pastor Miles' brother, a professional boxer, decided to take it easy during a sparring session with champion Davey Moore just to 'satisfy' the trainers and have a light workout. Instead, Davey came out swinging and almost knocked him out cold, teaching him never to compromise in the ring.
Spiritual Metaphor: We cannot 'spar' or compromise with our fleshly carnal nature. If we try to take it lightly or satisfy it slightly, it will swing hard and knock us out spiritually. We must completely crucify the flesh.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shares a story about his brother, who was a world-ranked professional boxer (ranked 20th in the world). While at the gym, his brother was asked to spar by Davey Moore, a legendary Golden Gloves champion. During their first session, Miles' brother fought with high intensity and beat Davey Moore. This angered Davey, who refused to speak to him for an entire week.

When they scheduled a second sparring session, Miles' brother decided to take it easy. His intent was merely to "satisfy" the trainers by having a light workout. However, the moment the session began, Davey Moore came out swinging with full force, nearly knocking Miles' brother out cold. In that moment, his brother realized the gravity of the situation, thinking, "Forget that, the fight is on!"

Tags: Fear, Terror, Spiritual Warfare, Childhood
Story Summary: Miles recalls watching the original movie 'The Exorcist' as a child. He was so terrified that night that he and his brother slept in the same bed, back-to-back, completely frozen with fear.
Spiritual Metaphor: The devil's tactics are designed to terrorize and paralyze us with fear. However, as believers, we do not need to cower in fear because 'greater is He who is in us than he who is in the world.'
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls an experience from his childhood after watching the original movie The Exorcist. The film left him profoundly terrified. That night, the fear was so overwhelming that he and his brother slept in the same bed, positioned back-to-back. They remained completely frozen in fear throughout the night, paralyzed by the terror the movie had instilled in them.

Illustration Name: Bat Echolocation of Moths vs. Rocks
Tags: Echolocation, Heart, Pharisees, Revelation
Story Summary: Bats navigate using echolocation. When sound waves bounce off a solid rock, the clean reflection tells the bat to dodge. But when waves hit the fluttering wings of a moth, the garbled reflection tells the bat it is an insect to eat.
Spiritual Metaphor: Human beings look at the outward appearance, but God 'echolocates' the heart. Jesus' spiritual waves bounce off self-righteous solid 'rocks' (Pharisees) but register the fluttering, broken, and desperate 'hearts' (Zaccheus).
Full Story Text: Bats are blind and navigate using echolocation—sending out sound waves that bounce off objects and return to them. When they send out sound waves that bounce off a solid rock, the wave returns clean and solid, telling the bat to dodge it. But when the wave hits the fluttering wings of a moth, it returns all garbled and vibrating, telling the bat it is an insect to eat.

Tags: Witnessing, Salvation, Immediate Response, Evangelism
Story Summary: While on a flight, Miles witnessed to a flight attendant who was living with her boyfriend. After debating her need for Christ throughout the trip, Miles asked if she wanted to be saved right now. She immediately jumped up from her seat in excitement and accepted.
Spiritual Metaphor: When God prompts us to receive His love and forgiveness, we shouldn't hesitate or rationalize. We should immediately jump up and receive His invitation.
Full Story Text: While on a flight, Miles was witnessing to a flight attendant who was living with her boyfriend. Throughout the flight, as she served drinks and walked back and forth, they debated her lifestyle and need for Christ. By the end of the trip, Miles asked her: 'Do you want to be saved right now?' She literally jumped up from her seat in excitement and replied, 'Yes, let's do it right now!'

Tags: Discipline, Fasting, Survival, Warrior, Training
Story Summary: In the movie 'Rambo', the main character is trained to survive in any harsh environment—eating wood, drinking stagnant water, and standing still for five days in the freezing rain to accomplish his mission.
Spiritual Metaphor: Spiritual disciplines like fasting are designed to train us as 'spiritual Rambos.' By mastering and denying our physical flesh, we become disciplined warriors who can accomplish God's mission in any environment.
Full Story Text: In the movie Rambo, the protagonist is depicted as a soldier trained to survive in the most unforgiving environments. His preparation is so rigorous that he can be dropped into a jungle and sustain himself by eating wood and drinking stagnant water. He possesses the discipline to sleep in the dirt and remain perfectly still for five consecutive days in freezing rain to ensure the success of his mission.

Tags: Baggage, Warfare, Focus, Simplicity, Sacrifice
Story Summary: In the movie 'Saving Private Ryan', Captain Miller recruits a clerk who tries to carry a heavy typewriter and massive duffel bags. Miller throws the typewriter on the ground and hands him a pencil: 'Leave all that junk behind, we're going to war.'
Spiritual Metaphor: When God calls us into spiritual warfare, we cannot drag our carnal 'typewriters' (fleshly habits, comfort zones, baggage) with us. We must leave them on the ground and pack light to be effective.
Full Story Text: In the film Saving Private Ryan, a pivotal scene occurs when Captain Miller (played by Tom Hanks) recruits a clerk/interpreter for a high-stakes, dangerous mission. The clerk attempts to bring along his heavy typewriter and several large, cumbersome duffel bags. Recognizing the weight would be a liability, Captain Miller takes the typewriter, drops it on the ground, and provides the clerk with only a simple pencil and paper, declaring, "Leave all that junk behind, we're going to war."

Tags: Miracles, Courage, Boldness, Faith, Power
Story Summary: Pastor Miles references the legendary NFL safety Ronnie Lott and his description of the "Woo Hit." In football, a "Woo Hit" is an impact so powerful and definitive that it causes the entire stadium to collectively gasp and say "Woo!" It isn't just a tackle; it is a moment of absolute commitment where a player gives everything they have to stop the opponent and change the momentum of the game.
Spiritual Metaphor: This serves as a metaphor for the Christian life and the way believers should pursue the promises of God. To "take the promise" requires a "Woo Hit" level of commitment. It is the difference between half-hearted effort and total spiritual immersion. When a believer engages their faith with that level of intensity and power, it creates a visible, undeniable impact on the world around them. It challenges the listener to stop playing tentatively and start living with the power and conviction that God’s promises demand.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles references the legendary NFL safety Ronnie Lott and his description of the "Woo Hit." In football, a "Woo Hit" is an impact so powerful and definitive that it causes the entire stadium to collectively gasp and say "Woo!" It isn't just a tackle; it is a moment of absolute commitment where a player gives everything they have to stop the opponent and change the momentum of the game.

Tags: Flesh, Discipline, Persistence, Temptation
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares a personal anecdote regarding his daughter’s persistence when asking for $100. She did not ask once and then walk away discouraged; she was relentless, coming back repeatedly to make her request known. Her persistence was born out of her relationship with her father and her confidence that he had the resources and the heart to provide what she was asking for.
Spiritual Metaphor: This illustration highlights the necessity of persistence in prayer and the boldness required to claim God's promises. Many believers ask God for something once and give up if they don't see an immediate result. Pastor Miles uses this to demonstrate that "taking the promise" often involves a continuous, confident pursuit. Just as a child persists with a loving father, we are encouraged to remain steadfast in our petitions, trusting in God's character and His timing to fulfill His word.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares a personal anecdote regarding his daughter’s persistence when asking for $100. She did not ask once and then walk away discouraged; she was relentless, coming back repeatedly to make her request known. Her persistence was born out of her relationship with her father and her confidence that he had the resources and the heart to provide what she was asking for.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - What Does Wisdom Say
Illustration Name: The Queen Termite and the 30,000 Eggs
Tags: Creation, Solomon, Science, Wisdom, Wonders
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes the biological function of a queen termite. He explains that her primary existence is dedicated to reproduction, characterizing her as a "laying machine" capable of producing 30,000 eggs in a single day. This illustration focuses on the singular focus and immense productivity of this creature within its ecosystem.
Spiritual Metaphor: This illustration serves as a reflection on divine design and the inherent wisdom found in creation. Just as the termite operates within its specific purpose with maximum output, wisdom dictates that individuals should understand their God-given design and fulfill their roles with diligence and consistency.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes the biological function of a queen termite. He explains that her primary existence is dedicated to reproduction, characterizing her as a "laying machine" capable of producing 30,000 eggs in a single day. This illustration focuses on the singular focus and immense productivity of this creature within its ecosystem.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - What Does Wisdom Say
Tags: Habits, Weaning, Discipline, Stewardship, Comfort
Story Summary: Pastor Miles discusses the habit of spending money on luxury items, specifically referencing a $9.00 Starbucks order. He shares the concept of "weaning" oneself off such expenses. The focus is on the intentionality required to stop a habitual expenditure in order to reallocate those resources elsewhere.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is a practical lesson in stewardship and financial wisdom. It illustrates that wisdom often requires the discipline to sacrifice immediate desires or "wants" for a greater spiritual or practical purpose. Weaning oneself from unnecessary costs is presented as an act of taking control over one’s resources to align with wiser living.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles discusses the habit of spending money on luxury items, specifically referencing a $9.00 Starbucks order. He shares the concept of "weaning" oneself off such expenses. The focus is on the intentionality required to stop a habitual expenditure in order to reallocate those resources elsewhere.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - The Reasons We Run From God
Illustration Name: Witnessing to His Father by Miramar Lake
Tags: Family, Witnessing, Altar Call, Salvation, Father
Story Summary: In this personal account, Pastor Miles McPherson describes a pivotal moment spent with his father at Miramar Lake. During their time together, Pastor Miles felt a deep conviction to share the Gospel and the message of salvation with his father. The story explores the initial hesitation and the eventual breakthrough that occurred during their conversation by the water.
Spiritual Metaphor: This illustration serves as a powerful metaphor for the urgency of evangelism within the family unit. It demonstrates that "running from God" can often be a generational or relational barrier that requires bold, loving intervention. The application focuses on the courage required to witness to those closest to us and the restorative power of God's grace when we stop running and start sharing.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson describes a pivotal moment spent with his father at Miramar Lake. During their time together, Pastor Miles felt a deep conviction to share the Gospel and the message of salvation with his father. The story explores the initial hesitation and the eventual breakthrough that occurred during their conversation by the water.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - The Reasons We Run From God
Tags: Urgency, Witnessing, Tragedy, Salvation, Testimony
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares the heart-wrenching and profound entries from the journal of a 14-year-old girl named April. The journal provides a window into the soul of a young person grappling with her faith, her struggles, and her desire to be close to God despite the temptations and distractions that lead people to run away from Him.
Spiritual Metaphor: April’s journal serves as a metaphor for the internal tug-of-war between the flesh and the spirit. It highlights the transparency and sincerity required to maintain a relationship with God. The preaching application emphasizes that even in our youth or our darkest moments of running, God is listening to the honest cries of our hearts and offers a path back to hope and redemption.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares the heart-wrenching and profound entries from the journal of a 14-year-old girl named April. The journal provides a window into the soul of a young person grappling with her faith, her struggles, and her desire to be close to God despite the temptations and distractions that lead people to run away from Him.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - The Plan - Part 1, Remember
Illustration Name: The Orange Boundary Line at Donovan Prison
Tags: Prison, Warriors, Calling, Identity, Battle
Story Summary: Pastor Miles McPherson recounts his experience visiting Donovan State Prison. During the visit, he observed a physical orange line painted on the floor of the facility. This line serves as a strict boundary for the inmates; they are instructed that they must never cross it. The illustration highlights the absolute nature of this boundary within the prison environment and the immediate consequences or tension that arises when such a line is approached or ignored.
Spiritual Metaphor: The orange line serves as a powerful metaphor for God's boundaries and "The Plan" He has for our lives. Just as the inmates have a clear marker that ensures order and safety within the prison context, God provides spiritual and moral boundaries designed for our protection and purpose. When we "Remember the Plan," we are called to acknowledge these divine boundaries. Crossing the line represents a departure from God's intended path, leading to spiritual danger or a loss of freedom. The illustration encourages the congregation to respect the "orange lines" in their own lives—those clear scriptural mandates and convictions that keep them within the safety of God's will.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson recounts his experience visiting Donovan State Prison. During the visit, he observed a physical orange line painted on the floor of the facility. This line serves as a strict boundary for the inmates; they are instructed that they must never cross it. The illustration highlights the absolute nature of this boundary within the prison environment and the immediate consequences or tension that arises when such a line is approached or ignored.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Vision Sunday 2012 by Miles McPherson
Tags: Vision, Goals, Persistence, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Pastor Miles wanted to go to the NFL, but no one from his small Division III school had ever made it. He put a Dallas Cowboys team photo in a cheap frame on the floor of his roach-infested apartment and stared at it daily, declaring his preferred future.
Spiritual Metaphor: A vision is a picture of your preferred future. God wants us to have a thriving, thriving-focused spiritual vision for our lives instead of a mere survival mentality.
Full Story Text: In this illustration, Pastor Miles McPherson reflects on his time as a professional football player with the San Diego Chargers. He shares a personal anecdote regarding his relationship with his wife, Debbie, noting that they were dating during this period. The core of the story centers on the Dallas Cowboys team picture, using the concept of being "in the frame" or part of a professional team's collective visual identity to set the stage for the church’s vision.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Vision Sunday 2012 by Miles McPherson
Tags: Service, Redemption, Ministry, Prison
Story Summary: Pastor Miles met a 6'3" former Hell's Angel member who was nervous about attending his first church knitting/crochet ministry meeting. He had learned the skill during his 12-year prison sentence and was now using it to knit hats for premature babies.
Spiritual Metaphor: God can redeem any rugged, broken past and use our hands to perform beautiful, gentle, and unexpected acts of service for His kingdom.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes a specific volunteer whose outward appearance contrasts sharply with his service activity. The man, described as having the rugged, tough appearance of a "Hell's Angel" biker, is found engaged in the delicate and meticulous task of crocheting. This striking visual juxtaposition highlights the unexpected ways individuals contribute to the church's mission.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Why Bad Things Happen to Good People by Miles McPherson
Tags: Sin, Temptation, Danger, Wisdom
Story Summary: Pastor Miles entered a 4-acre cage in South Africa to feed six lions directly. The local pastor warned him that although they appeared tame, they were still wild. Six months later, a tourist was killed by those exact lions.
Spiritual Metaphor: Secret sins we try to "tame" and play with are still wild, dangerous predators waiting to destroy us if we let them remain.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts an experience in South Africa where he had the opportunity to feed lions. He describes the visceral reality of being in close proximity to such powerful predators. The core of the story revolves around the specific instructions required to feed them safely: you have to throw the meat properly and stay behind the protection, recognizing that while the lions are magnificent, they are also dangerous and demand respect. He emphasizes the focus required during the exchange between the provider and the lion.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - The Fine Line - Part 1, Wake Up and Look at The Fine Line by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: The Republican National Convention (RNC) Teleprompter Training
Tags: Worldview, Distraction, Focus, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Pastor Miles was trained by a strict speech coach for his prayer at the RNC. She taught him to read the teleprompter based on exact symbols and ignore the distracting crowd, focusing only on the skeptical camera.
Spiritual Metaphor: We must learn to walk "the fine line," ignoring the massive distractions of the world and keeping our focus entirely on the lens of God's truth.
Full Story Text:
In preparation for speaking at the Republican National Convention, Pastor Miles McPherson describes the rigorous training required for teleprompter usage. He recounts the intense environment of the convention floor and the need for absolute focus.

A pivotal detail of this preparation involved his wife, Debbie. To help him master his delivery amidst potential chaos, they practiced in their hotel room. Debbie roleplayed as a highly distracted audience member—talking on a cell phone, walking around, and creating noise—to simulate the environment he would face (referenced further at 01:03:17). Additionally, Miles reflects on the foundation of their relationship, noting how they got married around the time his ministry journey was taking shape (around 00:34:04).

Sermon Title: Rock Church - The Fine Line - Part 1, Wake Up and Look at The Fine Line by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: The Double-Pound Sign and the "In Jesus' Name" Edit
Tags: Integrity, Witnessing, Courage, Jesus
Story Summary: At the RNC, the speech editor crossed out "In Jesus' Name" at the end of Miles' prayer and replaced it with "To the Lord Our God." Miles chose to ignore the edit and boldly prayed "In Jesus' Name" on national television.
Spiritual Metaphor: The world will try to edit and water down our Christian testimony to make it non-offensive. We must remain bold and proclaim the name of Jesus without compromise.
Full Story Text:
While reviewing the script for his national address, Pastor Miles encountered the technical formatting of a teleprompter script. Specifically, he explains the "double-pound sign" (##), which signifies the end of the text—the point where the speaker must stop because there are no more words provided.

During the script editing process, there was a discussion regarding the conclusion of his prayer.
