He was encouraged to edit or shorten the phrase "In Jesus' Name" to fit the secular platform's constraints. This created a moment of decision regarding his identity as a representative of Christ versus a guest on a political stage.

Tags: Character, Suffering, Trials, Testing
Story Summary: To find out what flavor is inside a teabag without a paper label, you must submerge it in hot water. Similarly, trials and hot water in life reveal a Christian's true internal character and faith.
Spiritual Metaphor: Peaceful times don't test our true spiritual standing. The hot water of trials, suffering, and sacrifice reveals our authentic character and devotion.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles McPherson uses the simple, relatable metaphor of a teabag to explain the mechanics of suffering. He describes how a teabag can sit in a cup, and you may know what kind of tea it is by the label, but you don't actually experience the "stuff" inside until it is submerged in hot water. The hot water doesn't put the tea inside the bag; it merely draws out what is already there.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Easter 2011 by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: The Atheist Professor and the Marine
Tags: Testimony, Courage, Humour, Marines, Defense of Faith
Story Summary: A Marine taking college classes after tours in Afghanistan and Iraq is in a class where an atheist professor challenges God to prove His existence by knocking him off the stage in five minutes. Just past four minutes, the Marine walks up and knocks the professor off the stage. When the professor wakes up and asks why, the Marine explains that God was too busy protecting troops in Afghanistan, so he figured he would help Him out.
Spiritual Metaphor: God is active, alive, and powerful, even when mockers demand immediate, supernatural signs. The ultimate, infallible proof of His existence is not found in cheap, on-demand tricks, but in the historic reality of the Resurrection.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles McPherson tells a humorous but impactful story about a Marine who was taking college classes after completing tours in Afghanistan and Iraq. During a class, the atheist professor challenged God's existence by stating: "I challenge God to prove He really exists by knocking me off the stage in the next five minutes." The professor continuously mocked God, and the class sat in shocked silence as the minutes ticked by.

Just past the four-minute mark, the Marine suddenly jumped out of his seat, marched to the front, and punched the professor in the face, knocking him off the stage. After sitting back down, the stunned professor regained consciousness and asked the Marine why he did that. The Marine replied: "Brother, God is too busy protecting the troops in Afghanistan to give you the right to say nonsense like that, so I figured I would help Him out." While Pastor Miles clarifies that he is not advocating physical violence, the story illustrates a bold refusal to let God's existence be mocked.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Easter 2011 by Miles McPherson
Tags: Football, Authority, Confidence, Prediction, Victory
Story Summary: Pastor Miles recalls DOI Williams, a fast receiver from his professional football days. During practice, DOI Williams would come out of the huddle and tell defenders exactly what he was about to do: 'I'm going deep... straight up the field, and there's nothing you can do about it.' Despite knowing his route, they could never stop him because of his speed.
Spiritual Metaphor: Jesus Christ announced His resurrection in advance to His disciples and Roman authorities. Like DOI Williams, Jesus told death and the Roman guards that He would get up in three days and there was nothing they could do about it, demonstrating His unstoppable power.
Full Story Text: Drawing from his professional football career, Pastor Miles describes a fast receiver named DOI Williams. During practices, when the defense was covering him one-on-one, DOI Williams would boldly come out of the huddle and tell the defenders exactly what he was about to do: "I'm going deep... straight up the field, and there's nothing you can do about it." Because he possessed such extraordinary speed, he could warn his opponents in advance, and they still had no way of stopping him from catching the ball.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Easter 2011 by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: Stepping on a Loose Rock in the Mountain Stream
Tags: Childhood, Brother, Stream, Instability, Trust, Rock
Story Summary: As children visiting their grandparents in the mountains, Miles and his brothers were warned by their father not to get wet in the property's stream. To cross it, they stepped on protruding rocks. Miles' brother stepped on a large, seemingly stable rock, but it was sitting on a much smaller, unstable rock underneath. The rock toppled over, and his brother fell completely in the water in slow motion, crying out about getting a whooping.
Spiritual Metaphor: The rock looked solid but had an unstable foundation, leading to a fall. Many place their faith in unstable 'rocks' of the world like feelings or human promises. Jesus Christ is the only truly immovable and secure foundation of our salvation.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles recalls a childhood memory of traveling up into the mountains to visit his retired grandparents. Their property had a beautiful, thirty-foot-wide stream running through it. Every time they went outside, their father gave them one strict instruction: "Don't get wet!" Naturally, as boys, they immediately went to play in the stream.

To get across to the other side without getting caught, they attempted to step on the rocks protruding from the rushing water. Miles' brother spotted a large, seemingly stable rock and stepped onto it. Unfortunately, the large rock was resting on a much smaller, unstable rock underneath. The moment he put his weight on it, the rock toppled over. In slow motion, his brother fell into the stream, crying out, "I'm getting a whoop [whooping]!" before being completely submerged. Miles immediately ran to tell their father.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Blessed - Part 1, The Money Metaphor by Miles McPherson
Tags: Childhood, Rain, Gutter, Blockage, Flow, Stewardship
Story Summary: During rainstorms, Miles and his brothers would sit on the curb and watch water flow down the gutter, placing sticks in it to form a dam. Once a large puddle built up behind the sticks, they would suddenly pull the sticks out, causing the water to rush down with a dramatic whoosh.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's blessings are ready to pour out on our lives, but we often have blockages (sticks) like greed or fear clogging the flow. Removing these blockages through stewardship lets blessings flow as a pass-through (whoosh) to others, rather than stopping in a cul-de-sac.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls a childhood memory of playing in the rain. He and his brothers would sit on the curb and watch the rainwater rush down the gutter. They would place sticks in the gutter, which would occasionally get caught and begin forming a small dam. As the sticks piled up, the water would build up behind them, creating a large, growing puddle. Once the puddle reached its peak, they would suddenly remove the sticks, and the water would rush down the street with a dramatic "whoosh!"

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Blessed - Part 1, The Money Metaphor by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: How Did That Brother Get That Girl?
Tags: Humour, Relationships, Favour, Undeserved Blessing, Sovereignty
Story Summary: Miles humorously notes that we often see an ordinary-looking man walking with an exceptionally beautiful wife or girlfriend. When asked how he got her, the man whispers that he doesn't know and is just waiting for her to wake up.
Spiritual Metaphor: Being blessed is experiencing favor and grace far beyond what our own efforts could ever earn. We cannot explain or take credit for God's blessings (like being born in a wealthy country or receiving eternal life); we must simply receive them with humble gratitude.
Full Story Text: In a lighthearted moment, Pastor Miles talks about the common experience of seeing an ordinary-looking man walking with an extraordinarily beautiful wife or girlfriend. Observers naturally wonder, "How did that brother get that girl?" If you ask the man, he will often whisper, "Brother, I don't know, but don't say anything! I'm just counting the days before she wakes up!"

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Blessed - Part 1, The Money Metaphor by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: The Three Employees and the Businessman's Wife
Tags: Stewardship, Business, Accountability, Bride, Church, Trust
Story Summary: A businessman goes on a six-month trip, leaving three employees with $10,000 a month each and instructing them to give his wife $1,000 a month. The first gives exactly $1,000 (10%), the second gives $2,000 (20%), and the third reduces it to $800, then $300, and then stops. Upon returning, the businessman rewards the first, double-rewards the second, and promises to punish the third.
Spiritual Metaphor: Jesus is the businessman, His Bride (the Church) is the wife, and we are the employees entrusted with resources. Giving our tithe is returning what belongs to Him to support His Bride. Withholding our giving is withholding from Christ's Bride, for which we will be held accountable.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shares a hypothetical story of a businessman who goes on a six-month trip and leaves three of his employees with $10,000 a month each. Before leaving, he gives them one simple instruction: "I want each of you to give my wife $1,000 a month while I am away." The first employee does exactly what was asked, giving the wife $1,000 each month (10%). The second employee is exceptionally generous and gives her $2,000 a month (20%). The third employee, however, decides she doesn't need that much and reduces his giving—providing $800 the first month, $300 the next month, and then stopping entirely, keeping the rest for himself.

When the businessman returns, he asks his wife how the employees treated her. She reports their actions. The businessman rewards the first, double-rewards the second, and says of the third: "Oh, I'm gonna get him."

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Blessed - Part 1, The Money Metaphor by Miles McPherson
Tags: Creation, Nature, Trust, Provision, Stewardship, Worry
Story Summary: Miles describes a bird that sings in his yard around 6:20 AM when it's still pitch black. The bird has no food in its nest, no 401k, no retirement, and no roof over its kids' heads, yet it sings with joyful trust.
Spiritual Metaphor: The bird trusts God's daily design for provision, completely free of stress or worry. If God takes perfect care of the birds, how much more will He care for His children? Financial worry reveals a lack of faith in our Creator's character.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes his early morning routine. He often wakes up before sunrise, when it is still pitch black outside. Right around 6:20 AM, the same bird starts singing its heart out. He notes that the bird has absolutely no food in its nest, no roof over its kids' heads, no 401k, and no retirement fund. Yet, despite having zero physical security or stored-up resources, the bird sings joyfully in the dark.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - What if? - Rock Church 10-Year Anniversary by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: The CUP Hearing and the Haircut
Tags: Trials, Focus, Commitment, Marriage, Wife, Liberty Station
Story Summary: During the stressful Conditional Use Permit (CUP) hearing for Liberty Station, Miles shaved his head on vacation and vowed not to cut it again until they got the permit. A photo of the hearing shows Miles looking stern and intense, but his wife Debbie looks equally mad because she absolutely hated his haircut. Miles was praying they would get the permit so he could grow his hair back.
Spiritual Metaphor: Pursuing God's vision requires enduring stressful trials and standing firm, even through uncomfortable situations (represented by the haircut). It also highlights the importance of spouse partnership and supporting each other through high-stakes spiritual burdens.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles describes the highly stressful process of securing the Conditional Use Permit (CUP) from the city of San Diego to open their landmark Liberty Station facility. While on vacation during this period, he shaved his head and made a vow to God: he would not cut or shave his hair again until the church successfully received the permit.

A photograph from the final city hearing captures the tension. In the picture, Miles looks incredibly stern and intense because of the high stakes of the vote. However, his wife, Debbie, looks equally upset in the photo—not because of the hearing itself, but because she absolutely detested his haircut! Miles jokes that during those intense months, he was praying they'd get the permit not just for the building, but so he could grow his hair back and his wife would love him again.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - What if? - Rock Church 10-Year Anniversary by Miles McPherson
Tags: Service, Volunteering, Fire Evacuation, Elders, Fun, Humour
Story Summary: During the 2007 San Diego fires, the church evacuated 122 elderly convalescent home residents. Miles challenged the sweet lady resident who was known as their baddest shuffleboard player to a match. He later went to the home for a best-of-three, trash-talking her in the final rubber match until he won, joking that he couldn't let granny win.
Spiritual Metaphor: The true power of the church is demonstrated when it serves others in crisis. Ministry is fundamentally relational and down-to-earth; it builds bridges through practical support, fun, and laughter, showing the joy of God's house.
Full Story Text:
During the devastating San Diego wildfires of 2007, the Rock Church transformed its facility into an evacuation center, collecting massive amounts of supplies and mobilizing thousands of volunteers. They sheltered 122 elderly residents who had been displaced from their convalescent home. Pastor Miles went to spend time with them on the second floor and asked what game they usually played.

One resident replied that they played shuffleboard and identified a sweet lady as the "baddest shuffleboard player" in their home. Miles immediately challenged her, saying he was going to come and beat her. Later, Miles visited their convalescent home for a best-of-three match. After winning one game each, the match came down to a final, high-stakes rubber match. Miles trash-talked her, saying, "Come on Grandma, you can't get that!" and made her nervous enough that he won. Miles jokes, "Grandma or not, I can't be letting granny win! It's just how God made me."

Sermon Title: Rock Church - What if? - Rock Church 10-Year Anniversary by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: The Mexico City Movie Theater Chase
Tags: Testimony, Fear, Spiritual Warfare, Courage, Devil, Victory
Story Summary: Miles tells of a teenage friend who was alone in a movie theater in Mexico City when a creepy man entered, sat in his row, and moved progressively closer. The teen bolted, with the man chasing him into a bathroom, and then down the street until the teen hid under a parked car and watched the man's feet run past.
Spiritual Metaphor: Many Christians live in constant flight from the devil's lies, guilt, and fear, hiding under metaphorical cars. Since greater is He who is in us, we must stop running from ghosts, stand our ground, confront the devil, and walk in victory.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shares a chilling but true story of a friend who went to a movie theater in Mexico City as a teenager. His friend was completely alone in the theater until a strange man walked in. Instead of sitting far away, the man sat in the very same row, just five seats down. A few minutes later, the man moved one seat closer. Then another seat closer.

Terrified, the teenager decided that if the man moved one more seat closer, he would run. The man moved, and the teenager bolted from the row. The man immediately chased him out of the theater, into the lobby, and then into a bathroom where the teenager hid inside a toilet stall. The man ran into the bathroom, missed him, and the teenager fled into the street, eventually diving under a parked car. From beneath the car, he watched the man's feet run past. The man never caught him.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 1, The Plan by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: The Road Trip Competition to New York
Tags: Personal Testimony, NFL, Chargers, Travel, Competition, Journey
Story Summary: After completing his rookie season with the Chargers in 1982, and right before the second season in 1983, Pastor Miles decided to drive home to his family in New York in his 5-speed 280ZX car. He wanted to make it a competition and drive it faster than anyone had ever told him they did. Treating it as a game to win, he drove from San Diego to New York in just two and a half days, pushed by his competitive drive.
Spiritual Metaphor: Your life is like a journey from the womb to the tomb, and everything in between is your trip. Just as you must make careful preparations when going on a long road trip—such as checking the oil, inflating tires, preparing a first aid kit, and consulting a map—you must do the exact same in your spiritual life. The road of life is long, and we encounter the same kinds of trials and unexpected detours that we do on a cross-country drive. To navigate safely, we must ensure we have made proper spiritual preparations.
Full Story Text: After completing his rookie season with the San Diego Chargers in 1982, and right before the second season in 1983, Pastor Miles decided to drive home to his family in New York. He had a 5-speed 280ZX car (280Z), which he describes as "the love of my life," and wanted to drive it across the country. He started asking around to find out how long the drive would normally take because he wanted to make it into a competition and drive it faster than anyone had ever told him they did. Treating it as a little game to win, he ended up driving from San Diego to New York in just two and a half days, pushed by his competitive drive.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 1, The Plan by Miles McPherson
Tags: High School, Teacher, Letter of Recommendation, MIT, Abilities, Identity
Story Summary: In the 10th grade, Miles wanted to pursue engineering and applied for a summer program at MIT. Since they only selected 40 students from the East Coast, he had to get a letter of recommendation from his chemistry teacher. The teacher wrote a glowing letter about Miles' character and intelligence. Surprised, Miles asked him, 'Do you really believe this?' to which the teacher replied, 'Absolutely.' This letter changed how Miles saw himself and helped him get accepted. Over 30 years later, he still keeps that letter.
Spiritual Metaphor: God has placed qualities, abilities, and potential within us that we do not even realize we have. He has created us for good works that He has prepared in advance. Often, it is during painful situations or when a spiritual authority speaks truth over us that we realize we are much stronger, smarter, wiser, and more patient than we believed. God has already fully prepared and equipped you for everything He has called you to do—you simply have to believe Him.
Full Story Text: When Pastor Miles was in the 10th grade, he wanted to pursue engineering and heard about a summer program at MIT (Massachusetts Institute of Technology), one of the best schools in the world. The program only selected 40 high school students from the East Coast, and to apply, Miles had to submit his grades and get a letter of recommendation from a teacher. He asked his chemistry teacher, with whom he was very close. The teacher wrote a letter praising Miles' character, intelligence, and potential that impacted him so deeply that he went up to him and asked, "Do you really believe this?" The teacher looked at him and said, "Absolutely." The letter made Miles see himself in a way he had never seen himself before, helping him get accepted into the program. Over 30 years later, he still keeps that letter.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 1, The Plan by Miles McPherson
Tags: AAA, Travel, Map, TripTik, Directions, Obedience
Story Summary: Before driving across the country, Pastor Miles went to AAA because he had no idea how to navigate. They provided him with a custom 'TripTik' map consisting of pages representing 100 miles, with a yellow highlighted line marking his route. His job was simple: follow the yellow line and turn where the highlighted line turned. Whenever he missed an exit, he would become lost, forcing him to turn around and get right back on the yellow highlighted route to continue home safely.
Spiritual Metaphor: The yellow highlighted line represents God's Word and His commandments. Our primary purpose in life is very simple: obey God and follow His directions. God has already laid out the path and mapped out the route to get us safely from point A to point B. When we deviate from His instructions or stop paying attention, we get spiritually lost. Repentance is the spiritual equivalent of making a U-turn—turning away from our missteps to get right back on God's highlighted path of obedience.
Full Story Text: Before driving across the country to New York, Pastor Miles visited AAA because he had absolutely no idea how to navigate. They provided him with a custom "TripTik" map, which consisted of individual pages representing about 100 miles of the trip. The map had a yellow highlighted line drawn over the specific roads he was supposed to take. His job was incredibly simple: follow the yellow line and turn where the highlighted line turned. Whenever he wasn't paying attention and missed an exit, he would immediately become lost, forcing him to turn around and get right back on the yellow highlighted route to continue home safely.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 1, The Plan by Miles McPherson
Tags: Missions, Mexico, Disobedience, Discernment, Scam, Shoes
Story Summary: On a mission trip to Mexico, a 5-year-old girl standing in the mud in the rain with no shoes asked Pastor Miles for shoes. Miles wanted to help her, but God clearly spoke to him: 'No zapatos.' Miles argued with God in Spanish, but eventually obeyed. Later, someone else from the youth group gave her shoes, and she walked away. Immediately after, the local pastor came out and warned them not to give her anything because her mother sends her to pretend to be poor and then sells the clothes and shoes down the street.
Spiritual Metaphor: This story illustrates that our job is to obey God, even when His instructions make no sense to our human logic or conflict with our emotions. Because God is all-knowing, He sees the entire picture and knows the hidden motives, while our understanding is highly limited. Walking by faith means trusting God's direction over what we see or feel, knowing that His guidance is always right, even when it appears counterintuitive.
Full Story Text: While on a youth missions trip to Mexico giving out clothes and food to children, a 5-year-old girl standing in the mud in the cold rain with ponytails and no shoes came up to Pastor Miles and said, "Necesito zapatos" (I need shoes). Miles wanted to give her shoes immediately, but God spoke to his heart and said, "No zapatos." Miles tried to argue with God, attempting to explain in Spanish why she needed them, but eventually obeyed. Later on, another guy from the youth group gave her shoes, and she walked away. Immediately after, the local pastor came out and warned them: "If that girl comes back, do not give her anything. Her mother sends her up here to pretend to be poor; they take the shoes and clothes, go down the street, and sell them."

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 1, The Plan by Miles McPherson
Tags: Football, Chargers, Injury, Monday Night Football, Raiders, Knee Surgery, Witnessing
Story Summary: In his third year with the Chargers, starting defensive back Miles went up to intercept a pass on Monday Night Football. Distracted by the lights and planes, he collided with his own teammate, Lyndon King, who was running in the wrong direction. Lyndon's casted arm smashed Miles' knee, tearing it and ending his starting season. On the two-hour bus ride back to LA with a massive ice pack, God challenged Miles to witness to the surgeon next to him. Despite his devastating pain, Miles spent the ride sharing Jesus with the doctor.
Spiritual Metaphor: Collisions and painful trials will happen in life, often coming from 'friendly fire'—our own teammates or loved ones. These events can feel like the end of our dreams and plans. However, pain is not a useless experience. While we cannot always control or understand our pain, we can control how we respond to it. God often uses our brokenness and painful situations to place us in positions where we can be powerful witnesses to others who are trapped next to us, displaying His strength through our weakness.
Full Story Text: During his third year playing for the Chargers, Miles had earned his spot as a starting defensive back. In a highly anticipated Monday Night Football matchup against the Raiders at the LA Coliseum, Jim Plunkett threw a pass that was perfectly set up for Miles to intercept. Miles jumped high into the air, expecting to make a clean catch and score an easy touchdown. However, distracted by the bright stadium lights and planes overhead, he was suddenly hit by his own teammate, Lyndon King, who was running in the wrong direction swinging his casted arm. The cast struck Miles directly across his knee, tearing it and ending his starting season. While on the bus for the two-hour ride back to LA with a massive ice pack on his knee, God challenged Miles to witness to the team surgeon sitting next to him. Despite his devastating, career-altering pain, Miles spent the ride sharing Jesus with the doctor.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 3, Purpose by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: Akeelah and the Bee & Crying in Movies
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Movie, Akeelah and the Bee, Crying, Rules, Dr. Larabee, Debbie, Feelings
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares about watching the movie Akeelah and the Bee, about an 11-year-old spelling bee prodigy coached by the strict Dr. Larabee. Dr. Larabee demands she stop speaking ebonics, saying 'We don't talk like that here.' When she disobeys, he kicks her out, forcing her to realize she must do things his way if she wants to win. Miles admits he cried five times, comparing himself to his wife, Debbie: Debbie openly cries and says 'I'm crying right now,' while Miles tries to hide his tears and act tough.
Spiritual Metaphor: Just as Dr. Larabee insisted on absolute compliance with his rules to coach Akeelah, God is incredibly stubborn and uncompromising when it comes to His standards. God does not operate a democracy or negotiate His terms; it is His way or no way. If we want His blessing, we must completely submit to His standards and let go of our pride, emotions, and habits. Loving God is not about feelings, but about absolute obedience to His written Word.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares about watching the movie Akeelah and the Bee, which tells the story of an 11-year-old spelling bee prodigy from South Central Los Angeles. She seeks coaching from Dr. Larabee (played by Laurence Fishburne), a stern professor. Dr. Larabee is very hard and strict with her, telling her she must stop speaking double negatives and ebonics, saying "We don't talk like that here." When she continues to disobey, he immediately kicks her out of his house, forcing her to realize that she must do things his way if she wants to win. Pastor Miles admits he cried five times during the movie. He humorously compares himself to his wife, Debbie: when Debbie cries, she openly admits it, saying "I'm crying right now," while Miles tries to hide his tears and act tough, saying "I ain't telling nobody I'm crying."

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 3, Purpose by Miles McPherson
Tags: Nature, Orange Tree, Fruit, Salvation, Obedience, Holy Spirit
Story Summary: Pastor Miles notes that you will never see an orange tree producing apples. When they are just green trees with leaves, most people cannot distinguish an orange tree from an apple tree until the fruit appears. He acts out a tree grunting and straining to force fruit out, noting that real trees don't grunt—they just blow in the wind, and oranges naturally grow from within because of what they are.
Spiritual Metaphor: In the Christian life, we do not perform good works or obey rules in order to become saved (get on the team), but rather, our obedience and good works are the natural 'fruit' of our salvation. The Holy Spirit inside us naturally produces spiritual fruit (love, obedience, change of habits) without us having to gruntingly force it in our own strength. True fruit is evidence of who we are in Christ.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles notes that you will never see an orange tree producing apples. When they are just green trees with leaves, most people cannot distinguish an orange tree from an apple tree. It is only when the fruit actually appears that its identity becomes unmistakable. He acts out a tree grunting and straining to force fruit out, noting that real trees don't grunt—they just blow in the wind, and oranges naturally grow from within because of what they are.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 3, Purpose by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: Jumping on Unstable Rocks in the Stream
Tags: Childhood, Grandparents, New York, Stream, Brother, Rocks, Foundation, Trust
Story Summary: As kids visiting their grandparents in upstate New York, Miles and his brothers ignored their father's warning: 'Don't get wet!' and played in the stream. They made a game of jumping across protruding rocks to the other side. One day, Miles' brother jumped on a large rock that seemed stable but was actually sitting on a much smaller, unstable rock underneath. The rock tumbled, and in slow motion, his brother fell backward and was completely submerged.
Spiritual Metaphor: Faith is only as good as the object and foundation it is placed on. When we make decisions based on shifting, unstable things—like our emotions, the culture, or human opinions—we are jumping on unstable rocks and will inevitably fall. God's Word is the only solid, objective fact we can trust. Building our lives on His truth ensures a solid and secure footing.
Full Story Text: When Pastor Miles and his two brothers were young, they would visit their retired grandparents in upstate New York. Their property had a beautiful, thirty-foot-wide rushing stream. Every time they left the house, their father would warn them, "Don't get wet!" which naturally made them want to play near the stream. They created a game to jump across the protruding rocks to the other side without getting wet. One day, Miles' brother jumped on a large, prominent rock that seemed safe, but it was sitting on a much smaller, unstable rock beneath the surface. The rock tumbled, and in slow motion, his brother fell backward into the water, getting fully submerged.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - DO Something - Part 3, Purpose by Miles McPherson
Tags: Gibbons Monkey, Trap, Coconut, Hunter, Candy, Surrender, Money, Pride
Story Summary: To catch the Gibbons monkey, hunters nail a hollowed-out metal coconut with a chain to a tree, filling it with rice and candy. The monkey reaches its hand inside and grabs a handful. Because its fist is clenched around the food, it cannot pull its hand back out. Refusing to let go of the food, the monkey screams and pulls for hours. The hunter simply walks up and shoots the monkey.
Spiritual Metaphor: The monkey's captivity and death are entirely caused by its refusal to let go of the candy. Similarly, we often find ourselves trapped and spiritually defeated because we refuse to let go of the worldly things we trust in—whether it's money, physical beauty, intelligence, relationships, or career. To experience God's freedom and blessing, we must open our hands and surrender everything to Him.
Full Story Text: To catch the elusive Gibbons monkey, hunters nail a hollowed-out metal coconut to a tree trunk with a chain and spike, filling it with rice and candy. The monkey smells the sweet treats, reaches its hand inside the small hole, and grabs a huge handful. However, because its fist is now clenched and full of candy, it cannot pull its hand back out of the hole. Despite screaming, grunting, and pulling for hours, the monkey refuses to let go of the food. The hunter simply walks up and easily shoots the monkey.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - Edge of Darkness - Good Friday by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: Scaring the Kids & The Nature of Darkness
Tags: Father, Childhood, Scaring, Kids, Darkness, Devil, Strategy, Light
Story Summary: Pastor Miles' father went to great lengths to scare his children, such as hiding under their beds or wearing a monster mask with a candle under his head. This made Miles and his siblings terrified of the dark. Miles jokes that he passed this scaring tradition down to his own kids, still scaring them in their 20s. He explains to his children that physical darkness is not a real entity; it is simply the absence of light.
Spiritual Metaphor: While physical darkness is merely the absence of light, spiritual darkness is a very different and active force. It has a mind, an intent, a leader (the devil), a strategy, and a plan. The devil's goal is to destroy us and bring us to the edge of his darkness, throwing everything he can to pressure us. While the devil cannot force us to sin, he pressures us so intensely that we choose to disqualify ourselves from God's blessings. To defeat spiritual darkness, we must bring the active 'light' of Jesus Christ into our lives.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares how his father would go to great lengths to scare him and his siblings when they were children. His father would lay under their beds all night, waiting to grab them, or stand outside their bedroom window wearing a terrifying Halloween monster mask with a candle underneath. These experiences made the kids terrified of the dark, forcing them to sleep with their lights on. Miles jokingly admits that he passed this scaring tradition down to his own kids, still scaring them even now that they are in their 20s. He explains to his children that physical darkness is not actually a tangible entity; it is simply the natural result of removing light.

Illustration Name: The 40-Day Fast in Zero-Degree Marion, Indiana
Tags: Personal Testimony, Fasting, Discipline, Cold, Marion Indiana, Miles Ahead
Story Summary: In 1995, Pastor Miles was preparing for his first big youth Crusade at the San Diego Sports Arena with Miles Ahead Ministries. He met a couple in their mid-50s who had completed a 40-day fast, and he thought, "If they can do it, I can do it." He challenged his team and went on a fast: 19 days in October, then started again after Christmas for 40 days (ending Feb 4, 1996). During the final 10 days, he was traveling and preaching two or three times a day back East. In Marion, Indiana, it was literally zero degrees below zero. Miles had lost over 20 pounds, carrying his juicer from place to place in the freezing cold. He was skin and bones, but it was the most spiritually incredible experience of his life.
Spiritual Metaphor: Fasting is not about starvation; it is about separating yourself from your "perceived" life support (like food or coffee) so you can focus exclusively on your actual Life Support—Jesus Christ. Many people believe a long fast is impossible, but God provides the grace and strength when we submit our physical bodies to Him. By emptying our physical frames, we open ourselves up to be filled and empowered by the Holy Spirit for supernatural tasks.
Full Story Text: In 1995, Pastor Miles was preparing for his first big youth Crusade at the San Diego Sports Arena with Miles Ahead Ministries. He met a couple in their mid-50s who had completed a 40-day fast, and he thought, "If they can do it, I can do it." He challenged his team and went on a fast: 19 days in October, then started again after Christmas for 40 days (ending Feb 4, 1996). During the final 10 days, he was traveling and preaching two or three times a day back East. In Marion, Indiana, it was literally zero degrees below zero. Miles had lost over 20 pounds, carrying his juicer from place to place in the freezing cold. He was skin and bones, but it was the most spiritually incredible experience of his life.

Tags: Beach, Ocean, Horizon, Visual Metaphor, Perspective, Greatness
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes standing on the beach and looking out at the ocean. He notes that the Horizon we see is exactly 14 miles away. If you get in a boat and travel toward that Horizon, it just moves. The ocean stretches thousands of miles across, and the water is deeper than the mountains are high.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is like measuring God's power and presence in our lives. We only experience a tiny fraction of His power—a mere 14-mile horizon of our own limited perspective. But His power, wisdom, and love are infinite and immeasurable, extending far deeper and wider than we can comprehend. When we pray and fast, we are stepping out into the deep waters of His vast resources, moving past our limited personal horizons.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes standing on the beach and looking out at the ocean. He notes that the Horizon we see is exactly 14 miles away. If you get in a boat and travel toward that Horizon, it just moves. The ocean stretches thousands of miles across, and the water is deeper than the mountains are high.

Tags: Habits, Coffee, Caffeine, Addiction, Withdrawals, Self-Discipline
Story Summary: Miles tells about a close friend who decided to fast from coffee. Within a few days of stopping, his friend was literally found shaking under his desk with a massive withdrawal headache.
Spiritual Metaphor: We frequently spoil ourselves with minor daily comforts and luxuries, such as coffee, without realizing we have formed a deep dependency. When we claim "I gotta have my coffee to get my day started," we are putting a chemical before God. We are relying on caffeine for our daily strength instead of relying on the Holy Spirit. Fasting is designed to expose and break these minor daily addictions, training our flesh to submit to God so that our physical desires do not dictate our spiritual walk.
Full Story Text: Miles tells about a close friend who decided to fast from coffee. Within a few days of stopping, his friend was literally found shaking under his desk with a massive withdrawal headache.

Tags: Medical, Treadmill, Stress Test, Solitude, Rest, Hectic Life, Devil
Story Summary: In a medical stress test, doctors put you on a treadmill, hook up electrodes, speed it up, and incline it to push your heart rate to the max. You are forced to run faster and faster just to keep up with the machine, gasping for air as the test intensifies.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is what the devil does to our lives. He gets us running on a hectic, endless "treadmill" of work, worry, and busyness for years, wearing us into the ground. We are so busy keeping up with the treadmill that we have no time for prayer, solitude, or hearing God's voice. Fasting is God's way of stepping in and turning the treadmill off. It forces us to slow down, rest, and spend time in quiet solitude, realizing that God has everything under control and we don't need to live on the hectic, stressful treadmill of the world.
Full Story Text: In a medical stress test, doctors put you on a treadmill, hook up electrodes, speed it up, and incline it to push your heart rate to the max. You are forced to run faster and faster just to keep up with the machine, gasping for air as the test intensifies.

Illustration Name: Meeting Terry Jackson of the NY Giants
Tags: College, Coach, NFL, Terry Jackson, NY Giants, Relationship, Conversation
Story Summary: When Miles was a 19-year-old college student dreaming of going to the NFL, his coach introduced him to his first professional NFL player, Terry Jackson of the New York Giants. Miles was terrified and incredibly nervous, viewing Terry as a massive, untouchable celebrity. However, when they sat down in a room and talked for an hour, Miles realized that Terry was just a normal, down-to-earth human being. They ended up becoming close friends and eventually played against each other in the NFL.
Spiritual Metaphor: We often get incredibly nervous about praying because we view God as an untouchable, distant celebrity, and we don't know how to talk to Him. But prayer is simply a conversation with a Person who loves us and desires a relationship with us. God is not a good-luck charm, a rabbit's foot, or a genie in a bottle; He is a personal God. When we pray, we are having a two-way conversation, where we must speak honestly and, most importantly, quiet our hearts to listen to Him.
Full Story Text: When Miles was a 19-year-old college student dreaming of going to the NFL, his coach introduced him to his first professional NFL player, Terry Jackson of the New York Giants. Miles was terrified and incredibly nervous, viewing Terry as a massive, untouchable celebrity. However, when they sat down in a room and talked for an hour, Miles realized that Terry was just a normal, down-to-earth human being. They ended up becoming close friends and eventually played against each other in the NFL.

Tags: Acronym, AWCIPA, Prayer Model, Discipline, Focus, Alignment
Story Summary: Pastor Miles notes that many people pray randomly and unfocused—jumping from praying for their sick mother, to confessing a sin, to asking for a new car all in the same breath. To provide order and focus, Miles developed This Acronym: AWCIPA (A-W-C-I-P-A): A - Admire God, W - Wait on God, C - Confess your sin, I - Intercede for others, P - Petition for yourself, A - Admire God again.
Spiritual Metaphor: The AWCIPA model is designed to align our hearts with God's will. By focusing on one letter at a time, we learn to order our thoughts, humble ourselves, and listen to God. God already knows what we need before we ask, but prayer is about us getting in line with Him, not trying to get Him in line with us. When we pray through this structure, we learn to desire what He desires, leading to answered prayers and deep spiritual peace.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles notes that many people pray randomly and unfocused—jumping from praying for their sick mother, to confessing a sin, to asking for a new car all in the same breath. To provide order and focus, Miles developed This Acronym: AWCIPA (A-W-C-I-P-A):

A - Admire God (Start by telling God how good He is, putting Him first)

Sermon Title: The Invisible Man - Part 1, The Invisible Man is Qualified
Illustration Name: The Three Roles of a Construction Project
Tags: Construction, Liberty Station, Architect, Contract, Project Manager, Trinity
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes the major construction and remodeling process required to turn their 365-acre Naval Training Center (NTC) building into Liberty Station. In any massive building project, there are three distinct roles: 1. The person with the big plan/vision (who sees what the empty building can become—Miles jokes this is usually the wife/woman who sees how to remodel a house). 2. The person who executes the contract (who draws up the legal documents and pays for it). 3. The project manager (who is on the job every day, overseeing the construction, ensuring it is built according to specifications, on time, and on budget).
Spiritual Metaphor: This three-role process serves as a metaphor for the Holy Trinity. In the Plan of Salvation for our lives: God the Father is the visionary with the big plan. Jesus Christ is the one who executed the contract on the cross, purchasing our salvation with His blood. The Holy Spirit is the Project Manager, who is on the job inside our hearts every single day, implementing the plan, managing our struggles, performing miracles, and guiding us according to God's specifications.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles describes the major construction and remodeling process required to turn their 365-acre Naval Training Center (NTC) building into Liberty Station. In any massive building project, there are three distinct roles:

The person with the big plan/vision (who sees what the empty building can become—Miles jokes this is usually the wife/woman who sees how to remodel a house).

The person who executes the contract (who draws up the legal documents and pays for it).

The project manager (who is on the job every day, overseeing the construction, ensuring it is built according to specifications, on time, and on budget).

Sermon Title: The Invisible Man - Part 1, The Invisible Man is Qualified
Tags: Airport, Terminal 2, Glass, Architect, Design, Holy Spirit, Qualifications
Story Summary: When the church was selecting an architect to remodel their building, the developers asked Miles what San Diego buildings he liked. He admitted he liked Terminal 2 at the airport because of its massive, beautiful glass windows and grand pillars. The church ended up hiring the exact architectural firm that built Terminal 2, which is why the front lobby of the Rock Church features a massive glass window wall and towering atrium.
Spiritual Metaphor: The Holy Spirit is highly qualified and has an incredible portfolio. He didn't just build the physical universe; He designed us and wants to construct a beautiful, spiritual temple inside our hearts. When we look at the qualifications and portfolio of the Holy Spirit, we see that He is God, all-powerful, and all-knowing. We must learn to trust His "construction" in our lives because He is more than qualified to remodel our hearts.
Full Story Text: When the church was selecting an architect to remodel their building, the developers asked Miles what San Diego buildings he liked. He admitted he liked Terminal 2 at the airport because of its massive, beautiful glass windows and grand pillars. The church ended up hiring the exact architectural firm that built Terminal 2, which is why the front lobby of the Rock Church features a massive glass window wall and towering atrium.

Sermon Title: The Invisible Man - Part 1, The Invisible Man is Qualified
Tags: Board Meeting, Heaven, Care, Plans, Calling, Gifts
Story Summary: Miles imagines a celestial "board meeting" where God the Father, Jesus, and the Holy Spirit sit down together specifically to discuss an individual (like "Debbie"). They pull up her life on a big screen in space and review her progress. The Holy Spirit reports: "She's doing really well, but she is terrified of her calling to speak in public. I've been whispering to her, but she doesn't believe she has the gift." To help her discover her gift, they decide to have her pastor bring her up on stage.
Spiritual Metaphor: This illustration highlights that God's care for us is highly personal, detailed, and active. The Trinity is constantly coordinating, planning, and discussing your life, seeking to reveal your unique spiritual gifts, assignments, and calling. You are never forgotten or alone; the very Spirit of God is on the job daily, guiding you into your divine purpose.
Full Story Text: Miles imagines a celestial "board meeting" where God the Father, Jesus, and the Holy Spirit sit down together specifically to discuss an individual (like "Debbie"). They pull up her life on a big screen in space and review her progress. The Holy Spirit reports: "She's doing really well, but she is terrified of her calling to speak in public. I've been whispering to her, but she doesn't believe she has the gift." To help her discover her gift, they decide to have her pastor bring her up on stage.

Sermon Title: The Invisible Man - Part 1, The Invisible Man is Qualified
Tags: Beach Ball, Universe, Physics, Gravity, Astronomy, Supernatural, Outside the Box
Story Summary: Miles describes the physical universe as a giant beach ball expanding at the speed of light, containing all solar systems, planets, and galaxies. Inside this beach ball, there are precise physical laws (gravity, physics, biology, chemistry) that God set in motion to govern everything. High tide and low tide are precisely controlled by the moon's gravity, and everything operates on perfect Clockwork.
Spiritual Metaphor: Human beings are trapped inside this "beach ball" and make all their decisions based on its natural, physical laws. However, God exists entirely on the *outside* of the beach ball (the supernatural realm) and is not bound by its laws. When the Holy Spirit lives inside us, He brings the supernatural laws and power from *outside* the beach ball into our daily lives. This enables us to experience miracles, answers to prayer, and divine provision that completely defy natural math, science, and limitations.
Full Story Text: Miles describes the physical universe as a giant beach ball expanding at the speed of light, containing all solar systems, planets, and galaxies. Inside this beach ball, there are precise physical laws (gravity, physics, biology, chemistry) that God set in motion to govern everything. High tide and low tide are precisely controlled by the moon's gravity, and everything operates on perfect Clockwork.

Sermon Title: The Invisible Man - Part 1, The Invisible Man is Qualified
Illustration Name: Getting Cut from the Chargers while Debbie was Pregnant
Tags: Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Miles recounts a highly stressful period in his early career. After being drafted and cut by the Rams, he signed with the Chargers. Four years later, his wife, Debbie, was seven months pregnant with their first child. The team cut Miles on a Monday—literally the day before he was set to qualify for his NFL retirement on Tuesday. Miles cried out, "God, my wife is seven months pregnant, and I didn't get my retirement!" God told him, "Don't you think I know she's pregnant? I've got you." Five weeks later, he was put back on the team. But the next May, he came home after being cut again, only to find Debbie telling him she was pregnant with their second child. He had no job and no insurance, making only $125 a week on a local job while his mortgage payment was $1,500 a month.
Spiritual Metaphor: Natural math ($500 a month salary vs. $1,500 a month mortgage) did not add up inside the "beach ball." But God is not limited by natural math; He has infinite resources outside the beach ball and was completely faithful to provide for their family. This story demonstrates that even when our security is stripped away and our circumstances seem impossible, we can trust God's provision. He is never caught off guard by our crises and will always provide for His children when we walk in faith.
Full Story Text: Miles recounts a highly stressful period in his early career. After being drafted and cut by the Rams, he signed with the Chargers. Four years later, his wife, Debbie, was seven months pregnant with their first child. The team cut Miles on a Monday—literally the day before he was set to qualify for his NFL retirement on Tuesday. Miles cried out, "God, my wife is seven months pregnant, and I didn't get my retirement!" God told him, "Don't you think I know she's pregnant? I've got you." Five weeks later, he was put back on the team. But the next May, he came home after being cut again, only to find Debbie telling him she was pregnant with their second child. He had no job and no insurance, making only $125 a week on a local job while his mortgage payment was $1,500 a month.

Tags: Potential, God's perspective, Inner Heart, Faith
Story Summary: A professional photographer understands how to paint with light and maximize "light potential" in any setting. God looks at our hearts the same way, evaluating our willingness to trust him rather than our outward appearances.
Spiritual Metaphor: God operates like a master photographer: he looks past the surface and assesses our "light potential"—the capacity of our heart to reflect his glory.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles explains the difference between a good and a bad picture. Two photographers in the same place, at the same time, looking at the same subject can take completely different quality photographs. This is because photography is "painting with light." A professional photographer understands "light potential"—how to maximize whatever light is available, whether it is midday or setting sun. Similarly, God does not look at our outward traits (our money, car, height, or looks); he looks at our "light potential." He looks at our inner heart to see how much we are willing to trust him, commit to him, and step out in faith.

Tags: Perception, Senses, Faith, Spiritual Sensitivity
Story Summary: Elephants can hear subsonic sounds up to 20 miles away and feel vibrations through their feet. During a tsunami, they detected waves from 1,000 miles away and fled to safety, while humans remained oblivious. Elephants will lift a foot to hear better.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our natural senses are limited. We must have "subsonic" spiritual hearing—pressing into God to detect his guidance in ways our logical mind cannot perceive.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares details about how elephants perceive sound. While humans can only hear in a certain range, elephants can detect subsonic frequencies (infrasound) up to 20 miles away. They can feel vibrations in the ground through their feet. During the Indian Ocean tsunami, elephants felt the seismic activity 1,000 miles out and ran up into the safety of the mountains long before humans realized the danger. Elephants will even lift one foot to press more firmly into the dirt to improve their seismic "hearing."

Illustration Name: Building Campaign at East County (Don't Bet Against God)
Tags: Faith, Boldness, Miracles, God's Glory
Story Summary: When buying the East County building, real estate agents said Pastor Miles was crazy and over his head. Miles agreed he was, but told them "never bet against God." Years later, seeing a skeptic staring in awe, Miles stopped his car and yelled: "It's the bomb, ain't it? Don't ever bet against God!"
Spiritual Metaphor: God deliberately places us in situations where we are in over our heads, so that when the miracle occurs, he gets 100% of the glory instead of our human wisdom.
Full Story Text: When the Rock Church was buying their building, real estate agents and secular friends in East County told Pastor Miles that he was crazy, way over his head, and did not know what he was getting into. Miles responded by telling them they were right about him not knowing what he was doing, but they should "never bet against God." Years later, Miles saw one of those real estate friends looking at the building in amazement. Miles pulled over his car, got out, and yelled, "It's the bomb, ain't it? Go back and tell your boys: don't ever bet against God!"

Tags: Distractions, Petty Battles, Perspective, Kingdom Focus
Story Summary: Miles watched a documentary where two beetles fought fiercely over a tiny, three-square-foot patch of dirt. The battle was absurd because they were in a massive rainforest of millions of square miles. Miles relates this to humans fighting petty conflicts while ignoring God's kingdom.
Spiritual Metaphor: The enemy uses trivial arguments and material comparisons (three square feet of dirt) to distract us from fighting the real, spiritual battles of God's massive kingdom.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls watching a documentary on the Discovery Channel where two beetles fought intensely over a tiny, three-square-foot patch of dirt. They wrestled and battled until one finally lost and crawled away, never to return. Miles points out how absurd this battle was considering they were in the middle of a massive rainforest spanning millions of square miles. He compares this to humans fighting petty battles over shoes, cars, or social comparisons while missing out on God's massive kingdom purpose.

Tags: Courage, Boldness, Faith, Confronting Giants
Story Summary: Mike Tyson was an intimidating fighter who knocked everyone out. Evander Holyfield faced him with "Philippians 4:13" stitched on his shorts and ran straight at Tyson when the bell rang, completely throwing him off. Miles compares this to David running toward Goliath.
Spiritual Metaphor: We cannot be passive with our giants of fear, addiction, or depression. We must run directly at them, relying on the boldness and victory of Jesus Christ.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes the heavyweight boxing match between Mike Tyson and Evander Holyfield. Tyson was a terrifying, aggressive fighter who knocked opponents out in the first round with sheer, brutal strength. Most fighters were terrified, but Holyfield walked out smiling. He had "Philippians 4:13" ("I can do all things through Christ...") stitched onto his shorts. When the bell rang, instead of backing away in fear like everyone else, Holyfield ran straight at Tyson. Miles compares this to David running toward Goliath instead of shrinking back.

Tags: God's Sovereignty, Humour, Trust, Father's Power
Story Summary: A joke where Moses and Jesus hit golf balls into the water and use miracles to chip them out. An old man hits a terrible shot, but a fish eats it, an eagle grabs the fish, flies over the green, and drops the ball directly into the hole. Jesus says, "Good shot, Dad."
Spiritual Metaphor: We try to solve problems with clever human strategies (parting or walking on water), but God the Father has sovereign, unimaginable ways of directing our "stones" to achieve perfect victory.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles tells a humorous joke about Jesus and Moses playing golf. On a par three with water, Moses hits a ball into the water, parts the water with his nine iron, and chips it onto the green. Jesus hits his ball into the water, walks on the water, and chips his ball close to the hole. An old man with a cane then steps up, hits a terrible shot that goes toward the lake, but a fish catches it, an eagle grabs the fish, flies over the green, the fish drops the ball, and it falls straight into the cup for a hole-in-one. Jesus looks at Moses and says, "Good shot, Dad."

Tags: Overcoming Obstacles, Perseverance, Training
Story Summary: Olympic sprinter Gail Devers trained relentlessly to clear physical hurdles faster than any woman on earth. A low camera angle showed her running straight at the hurdles and effortlessly clearing them.
Spiritual Metaphor: We shouldn't see life's obstacles as reasons to panic. Like a trained hurdler, we should see them as opportunities to showcase our faith and training.
Full Story Text: Gail Devers is a world-class Olympic sprinter and hurdler from San Diego. Pastor Miles recalls seeing a camera angle placed at the exact height of the hurdles, looking down the track. When Gail warmed up, the perspective showed her running directly at these obstacles and effortlessly clearing them. She spent her entire life training to prove that she could overcome physical barriers faster than any other woman on earth.

Tags: Wife, Marriage, Communication, Weakness, Vulnerability
Story Summary: Pastor Miles' wife, Debbie, compromised on a car that wasn't exactly what she wanted. Miles asked if she got everything she wanted with him, and was met with complete "crickets." In the car, she pointed out his lack of patience and house-repair skills.
Spiritual Metaphor: Leadership in relationships requires vulnerability and a willingness to listen to honest feedback about our shortcomings rather than pretending we are perfect.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles' wife, Debbie, needed a new car. They went to the dealership, but they didn't have the exact car, color, or seat customization she had researched. Debbie decided to compromise on an alternative. When Miles asked if she would really be happy with it, she replied, "Well, you don't always get everything you want." Miles paused and jokingly asked, "What about me? Did you get everything you wanted with me?" He describes hearing absolute "crickets." Once they got in the car, she pointed out his lack of patience and his failure to fix things around the house.

Tags: Honesty, Restoration, Trust, Fixing Broken Things
Story Summary: Miles isn't handy, but praises his honest mechanic at Scripps Autocare who often tells them when repairs are unnecessary and masterfully restores broken cars.
Spiritual Metaphor: A sensitive leader must be like an expert mechanic: willing to walk into a room with highly damaged relationships, speak honest truth, and help put things back together.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares that he is not a handy "fix-it" man, but he is amazed by people who can repair complex machinery. He praises his car mechanic at Scripps Autocare, noting that what makes him truly outstanding is his honesty. The mechanic will frequently tell them when a repair is unnecessary and send them home, or he will masterfully disassemble a broken vehicle and put it back together completely restored.

Tags: Conviction, Grace, Integrity, Restitution
Story Summary: Bibles are the number one stolen item at the Rock Church bookstore. Miles preached a sermon offering amnesty with "no questions asked" if they paid for it. After the service, a person paid $40 to clear their conscience.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's grace convicts us not to condemn us, but to lead us to voluntary repentance and restoration. Even when we steal from him, his love is waiting to welcome us back.
Full Story Text: At the Rock Church, people frequently steal items from the bookstore, and ironically, the number one stolen item is the Bible. Pastor Miles jokes about how difficult it must be to read and pray over a stolen Bible. During a service the previous week, Miles made an appeal: he promised that if anyone who stole a Bible came back to return the money, they wouldn't ask any questions, call the police, or judge them. Immediately after that service, someone walked up to the counter, handed over their credit card, and said, "No questions about the Bible—take $40 off my card."

Illustration Name: Hurricane Gustav and Mayor Ray Nagin's Warning
Tags: Dependence on God, Life's Overwhelming Storms, Humility
Story Summary: Mayor Ray Nagin warned New Orleans residents to evacuate because they could not stop the water, telling them: "If you stay, you are on your own."
Spiritual Metaphor: Trying to face life's overwhelming storms on your own is destructive. Realizing our total dependence on God is key to finding shelter and surviving the storm.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls watching the news about Hurricane Gustav. Ray Nagin, the Mayor of New Orleans, issued a stark warning to the city's residents. He told everyone they must evacuate because they could not stop the incoming water, stating bluntly: "If you stay here, you are on your own. We cannot help you." Miles uses this to illustrate how some storms in life are too big for us to handle in our own strength.

Tags: Perseverance, Resilience, Spiritual Battles, Continuing the Fight
Story Summary: In amateur boxing, a knocked-down fighter gets a standing eight count. One boxer told the referee stopping the fight, "I think that's a good idea." Miles says Christians coming to the altar are saying they won't quit the fight.
Spiritual Metaphor: Getting knocked down is guaranteed in life, but with God's strength, you can stand up, shake off the blow, and refuse to quit the spiritual battle.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles' brother was a boxer who fought in the Golden Gloves. Miles shares a story about one of his brother's friends who got knocked down during a fight. In amateur boxing, when a fighter is knocked down, the referee gives them a "standing eight count" to assess their condition. If the fighter is too dazed, the referee will stop the fight. In this case, the referee finished counting to eight, looked at the dazed boxer, and said, "We're going to stop the fight." The boxer, completely exhausted and beaten, replied, "I think that's a good idea."

Tags: Obstacles, Spiritual Blockages, God's Power
Story Summary: As a child in NY, Miles would play in curb water, building miniature dams of sticks to block the stream, then releasing it.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's power is a rushing stream, but we block it with spiritual "sticks" (lack of prayer, Bible study, unrepentant sin). Removing the blocks allows his power to flow.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls a lonely childhood growing up in New York where he would play by himself in the water running down the street curb. He would place a stick in the water to watch it float, but eventually, he would get bored and build a miniature dam out of sticks and dirt. He would block the water, letting it pool up and telling the water, "You can't get by my dam!" When he finally moved the sticks out of the way, the water would suddenly rush down. Miles compares the water to the power of God, and the blocking sticks to things in our lives that restrict that power.

Tags: Disobedience, Blessings, Consequences, Materialism
Story Summary: Miles bought a house in Escondido despite clear warnings from God. The house had constant fires, leaks, El Nino flooding, and gophers. Before they moved, a giant rat stared at him in his bedroom.
Spiritual Metaphor: When we buy things or enter relationships outside of God's will, we won't enjoy them. God will not bless our disobedience; our material pursuits become sources of torment.
Full Story Text: About 14 years prior, Pastor Miles bought a house in Escondido. He admits that God clearly told him in multiple ways not to buy that house, but he was disobedient, thinking, "We can make it work." He lived there for 15 months and hated every moment. Because he was out of God's will, the house felt cursed: they had fires in the house, massive roof leaks, water flooding through the doors during El Niño, gophers, and an infestation of bold, fearless rats. The night they were preparing to leave, a massive rat came down the fireplace, stared directly at Miles in his bedroom, and refused to run away.

Tags: Respect, Adversity, Hardship, Endurance
Story Summary: A massive rat fought a trap in Miles' attic for three hours, clattering loudly before dying. Miles was so impressed by its fighting spirit that he gave it a small burial.
Spiritual Metaphor: Facing adversity requires a relentless, fighting spirit that refuses to go down easily under the enemy's traps.
Full Story Text: During his time in the Escondido house, Pastor Miles was constantly setting traps in the attic to catch the rats. He successfully caught many, but one night, a massive rat got caught in a trap directly above his bedroom. For three straight hours (180 minutes), Miles heard the trap clattering against the floor as the rat fiercely fought the trap, refusing to die easily. The next morning, Miles went into the attic and was so impressed by the rat's incredible strength and will to survive that he actually held a small "burial funeral" out of respect for its fight.

Tags: Apology, Forgiveness, Reconciliation, Humility, Obedience
Story Summary: Miles told an angry NFL player to apologize to teammates who talked behind his back, teaching him that spiritual maturity requires applying the Bible through radical humility.
Spiritual Metaphor: True maturity is taking responsibility for our own portion of a conflict, regardless of how wrong the other side was, choosing reconciliation over pride.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles counsels an NFL player who had recently switched teams. The player spent 15 minutes bitterly complaining about his former teammates who had talked behind his back. Miles challenged him to drop the grudge. A month later, the player called back, and Miles gave him "Part B" of the assignment: call his former teammates and apologize for his own part in the conflict. The player protested, "Man, I can't do that!" Miles insisted that if he wanted to grow as a man of God, reading the Bible was not enough—he had to apply it through radical humility.

Tags: Anger, Grudges, Heart Issues, Displaced Conflict
Story Summary: Cain's sacrifice was rejected by God, so he took his displaced anger out on his innocent brother Abel and murdered him.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our horizontal conflicts with others are often displaced vertical conflicts with God. When we are not right with God, we strike out at those around us.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles breaks down the biblical story of Cain and Abel. Cain offered an unacceptable crop sacrifice, which God rejected, while Abel offered an acceptable animal sacrifice, which God accepted. Cain became deeply angry with God. Instead of resolving his issue with God, Cain took his displaced anger and resentment out on his innocent brother Abel, murdering him in the field. Miles notes that "hurt people hurt people," and conflict with others is often a symptom of a broken relationship with God.

Illustration Name: The Breastfeeding Mother and the Milk Cows
Tags: Sacrifice, Obedience, Holy Spirit, Supernatural Direction
Story Summary: The Philistines tested the Ark's power by using breastfeeding milk cows to pull a cart away from their locked-up calves. Biologically, a mother cow would never walk away, yet they went straight down the road.
Spiritual Metaphor: Walking in the Spirit means going completely against our natural carnal instincts. Obedience often requires us to walk in directions that defy our flesh.
Full Story Text: When the Philistines returned the stolen Ark of the Covenant, they wanted to test if the curses they suffered were actually from God. They took two "milk cows" (breastfeeding mothers) who had never pulled a cart, hitched them to the cart carrying the Ark, and locked their calves away in a pen. Biologically and naturally, a breastfeeding mother cow would never willingly walk away from her crying calves to pull an unfamiliar cart. Yet, the cows walked straight down the road away from their calves, lowing as they went. Miles highlights this as a supernatural act of going against one's own natural instincts.

Illustration Name: Flashlight in the Upstate NY Mountains
Tags: Guidance, Faith, God's Word, Next Step
Story Summary: In pitch-black upstate NY, walking required a flashlight that only lit a small circle in front of Miles' feet. As he walked, the light moved to reveal the next step.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's Word is a lamp to our feet, not a spotlight on our entire life. He gives us just enough light for the immediate next step, requiring us to walk in faith.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls visiting his grandparents in upstate New York as a child. The mountainous area had absolutely no streetlights, rendering it pitch black at night. To walk outside, you had to use a flashlight or a handheld lamp. The light would not illuminate the entire street or the destination; it would only show a small circle of light directly in front of your feet. As you took a step, the light would move forward, revealing where to place your next step.

Tags: Nature, Creation, Peace, God's Glory
Story Summary: After visiting Pelican Bay Prison, a coordinator left Miles in the majestic Redwood Forest. He was overwhelmed by the towering trees and the glory of God's presence.
Spiritual Metaphor: God speaks through his creation, and the grand scale of the Redwoods serves as a megaphone of his care, reminding us that he can easily sustain us.
Full Story Text: After performing ministry at Pelican Bay State Prison (one of the most violent prisons in the country, where inmates were kept in small phone-booth-sized cages), Pastor Miles was driven to the airport by a local coordinator. The driver stopped in the middle of the massive Redwood Forest in Northern California and told Miles to get out of the car and walk into the trees. Miles, feeling slightly nervous as "a city brother in the middle of nowhere," walked in and was overwhelmed by the majestic silence, towering trees, and the literal glory of God's presence in creation.

Illustration Name: Interceptions, Punt Return, and Humble Pie
Tags: Humility, Pride, Marriage, Expectations, Wife
Story Summary: After college Miles had 4 interceptions and a 56-yard punt return. He proudly told Debbie, who paused and innocently asked, "Is that good?", giving him humble pie.
Spiritual Metaphor: We often try to impress God or others with our accolades. God serves us humble pie to remind us that he cares about our heart, not our accomplishments.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares a humorous story from college about his first date with his future wife, Debbie. He had just played a spectacular football game in Massachusetts, recording four interceptions and a 56-yard punt return—an incredibly rare feat. He called her exactly at 8:00 PM as promised. When she asked how the game went, Miles proudly detailed his outstanding performance, expecting her to be deeply impressed. Instead, she paused and innocently asked, "Is that good?" Miles describes this as his first major slice of "humble pie."

Tags: Expectations, Misunderstandings, Planning, Faith, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: Miles brought a change of clothes in a large bag to Debbie's apartment, causing her to panic thinking he was staying over. He packed because of active expectations.
Spiritual Metaphor: We must pray with active expectation. When we meet with God, we should have our Bibles open and a pen ready, expecting him to speak.
Full Story Text: Following that same college football game, Pastor Miles went over to Debbie's apartment for their date. He walked down the hallway carrying a large bag. Debbie saw him through the peephole and panicked, saying to herself, "What did I ever say to him? He's bringing an overnight bag!" In reality, Miles had packed a change of casual clothes because they were required to wear suits to the games, and he simply wanted to get comfortable. He notes that he packed because of an expectation of what would happen.

Illustration Name: Driving a Stationary Car (The Power of Movement)
Tags: Action, Obedience, Direction, Movement
Story Summary: A child trying to steer a stationary car will find the wheel locked, but once in motion, it can be steered with a finger. Many Christians wait for a complete plan before moving.
Spiritual Metaphor: God cannot steer a parked car. We must start moving in service and obedience, and then God will easily guide and redirect our path.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recalls being a small child sitting in his parent's parked car, grabbing the steering wheel, and trying to turn it. When the car was off and stationary, the wheel was incredibly stiff and almost impossible for a child to budge. But once the car is moving, even a child or a light touch of a finger can easily steer the car in any direction. Miles explains that many Christians remain completely stationary, demanding that God show them which way to turn before they start moving.

Tags: Peace, Simplicity, Finding Joy in Small Tasks
Story Summary: Rapper Snoop Dogg on "Fatherhood" happily vacuumed his messy house, saying "there's nothing more peaceful than vacuuming."
Spiritual Metaphor: Real peace isn't found in worldly achievements, but in slowing down, simplifying our lives, and finding joy in ordinary tasks.
Full Story Text: While watching the reality TV show "Fatherhood" starring rapper Snoop Dogg, Pastor Miles was amused to see Snoop Dogg come home to a messy house. Snoop walked into the closet, grabbed a vacuum cleaner, and happily began vacuuming the carpets. Snoop looked at the camera and said, "There's nothing more peaceful than vacuuming the floor." Miles notes the humor in seeing a famous, tough-persona hip-hop icon finding deep, child-like peace in a simple household chore.

Illustration Name: Standing in the Club to Say "No" to Cocaine
Tags: Temptation, Recommitment, Saying No, Spiritual Strength
Story Summary: After quitting cocaine, Miles stood next to a teammate at a club just to feel the spiritual high of saying "No" to drug offers and feeling God's approval.
Spiritual Metaphor: Saying no to temptation brings a deep sense of God's smiling approval that far exceeds any physical or worldly pleasure.
Full Story Text: In 1984, after five years of backsliding into severe sin, Pastor Miles radically recommitted his life to Jesus Christ and instantly quit his heavy cocaine addiction. Shortly after, he found himself at a popular Mission Valley club called "The Oz." An old teammate who didn't particularly like Miles kept offering him cocaine, saying, "Hey man, I want to get high." For the first time, Miles boldly said "No." He felt a profound, physical sense of God's approval and peace. He was so excited to experience this victory that he actually followed the teammate around the club just so he could keep saying "No" to the offers.

Sermon Title: The Ruth Romance - Part 1, Faith Chick Flick
Tags: Gospel, Distress, Hero, Proposal
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares how nosy he is with his family—his son and two daughters. One day, his wife Debbie and daughter tell him they are going to the movies, but refuse to let him join because they are going to see a "chick flick." Miles defines a "chick flick" as a movie where a girl is in serious distress or a major dilemma (lonely, broke, just broke up with her man, or lost her job) and a hero rides in on a horse to save her. The worse her dilemma and the larger the obstacles the hero must overcome to rescue her, the better the movie is.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is the ultimate metaphor for the Gospel. We are the "bride in distress." Before we accept Christ, we are sinners doomed to hell with no ability to save ourselves. Jesus is our ultimate Hero. He rode into human history, overcame the massive obstacle of the cross and death, paid for our sins, rose again, and offered us a proposal of relationship. When we accept His proposal, we are saved from our desperate situation.
Full Story Text:
The Story / Illustration: Pastor Miles shares how nosy he is with his family—his son (who is turning 19) and two daughters (ages 21 and 22). He always wants to know every conversation in his house. One day, his wife Debbie and daughter tell him they are going to the movies, but refuse to let him join. They explain that they are going to see a "chick flick" and he would not like it. Miles defines a "chick flick" as a movie where a girl is in serious distress or a major dilemma (lonely, broke, just broke up with her man, or lost her job) and a hero rides in on a horse to save her. The worse her dilemma and the larger the obstacles the hero must overcome to rescue her, the better and more satisfying the movie is.

The Spiritual Metaphor: This dynamic is the ultimate metaphor for the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We are the "bride in distress." Before we accept Christ, we are sinners doomed to hell with no ability to save ourselves or earn heaven. Jesus is our ultimate Hero. He rode into human history, overcame the massive obstacle of the cross and death, paid for our sins, rose again, and offered us a proposal of relationship. When we accept His proposal, we are saved from our desperate situation.

Sermon Title: The Ruth Romance - Part 1, Faith Chick Flick
Illustration Name: March of the Penguins Father Huddle
Tags: Devotion, Endurance, Covenant, Parenting
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes the extreme romantic devotion shown in the film March of the Penguins. After the mother penguin lays her egg, she must make a grueling, months-long walk back to the ocean to feed.
