Meanwhile, the father penguin must stand on the freezing Antarctic ice keeping the egg balanced on top of his feet, covered by his warm body fat. He stands huddled together with thousands of other fathers in pitch-black darkness, enduring 100 mph freezing winds and temperatures of 50 degrees below zero for months without eating a single bite of food. If the egg rolls off his feet onto the ice for even a second, the baby dies instantly.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is an incredible picture of the covenant and sacrificial commitment required in relationships. True love stands in the freezing storm, enduring severe hardship, starvation, and extreme elements to protect the next generation and fulfill a promise.
Full Story Text:
The Story / Illustration: Pastor Miles describes the beautiful, extreme romantic devotion shown in the film March of the Penguins. After the mother penguin lays her egg, she must make a grueling, months-long walk back to the ocean to feed. Meanwhile, the father penguin must stand on the freezing Antarctic ice keeping the egg balanced on top of his feet, covered by his warm body fat. He stands huddled together with thousands of other fathers in pitch-black darkness, enduring 100 mph freezing winds and temperatures of 50 degrees below zero for months without eating a single bite of food. If the egg rolls off his feet onto the ice for even a second, the baby dies instantly. The father risks starving to death on his eventual walk to the ocean once the mother returns to relieve him, displaying total commitment and endurance.

The Spiritual Metaphor: This is an incredible picture of the covenant and sacrificial commitment required in relationships. True love stands in the freezing storm, enduring severe hardship, starvation, and extreme elements to protect the next generation and fulfill a promise.

Sermon Title: The Ruth Romance - Part 1, Faith Chick Flick
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Friends, Separation, Boundaries
Story Summary: When Pastor Miles first got saved in 1984, he had a best friend named Danny with whom he spent almost all his time. Before they were married, Miles' wife Debbie jokingly referred to Danny as Miles' "wife" because they were inseparable. Miles humorously says that he and Danny "had a PhD in sin, and Danny was going for another degree while Miles was trying to drop out of school." Once Miles gave his life to Christ, he realized he had to get a spiritual "divorce" from Danny and separate himself from their old lifestyle. Later, when Miles and Debbie had their first child, Danny wanted to hold the baby, but Miles had to step in and say no because of Danny's scandalous lifestyle and negative influence.
Spiritual Metaphor: Giving our lives to God and pursuing His plan often means being willing to separate ourselves from close relationships and old friends who lead us back into sin. Our relationship with Jesus (our Groom) must become more important than any human relationship, even if it requires difficult boundaries or separation.
Full Story Text:
The Story / Illustration: When Pastor Miles first got saved in 1984, he had a best friend named Danny with whom he spent almost all his time. Before they were married, Miles' wife Debbie jokingly referred to Danny as Miles' "wife" because they were inseparable. Miles humorously says that he and Danny "had a PhD in sin, and Danny was going for another degree while Miles was trying to drop out of school." Once Miles gave his life to Christ, he realized he had to get a spiritual "divorce" from Danny and separate himself from their old lifestyle. Later, when Miles and Debbie had their first child (their daughter who is now 22), Danny wanted to hold the baby, but Miles had to step in and say no because of Danny's scandalous lifestyle and negative influence.

The Spiritual Metaphor: Giving our lives to God and pursuing His plan often means being willing to separate ourselves from close relationships and old friends who lead us back into sin. Our relationship with Jesus (our Groom) must become more important than any human relationship, even if it requires difficult boundaries or separation.

Sermon Title: The Ruth Romance - Part 2, Romanceable Ruth
Tags: Fiero, Freeway, Dog, Big Picture, Trust
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes driving up the 15 freeway toward Pasadena, just past Escondido. From the top of a hill, hundreds of yards away, he spotted a dog running across the highway from the other side. The dog successfully crossed the median and ran onto Miles' side of the freeway. Right in front of the dog was a young girl driving a low Pontiac Fiero. Because she was only looking at the road directly in front of her car, she didn't see the dog coming until it ran right into her path. She slammed on her brakes, tires smoking, and ran over and killed the dog. She pulled over, red-faced and crying, completely devastated by the accident. Miles didn't hit the dog because, from his vantage point on top of the hill, he saw the big picture and watched the dog's path from the very beginning.
Spiritual Metaphor: In our relationships and life decisions, we often get in trouble because we focus only on what is immediately in front of us—our current feelings, desires, and immediate circumstances. We make frantic, short-sighted choices that lead to emotional crash-ups. God, however, sits at the top of the hill and sees the entire big picture of our lives. We must trust His bird's-eye perspective and follow His rules instead of relying on our narrow, localized vision.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes driving up the 15 freeway toward Pasadena, just past Escondido. From his vantage point at the top of a hill, hundreds of yards away, he spotted a dog running across the highway from the opposite side. The dog successfully crossed the median and ran onto Miles' side of the freeway. Directly in the dog's path was a young girl driving a low Pontiac Fiero. Because she was only looking at the road directly in front of her car, she didn't see the dog coming until it ran right into her lane. She slammed on her brakes, tires smoking, and ultimately ran over and killed the dog. She pulled over, red-faced and crying, completely devastated by the accident. Miles avoided the danger because, from his position on top of the hill, he saw the big picture and watched the dog's path from the very beginning.

Sermon Title: The Ruth Romance - Part 2, Romanceable Ruth
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Cotton Mouth, Submission, Service
Story Summary: Pastor Miles jokingly shares how he can wake up in the middle of the night at 3:00 AM with dry throat and "cotton mouth," wake up his wife Debbie, and ask her to go downstairs to the kitchen to fetch him a glass of water in his favorite pink cup. He mimics Debbie waking up dazed, walking downstairs dragging her feet, and bringing the water back up to him, sometimes backsliding if she's grumpy. He points out that Debbie would only do this because she knows Miles would do the exact same thing for her in a heartbeat without hesitation.
Spiritual Metaphor: True biblical headship and leadership in a marriage are not about demanding authority or forcing a spouse to "bow down." It is about mutual, self-sacrificing service. To get your spouse to respect and serve you, you must be the first to bow down, serve her, anticipate her fears, and protect her. Mutual submission and love are the keys to a flourishing, Christ-centered marriage.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles jokingly shares a scenario where he wakes up in the middle of the night at 3:00 AM with a dry throat and "cotton mouth." He describes waking up his wife, Debbie, and asking her to go downstairs to the kitchen to fetch him a glass of water in his favorite pink cup. He mimics Debbie waking up dazed, walking downstairs dragging her feet, and bringing the water back up to him, noting that she might occasionally be grumpy or "backsliding" in the process. He points out that Debbie would only do this because she knows Miles would do the exact same thing for her in a heartbeat without hesitation.

Sermon Title: The Ruth Romance - Part 2, Romanceable Ruth
Illustration Name: The Grumpy Wife's Three-Year Baby Grace Period
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Baby, Grace, Household Chores
Story Summary: Pastor Miles gives practical, humorous advice to young husbands whose wives have just had a baby. He explains that there is an intense three-year "grace period" after a baby is born during which the house will be a mess, the wife's hair will be uncombed, and she might be grumpy or "evil" every night. He emphasizes that motherhood is the hardest job on the planet. Instead of complaining or demanding that food be ready, a Christ-like husband must step in to vacuum, do the laundry (sorting whites in hot water and colors in cold, not washing them together!), wash the dishes, take care of the kids, and pray for her.
Spiritual Metaphor: Christ-like love means actively stepping in to serve and support our partners during seasons of intense pressure and exhaustion, without expecting anything in return. We are called to be the chief servants in our homes, reflecting how Christ served and laid His life down for the church.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles offers practical, humorous advice to young husbands whose wives have just had a baby. He explains that there is an intense three-year "grace period" after a baby is born. During this time, the house may be a mess, the wife's hair may go uncombed, and she might be grumpy or "evil" every night due to exhaustion. He emphasizes that motherhood is the hardest job on the planet. Rather than complaining or demanding that food be ready, Miles explains that a Christ-like husband must step in to perform household tasks.

Vacuuming the floors.

Doing the laundry (specifically sorting whites in hot water and colors in cold, rather than washing them all together).

Washing the dishes.

Taking care of the children.

Praying for his wife.

Sermon Title: Image is Everything, Part 1, The Image Key
Tags: Declaration, Bush, Iraq War, Spoken Word
Story Summary: Pastor Miles highlights how on March 20, 2003, President George W. Bush made the formal decision and spoken declaration to go to war with Iraq. Just by speaking those words, global events were instantly set in motion: the United States began spending $164 million a day ($60 billion total), approximately 3,000 American soldiers died, countless military families were affected, and roughly 75,000 Iraqis lost their lives. The words of one human president acted like a powerful key that opened a door to massive, world-altering action and consequence.
Spiritual Metaphor: If the spoken word of a mere human leader carries such immense, country-shaping power, how much more powerful is the voice and Word of God? When God speaks, His words carry creative, redemptive, and transformative power. When His words are spoken into our lives, they act as the ultimate "key" that ignites our true individual identity, shapes our morality, and sets our calling in motion.
Full Story Text:

When God speaks, His words carry absolute creative, redemptive, and transformative power. When His words are spoken into our lives, they act as the ultimate "key" that ignites our true individual identity, shapes our morality, and sets our eternal calling in motion.

Sermon Title: Image is Everything, Part 1, The Image Key
Illustration Name: The Halloween Mask at the Window
Tags: Halloween, Mask, Prank, Hide-and-Seek, Comfort Zone
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares a terrifying but humorous family tradition passed down from his own father. When Miles was a kid, his father would play scary pranks, like laying under his bed or standing outside his window in the dark wearing a creepy Halloween monster mask with a lit candle underneath to scare him. Miles continued this tradition with his own children, playing hide-and-seek in a pitch-black house. His kids would walk through the dark hallways nervously calling out, "Dad? Dad?" until Miles would jump out, scaring them so badly they cried.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is a picture of how God calls us out of our comfortable, well-lit comfort zones. To grow in faith, God often tells us to leave behind the familiar and step into the unfamiliar, pitch-black areas of life where we must rely completely on His voice. We must trust Him as we walk in the dark, calling out "Dad" (our Heavenly Father), knowing He is there to guide and grow us through the unfamiliar.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - The Promise by Miles McPherson
Illustration Name: Chris Henry and the Cincinnati Bengals
Tags: Divine Appointment, NFL, Bengals, Chris Henry, Legacy
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares a story about NFL player Chris Henry of the Cincinnati Bengals, who passed away in late 2009. While the public and critics knew Chris Henry through his troubled record—being arrested five times in one year and suspended—the team had a completely different perspective. Running back Cedric Benson pointed out that Chris sat next to him for years and never said a single negative thing about another player. Another teammate shared how Chris lit up showing him New Orleans and discussing Hurricane Katrina. A coach shared how Chris sat in the pouring rain in the bleachers to watch his son's high school football game. On the day he died, the coach's daughter was actually in a Christian bookstore getting Chris's name engraved on a Bible.
Spiritual Metaphor: We frequently judge people by their failures and worst chapters. But God's grace continually reaches out to people, even to the last second of their lives, because of His ultimate Promise. Chris was walking in a new direction and turning his life around when his life was cut short. God keeps His promise to seek out the lost, love us despite our "junk," and give everyone an opportunity to be restored.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles shares a touchstone story about NFL player Chris Henry of the Cincinnati Bengals, who passed away in late 2009. Miles was scheduled to do chapel for the Bengals, which he had done several times before when they were in town. When he heard of Chris Henry's accidental death, he recognized a Divine Appointment. While the public and critics knew Chris Henry through his troubled record—being arrested five times in one year, suspended, and labeled by a judge as a "one-man wrecking crew"—the team had a completely different perspective.

Running back Cedric Benson pointed out that Chris sat next to him for years and never said a single negative thing about another player. Another teammate shared how Chris lit up showing him New Orleans and discussing Hurricane Katrina. A coach shared how Chris sat in the pouring rain in the bleachers to watch his son's high school football game. On the day he died, the coach's daughter was actually in a Christian bookstore getting Chris's name engraved on a Bible.

Sermon Title: Rock Church - The Promise by Miles McPherson
Tags: Barren Women, Old Testament, Mary, Virgin Birth, Miracles, Sarah
Story Summary: The Old Testament builds a perfect foundation for the New Testament, especially concerning the virgin birth. Throughout the Old Testament, God repeatedly selected barren women who could not biologically have children and made a promise to give them a child. Abraham's wife Sarah was a 90-year-old woman walking with a cane when God promised her a baby. She laughed in disbelief, but she eventually gave birth to Isaac. Isaac's wife Rebecca was barren, as was Jacob's wife Rachel. Samson's mother and Samuel's mother (Hannah) were also barren. Each had a supernatural birth ordained by God, establishing that the children belonged to God's divine purpose. Elizabeth, John the Baptist's mother, was also barren and had a child in her old age.
Spiritual Metaphor: All of these miraculous Old Testament births prepared the world for the ultimate supernatural birth: Mary's virgin birth. Mary was a young virgin who had never been with a man, and her pregnancy was biologically impossible. Yet, the Holy Spirit overshadowed her, establishing that with God, absolutely nothing is impossible. Just as God has been faithful to perform the impossible through barren women throughout history, we can trust Him to do the impossible in our lives.

Sermon Title: Lord of the Things - Part 1, Getting in the Fast Lane
Tags: Nursery Rhyme, Humpty Dumpty, Fall, False Comforts, Restoration
Story Summary: Pastor Miles recalls the nursery rhyme of Humpty Dumpty who sat on a wall and had a great fall. When he broke, "all the king's horses and all the king's men" could not put the egg back together again. Miles points out that Humpty did not need the king's servants or resources; he needed the King Himself.
Spiritual Metaphor: When our lives fracture and fall apart, we desperately reach out to "the king's men" (therapists, self-help books, career advice, support groups) to put ourselves back together. These resources are helpful but ultimately inadequate. Real restoration is only possible when we bypass intermediate sources and go directly to the King of Kings, establishing His personal lordship over our broken lives.

Sermon Title: Lord of the Things - Part 1, Getting in the Fast Lane
Tags: Physiology, Health, Harvard, Blood, Temple, Holy Spirit
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares findings from a wellness conference at Harvard University. The secular presenters defined "wellness" as the body's optimal ability to deliver blood to all its tissues and parts. Miles notes that the Bible has said for thousands of years that "life is in the blood." The reason we exercise, stretch, and eat nutritious foods is to increase the efficiency with which our heart pumps and transports oxygen-rich blood to all parts of our body.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our physical body is not our own; it is the holy temple of the living God. Stewardship means keeping the temple physically and mentally clean so that we are optimal "vessels" for His service. If our tissues are sluggish and clogged due to toxic food and a sedentary lifestyle, we are hindering the physical instrument God wants to use for His kingdom.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares findings from a wellness conference at Harvard University (pronounced "Harvard" with a New England accent). Miles notes that the Bible has said for thousands of years that "life is in the blood." The reason we exercise, stretch, and eat nutritious foods is to increase the efficiency with which our heart pumps and transports oxygen-rich blood to all parts of our body.

Sermon Title: Lord of the Things - Part 2, Lordship Comedy
Tags: Rancho Peñasquitos, Pregnancy, Selective Compliance, Hypocrisy
Story Summary: In his early years of youth ministry in Rancho Peñasquitos, Pastor Miles had a multi-ethnic Bible study at his house. One of the high school girls stopped attending for several weeks. When Miles called her to check in and find out what was wrong, she sadly told him, "God can't even help me with my problem." When Miles asked, "Are you pregnant?" she answered, "Kinda." Miles calls this "biological comedy" because it is physically impossible to be kinda pregnant—you either are or you aren't. He connects this to "lordship comedy," where Christians say, "Jesus is my Lord, and I kinda obey."
Spiritual Metaphor: Lordship is an all-or-nothing reality, just like pregnancy. You cannot selectively choose which commandments to obey and which to ignore while calling Jesus your "Lord." Half-hearted, selective obedience is actually complete disobedience. True lordship requires complete, absolute submission of every area of our lives to His authority.

Sermon Title: Lord of the Things - Part 2, Lordship Comedy
Tags: Beach, Sand, Foundation, False Security, Storms
Story Summary: While walking on the beach on a Saturday, Pastor Miles spotted a small bird standing on one leg in the wet sand. Miles sneaked up directly behind him to see how close he could get without scaring him. As the ocean waves swept back and forth over the shore, the bird remained perfectly still. However, Miles noticed that the longer the bird stood there as the water rushed over, the deeper his foot sank into the wet, shifting sand.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is a picture of building our lives on "sand" (worldly comfort, money, status, relationships). It may seem secure on a sunny day, but as life's waves sweep over us, we sink deeper and deeper into instability. If our foundation is not firmly set on the "Rock" of Jesus Christ through active obedience, our life's house will inevitably crash when the massive storms of life hit.

Sermon Title: Lord of the Things - Part 3, Lordship Lets Go
Tags: Gibbons Monkey, Trap, Coconut, Candy, Surrender, Money, Pride
Story Summary: In Africa, hunters catch the small, highly active Gibbons monkey using a hollowed-out metal coconut chained to a tree. They put rice and candy inside the coconut. The monkey smells the sweet food, reaches his hand inside the small hole, and grabs a huge handful. However, because his fist is now clenched, he cannot pull his hand back out of the narrow hole. Despite screaming, grunting, and pulling all day as the hunter calmly approaches with a gun, the monkey refuses to let go of the food. All the monkey had to do to be completely free was open his hand and let go of the candy.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is the ultimate picture of how we clench our hands around our "possessions" (money, bad habits, toxic relationships, pride). We are trapped and spiritually defeated, screaming in pain as the enemy closes in on us, entirely because we refuse to let go of the "candy" in our hands. Lordship means opening our hands, surrendering everything we cling to, and letting God have absolute control.

Sermon Title: Lord of the Things - Part 3, Lordship Lets Go
Tags: Rocky, Coma, Adrian, Rematch, Training, Perseverance, Victory
Story Summary: Pastor Miles jokingly shares how he was crying while watching Rocky II on television (which he attributes to the emotional effects of fasting). In the film, Rocky's wife, Adrian, is in a coma after giving birth. Rocky sits faithfully by her bedside, refusing to train for his heavyweight rematch with Apollo Creed because of her condition and opposition. When she finally wakes up, she weakly calls Rocky over and tells him one powerful word: "Win!" Trainer Mick immediately yells, "What are we waiting for?" and they start training aggressively.
Spiritual Metaphor: Many Christians complain that living a holy life and being completely obedient to God's standard is "too hard" or impossible. But God has complete confidence in us, and He has given us His Holy Spirit to empower us. When we feel exhausted and ready to quit, God looks at us and says, "Win!" He provides the strength, grace, and authority to overcome every spiritual battle.

Sermon Title: Leave it to Cleave Her, Part 1
Tags: Relationships, Driving, Danger
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes driving to Azusa Pacific University when he saw a dog running across the 15 freeway. He anticipated its path, but a driver in a Fiero only saw what was in front of her, slammed on her brakes, and tragically hit and killed the dog, leaving her completely traumatized.
Spiritual Metaphor: The driver crashed because she was reacting only to what was immediately in front of her instead of scanning the wider environment. Similarly, in relationships, reacting only to immediate desires without looking at the bigger picture of God's design leads to relational wreckage.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts a harrowing experience while driving to Azusa Pacific University (APU) to speak at a chapel service. He was driving his Pontiac Fiero—a car known for sitting very low to the ground—northbound on the 15 Freeway. While traveling at freeway speeds, a large dog suddenly darted across the lanes. Because the Fiero was so low, there was no way to avoid the impact or for the animal to pass under the chassis. The resulting collision was violent and instantaneous, causing significant damage to the vehicle and demonstrating how quickly a journey can be interrupted by an unexpected obstacle on the path.

Sermon Title: Leave it to Cleave Her, Part 1
Tags: Purity, Culture, Law
Story Summary: During a sexual purity seminar in Brooklyn, Michigan, Miles asked students if sex had any rules. They claimed it did not, but agreed that having sex with a child, another man's wife, or a same-sex partner was wrong. When asked why, they had no explanation other than "it just is."
Spiritual Metaphor: Relational and sexual boundaries are not arbitrary social conventions; they are absolute rules established by God, who designed human nature and marriage. We must choose whether to align our lives with God's word or the shifting standards of the world.
Full Story Text:
The freeway represents the path of life and the "speed" at which we pursue our goals and relationships.

The collision illustrates the sudden, jarring consequences of hitting a boundary we weren't prepared for. Just as the low-profile car was ill-equipped for the impact, our lives can be wrecked when we ignore the "rules of the road" God has established. Staying within the designated "lanes" of God's commands is the only way to ensure a safe arrival at our destination.
