Sermon Title: Leave it to Cleave Her, Part 1
Tags: Temptation, Children
Story Summary: While shopping in a grocery store, Miles noticed his young son had been quiet for ten seconds. He looked down and saw his son pointing his head and poking at a Cosmopolitan magazine cover model, saying, "Look at that, wow!" Miles jokingly thought "that's my boy" but corrected him.
Spiritual Metaphor: Physical attraction to the opposite sex is a natural, God-given drive. However, because our culture weaponizes sex to sell products and promote lust, we must actively guard our eyes and hearts by aligning with God's pure boundaries.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes the common experience of walking through an airport or standing in a grocery store checkout line and being confronted by the covers of Cosmopolitan magazine. He notes how these magazines often promote a version of "intimacy" and "cleaving" that is entirely physical, transactional, and devoid of the spiritual covenant God intended. He calls for a "check" on what we allow our eyes to see and our minds to process.

Sermon Title: Leave it to Cleave Her, Part 1
Tags: Desire, Children
Story Summary: Miles' young daughter was holding a Ken doll with a Jheri curl and six-pack abs, saying, "Daddy, this is my man." Feeling protective as her provider, Miles waited until she fell asleep, went into her toy box, stripped Ken's clothes, and disassembled his head, arms, and legs. The next morning she cried, "Daddy, Ken is dead!"
Spiritual Metaphor: The desire for companionship and romantic love is normal and healthy. However, we must be careful to choose partners who are spiritually and emotionally whole, rather than settling for hollow, superficial "torsos" that are falling apart.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares a story about his daughter’s Ken doll, which notably featured a Jheri curl hairstyle. He observes how children often play with dolls by pulling them apart—popping off the arms, legs, or head. Once a doll is disassembled, it loses its form and its intended purpose; it becomes a collection of plastic parts rather than a representation of a person.

Sermon Title: Leave it to Cleave Her, Part 1
Tags: Design, Control
Story Summary: At a dinner with singles, Miles met a man claiming to be a brain surgeon. The surgeon proved his profession by explaining how the brain stem automatically controls breathing, heartbeats, and blinking. He noted that if humans had manual control over these vital functions, we would easily kill ourselves out of distraction.
Spiritual Metaphor: God placed our most critical bodily functions on "autopilot" to protect us. However, He gave us full manual control over our relationships, sex life, and moral choices. Since these areas are not on autopilot, they require absolute obedience to God's instructions, or we will cause devastating damage.
Full Story Text: During a dinner with a brain surgeon, Pastor Miles learned about the function of the brain stem and the concept of "autopilot" in human physiology. The surgeon explained that while the higher brain handles conscious thought, the brain stem manages involuntary actions—things we do without thinking, like breathing or reacting to pain.

Sermon Title: Leave it to Cleave Her, Part 2
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Protection, Discernment
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes his quiet wife Debbie as having incredibly accurate spiritual radar. She can observe someone quietly with a "shifty eye" and tell Miles, "I don't trust her." Miles calls her his "pit bull" who sniffs out danger and protects him during ministry engagements.
Spiritual Metaphor: God designed the wife from the man's rib to walk by his side and protect his heart. A wife's spiritual discernment is a vital protective shield for the marriage and family; husbands must value and trust their wives' discernment to guard their hearts.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles describes a common dynamic in many marriages where a husband brings home a new friend or business associate, thinking the person is "the greatest guy in the world." The husband is focused on shared interests, a firm handshake, or a good conversation. Meanwhile, his wife is sitting quietly, observing the guest with what Miles calls a "shifty eye" or "pit bull radar."

The wife doesn't say much during the interaction, but as soon as the guest leaves, she turns to her husband and warns him that there is something "off" about the man. While the husband was oblivious to any red flags, the wife's internal radar has identified a "pit bull"—someone who might be untrustworthy or dangerous to the family's peace or finances. She senses a character flaw or a hidden motive that the husband completely missed because he was looking only at the surface.

Sermon Title: Leave it to Cleave Her, Part 2
Tags: Culture, Idolatry
Story Summary: Pastor Miles critiques the famous line from the movie Jerry Maguire, "You complete me," calling it a Hollywood fantasy. He explains that when two incomplete, broken people get married expecting the other to complete them, they only end up with a messy, unstable relationship.
Spiritual Metaphor: Marital union brings oneness, but true wholeness and spiritual completeness only come from a union with Jesus Christ. No human being can fill the void in your soul; placing that expectation on a spouse leads to disappointment and idolatry.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles references the famous scene from the movie Jerry Maguire, where the main character walks into a room and delivers the iconic line to his love interest: "You complete me." While this line is often celebrated as the height of romantic sentiment, Miles challenges the audience to look at it through a biblical lens.

He points out that in the movie, Jerry Maguire is a broken, desperate man who is looking for a woman to fill the empty holes in his soul. The world teaches that we are all "half-people" wandering around looking for our "other half" to make us whole. Miles argues that this Hollywood standard creates a fantasy that places an impossible burden on a spouse to provide a sense of identity and fulfillment that they were never meant to provide.

Tags: Prison, Future, Forgiveness
Story Summary: While visiting Calipatria State Prison, a knife fight broke out. Loud music blocked the sound of sirens, and the guards forced all inmates onto the ground (or be targets for snipers). Tear gas and rubber bullets were deployed. Later, in the Protective Custody yard with sex offenders, God told Miles to stay silent and look at them for two minutes. God showed him that He is not sadness-focused on their past, but excited about their potential from this day forward.
Spiritual Metaphor: God doesn't look at us with grief over our past sins or failures, but with excitement for what we can become from this day forward when we surrender our lives to Him.
Full Story Text:
This sermon, preached by Pastor Miles McPherson on April 4, 2004, examines the Triumphal Entry of Jesus into Jerusalem through the lens of Mark 11:1-11. The message focuses on the donkey—an animal chosen by God for a specific purpose—and how we, like that donkey, are called to be "untied" from our past to carry the presence of Christ.

1. The Calipatria State Prison Riot and PC Yard

Story / Illustration:Pastor Miles recounts a visit to Calipatria State Prison, a maximum-security facility. During a time of high tension, a riot broke out, highlighting the stark divisions within the prison walls. He specifically mentions the "PC Yard" or Protective Custody yard. This area is reserved for inmates who are targets of violence from the general population, often including sex offenders. In the hierarchy of prison culture, these individuals are the most despised, isolated even from the community of criminals.

Spiritual Metaphor:The donkey in the scripture was "tied up" at a place where two roads met, representing a life of restriction and labels. Just as the men in the PC yard are defined and bound by their worst mistakes and the labels society gives them, we are often tied up by our sins. The metaphor emphasizes that Jesus deliberately sends for the one who is "tied up"—the one society has cast aside—to be loosed for His divine service.

Tags: Service, Palm Sunday, Christ
Story Summary: On Palm Sunday, as Jesus entered Jerusalem, He rode on a young donkey. The donkey walked on fine robes and palm branches while crowds cheered and shouted "Hosanna!" The donkey got to experience all this incredible glory, but it was only because he was carrying Jesus on his back.
Spiritual Metaphor: We are like the donkey. We can never be God, but if we carry Jesus and let Him lead us, we get to experience the miraculous and amazing things He does. People might even give us credit, but we must always remember that we are just the donkey carrying the King.
Full Story Text: This sermon, preached by Pastor Miles McPherson on April 4, 2004, examines the Triumphal Entry of Jesus into Jerusalem through the lens of Mark 11:1-11. The message focuses on the donkey—an animal chosen by God for a specific purpose—and how we, like that donkey, are called to be "untied" from our past to carry the presence of Christ.

Illustration Name: Admitting You are Wrong in Marriage
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Pride, Communication
Story Summary: Pastor Miles asks the men in the congregation if they have ever had an argument with their wife or girlfriend, realized they were completely wrong, and found it nearly impossible to admit it. They usually blame the wife instead of taking responsibility.
Spiritual Metaphor: Christ sets us free from our pride and the bondage of our egos. True freedom in Christ means having the humility to say, "I was wrong," to repent, and to offer or receive forgiveness.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts a visit to Calipatria State Prison, a maximum-security facility. During a time of high tension, a riot broke out, highlighting the stark divisions within the prison walls. He specifically mentions the "PC Yard" or Protective Custody yard. This area is reserved for inmates who are targets of violence from the general population, often including sex offenders. In the hierarchy of prison culture, these individuals are the most despised, isolated even from the community of criminals.

Tags: Daughter, Newborn, Parents, Wife, Marriage
Story Summary: When his first daughter was born, her head was soft and had a distinct point on the side (like Gumby) from passing through the birth canal. Twelve hours after she was born, she was wrapped in a blanket in her incubator with only her pointy head sticking out. Miles stood there, completely captivated and busy watching her, because she belonged to him.
Spiritual Metaphor: We belong to God, and He watches over us with the same intense, loving pride of a father looking at his newborn child. He is excited about our specific purpose, even with our soft-headed "points" and imperfections.
Full Story Text: Miles recalls the birth of his daughter and the initial shock of seeing her head shape. Because of the pressure of the birth canal, her head appeared elongated and "pointy," resembling the character Gumby. As a new father, he was concerned, but the doctor assured him that over time, the head would round out and take its proper shape.

Illustration Name: Hepatitis C Ink and Wet Rag
Tags: Prison, Sickness, Pride
Story Summary: While ministering in the prison, Miles learned about the high rates of Hepatitis C among inmates due to sharing needles or contaminated ink. He also heard how inmates dip knives in feces to poison their targets. After hearing these details, Miles went into the yard to shake hands with inmates. One inmate came up holding a wet rag and asked Miles to sign it. When Miles signed it, his hand got wet, and he panicked, wondering what biological hazard was on that wet rag.
Spiritual Metaphor: The Bible says our self-righteousness is like filthy rags (Isaiah 64:6) - dirty, contaminated, and potentially poisonous. We cannot present our own good works to God as righteousness; we must be clothed in the perfect righteousness that Christ provides.
Full Story Text: Miles recalls the birth of his daughter and the initial shock of seeing her head shape. Because of the pressure of the birth canal, her head appeared elongated and "pointy," resembling the character Gumby. As a new father, he was concerned, but the doctor assured him that over time, the head would round out and take its proper shape.

Tags: Foundation, Youth, Childhood
Story Summary: Miles recalls watching actor Jackie Gleason perform a monologue about how little boys love to throw rocks in water, at cars, and at people. Miles was no different as a child. But now that he is older, he loves "big rocks" because they are solid.
Spiritual Metaphor: In a world of shifting sand and muddy pits, God takes our feet out of the "miry clay" and places them on a Rock-solid foundation (Psalm 40:2). Our steps are established when we are anchored to Christ, the Rock of our salvation.
Full Story Text: In the prison system, inmates often mark themselves with "ink" or tattoos that signify their gang affiliations or their past identities. Miles describes how some prisoners, in an attempt to change their lives or hide their past, would use a wet rag and harsh methods to try and scrub away or "wipe" a signature or mark. This process was often dangerous, involving shared needles and contaminated ink that led to the spread of Hepatitis C.

Tags: Preschool, Youth, Humor
Story Summary: Miles recalls visiting the preschool nursery last week, where he attempted to take a nap with the children. He lay in a tiny preschool bed with his feet hanging off one end, his head hanging off the other, wrapped in a Spider-Man blanket. The kids were laughing loudly at the sight.
Spiritual Metaphor: Developing meaningful relationships across generations—both with the elderly and with children—is essential for a rich life. It reminds us to constantly evaluate if our actions and the places we go please God.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes a peaceful scene in the church nursery during a service. He observes a small child taking a nap, completely tucked in and secure under a Spider-Man blanket. The child is oblivious to the chaos or the world outside, totally "tied" to the comfort and protection of that blanket.

Sermon Title: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Tags: Sickness, Balance, Hearing
Story Summary: Pastor Miles explains that he is experiencing ear, nose, and throat (ENT) issues, causing him to feel dizzy in the mornings. He consulted a doctor who explained that the inner ear contains fluid that maintains balance. There are tiny hairs in the ear called stereocilia that touch this fluid and send messages to the brain about the head's angle. If an infection occurs, the fluid gets thick or moves slowly, causing the ear to send delayed or incorrect messages to the brain. This creates a conflict: the eyes see that the head is upright, but the inner ear says it's tilted, causing the brain to spin in confusion.
Spiritual Metaphor: Every day, our spirit tries to line up God's will with our own. When our spiritual receptors (our "stereocilia") are clogged by sin, distraction, or a bad attitude, we receive a distorted signal. We must align ourselves with God's truth to regain our spiritual balance, especially when coming to church with a bad attitude.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles describes a visit to an Ear, Nose, and Throat (ENT) specialist to discuss the mechanics of human balance. He explains that the inner ear contains a specific fluid and microscopic "hairs" known as stereocilia. When a person moves, the fluid shifts, bending these stereocilia, which then send signals to the brain to indicate orientation. Dizziness or vertigo occurs when the fluid continues to spin or the hairs remain bent even when the body is stationary. This physical disorientation creates a sensation that the world is spinning, regardless of the objective reality of the ground beneath one's feet.

Sermon Title: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Tags: Ark, Stats, Flood
Story Summary: Miles shares details about the scale of Noah's Ark. It was 450 feet long, 75 feet wide, and 45 feet high (three stories)—strikingly similar to the building Rock Church was preparing to purchase (444 feet long, 180 feet wide, and 45 feet high). He explains that the Ark wasn't meant for navigation, only to float. Noah didn't need full-grown elephants or every single breed of dog. He only brought the ancestral "kinds" (genus level, e.g., two ancestral dogs to reproduce all modern dogs). Statistically, the Ark had the volume of 522 train boxcars, while 35,000 sheep-sized animals would easily fit into just 146 boxcars.
Spiritual Metaphor: God's instructions and provisions are incredibly practical and mathematically precise, even when they seem impossible or "crazy" to the world. When God calls us to a task, His provision is more than sufficient to preserve us and carry us through the storm.
Full Story Text: The sermon highlights the specific dimensions God gave Noah for the Ark: 300 cubits long, 50 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high. Pastor Miles notes that these proportions (a 30:5:3 ratio) are the exact standards used by modern naval architects to ensure maximum stability in rough seas. Furthermore, the total volume of the Ark was equivalent to approximately 522 standard railroad boxcars. This immense capacity was more than sufficient to house the necessary animal kinds and provisions, debunking the idea that the Ark was a cramped, whimsical boat.

Sermon Title: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Tags: Science, Fossils, Flood
Story Summary: To defend the historical reality of the global flood, Miles cites the fossil record. He notes that fossils can only form if an animal is buried rapidly in an airtight environment (like mud) before decaying. He highlights the Miocene shale beds in California, where more than one billion herring fish (averaging 6 to 8 inches in length) died and were fossilized within a four-square-mile area.
Spiritual Metaphor: The physical evidence of God's cataclysmic judgment is visible all around us in the earth. The flood is a real historical warning of judgment, but it also highlights the reality of God's salvation and covenant for those who believe.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles refers to the Miocene shale deposits found in Lompoc, California, which contain massive fossil beds of billions of herring. These fish are found in "mass mortality" layers, often preserved in pristine detail, sometimes even caught in the act of swallowing smaller fish. For such fossils to form, the organisms had to be buried instantly by a massive amount of sediment, preventing decay or scavenging. This geological evidence points to a sudden, catastrophic aqueous event rather than slow, uniform processes over millions of years.

Sermon Title: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Illustration Name: PBS Journey of Sperm and Egg
Tags: Biology, Birth, Children
Story Summary: Miles describes watching a PBS documentary called Life's Greatest Miracle about how babies are made. He explains the journey of the sperm swimming towards the egg. Inside the woman's tubes, tiny hairs called cilia wave back and forth, filtering the sperm. They check the sperm's DNA, and let only the healthy ones pass while holding back the rest. When the surviving sperm reach the egg, they hit a protective shell called the Zona. To get in, the sperm's head must perfectly match the shape of protein receptors sticking out of the Zona. When a match is made, a chemical reaction triggers, dissolving the head of the sperm to release a protein that burns a hole in the Zona, allowing entry and instantly closing the shell to others.
Spiritual Metaphor: This microscopic, highly complex process is scientifically undeniable yet mathematically impossible to attribute to random chance. It is a supernatural design. We must realize that our very existence is a miracle of God. When we face difficulties, we must thank God that we even survived the womb and are alive today.
Full Story Text: Drawing from the PBS documentary Life's Greatest Miracle, Pastor Miles recounts the harrowing and statistically improbable journey of a single sperm cell attempting to fertilize an egg. Out of millions, only one makes it past the various obstacles of the reproductive tract. The documentary captures the complexity and the "miracle" of life's beginning, showing how every detail must be perfectly synchronized for a new human being to be formed.

Sermon Title: Salvation in the Days of Noah
Tags: Sickness, Cross, Perspective
Story Summary: An inmate or contact recently gave Miles a metal nail the size of the ones used to crucify Jesus—nine inches long. Miles held it and immediately realized, "I have no problems." He suggests that if anyone brought their complaints to a counselor, and the counselor asked them to hold a nine-inch nail against their wrist and said they'd be nailed to a tree in an hour, their perspective would change instantly.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our daily complaints and problems are trivial and highly fixable compared to the agony Christ suffered to save us. Keeping our eyes on the cross eliminates self-pity and fills us with gratitude.
Full Story Text: To conclude the sermon, Pastor Miles describes the physical reality of the Roman crucifixion, specifically focusing on the nails used. He references the historical use of heavy, tapered iron nails, approximately nine inches in length. These were driven through the wrists and feet of the victim to secure them to the wooden cross. The sheer size and weight of such a nail paint a vivid, visceral picture of the agony involved in the execution.

Tags: Skills, Diving, Shark
Story Summary: While visiting his sister in Tortola for nine days, Pastor Miles and his family went snorkeling daily. Eager to go deeper, they took a beginner's scuba diving course. Before going down to 40 feet, they had to learn essential skills, such as how to clear water out of a mask underwater (fingers on top, blow out of nose, tilt head back) and how to recover a lost mouthpiece without swallowing water. While diving, they encountered a shark resting on the bottom. The instructor gave the hand sign for a shark (hand on head like a fin). He swam down and touched the shark, which jumped and swam away. Miles tried to touch it too, but the shark swam off before he could reach it.
Spiritual Metaphor: Extraordinary dreams require extraordinary skills. Just as diving in deep water requires mastery of specific survival skills beforehand, Joseph's dream only came true because he spent years in Egypt perfecting his skills in management and dream interpretation. We must identify and sharpen our God-given strengths.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shared a story about visiting his sister in Tortola, British Virgin Islands. During the trip, he participated in a scuba diving course. Part of the essential training involved learning specific hand signals for underwater communication. One particular sign taught was the signal for a shark: placing the hand vertically on top of the head like a dorsal fin. While actually diving in the ocean, the training became reality when Miles encountered a shark. He had to use that specific hand sign to alert the rest of his group.

Tags: Football, Sports, Hard Times
Story Summary: Miles shares his love for football, recalling playing running back in Pop Warner and high school. He notes that a running back's goal is the goal line, and they expect to get hit and knocked down along the way. Miles played alongside his friend Lorenzo. While he loved carrying the ball, he loved blocking for Lorenzo even more. If an opponent was standing between Lorenzo and the goal line, Miles would actively seek out the collision to knock them down. He notes that if you don't want to get hit, you should play basketball instead.
Spiritual Metaphor: In life and ministry, we cannot avoid obstacles, confrontations, or hard times. Instead of fearing them, we must welcome collisions as God's training ground to sharpen us, build our endurance, and prepare us for the next level.
Full Story Text: In his youth, Miles played Pop Warner football as a star running back. He was used to the glory of carrying the ball and scoring. However, his coach eventually gave him a different assignment: he was told he had to block for another player named Lorenzo. Miles initially resisted the idea, preferring the spotlight of being the primary runner. Despite his personal desires, he had to lower his shoulder and take the hits so that Lorenzo could find the opening to run.

Tags: Pride, Discipline, Correction
Story Summary: Miles asks the women in the audience if they have an emery board. They use its rough, abrasive surface to grind down corns, calluses, or rough heels on their feet.
Spiritual Metaphor: Our hard-headed pride and arrogance cannot be smoothed out by gentle means. God uses circumstances and other people—rough, abrasive tools—to grind down our rough edges. Just as "iron sharpens iron," we need other people to speak the truth in love and keep us sharp.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles used the physical example of an emery board and its effect on the skin. He described how over time, the heels of the feet can develop thick, hard, and rough calluses. To remove this dead skin and make the heel smooth again, one must use an abrasive emery board to sand it down. The process involves friction and the scraping away of the old, rough layers.

Tags: Preaching, MLK, Passion
Story Summary: Miles describes watching a video of Martin Luther King Jr. preaching in his church. King was so captured by the fire of his sermon that he actually walked away from the pulpit and continued preaching from the floor, entirely consumed by the passion of the message.
Spiritual Metaphor: Fulfilling a God-given dream requires absolute passion and fire. We must be so aligned with God's purpose that we are consumed by it, working diligently even when no one is looking.
Full Story Text: Miles reflected on the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. While Dr. King was a powerful and gifted preacher within the church, his dream for civil rights could not be fulfilled solely from behind the pulpit. He had to physically leave the safety and comfort of the church walls and go into the streets, organize boycotts, and march in the face of opposition.

Tags: Speech, Discipline, Humility
Story Summary: Miles recalls meeting with two coaches from Toastmasters about a year and a half ago. They explained that in Toastmasters meetings, speakers are fined a quarter every time they say "um" or "uh." Miles jokes that since that meeting, he hears a voice counting his "ums" every time he preaches, and he probably owes them $10,000. He contrasts this with an apologist debater he heard, who spoke flawlessly for 20 minutes in a high-pressure debate without saying "um" a single time.
Spiritual Metaphor: The process of training, refining your communication, and receiving critique is incredibly humbling. However, if we refuse to undergo the humbling process of feedback, we will never achieve the excellence required to stand before kings and fulfill our calling.
Full Story Text: Miles recounted his experience joining Toastmasters to improve his public speaking. The organization utilized a strict system of accountability. An "Ah-Counter" would listen to every speech, and the speaker was required to pay a quarter fine for every filler word used, such as "um," "ah," or "you know." Furthermore, he had to submit to a speech coach who would critique his delivery and force him to refine his technique.

Illustration Name: The Billy Graham Secret Service Bullet Shield
Tags: Sacrifice, Protection, Jesus
Story Summary: Prior to speaking at his Qualcomm Stadium Crusade, Pastor Miles got to spend a few minutes backstage with Billy Graham. Graham was very weak and traveling in a golf cart. As he got to the lift taking him to the stage, a Secret Service guard stood directly in front of him. Miles realized that this guard was positioned specifically to take a bullet for Graham in the event of an assassination attempt. Miles walked up to the guard, saying, "God bless you for what you do. If anyone wanted to shoot him, they'd have to shoot you first."
Spiritual Metaphor: This is a physical picture of Jesus Christ's protection and ultimate sacrifice. Christ took the "bullet" of sin and death for us on the cross, taking our place so that we could be saved and shielded from judgment.
Full Story Text: Prior to speaking at his Qualcomm Stadium Crusade, Pastor Miles got to spend a few minutes backstage with Billy Graham. Graham was very weak and traveling in a golf cart. As he got to the lift taking him to the stage, a Secret Service guard stood directly in front of him. Miles realized that this guard was positioned specifically to take a bullet for Graham in the event of an assassination attempt. Miles walked up to the guard, saying, "God bless you for what you do. If anyone wanted to shoot him, they'd have to shoot you first."

Tags: Identity, Misunderstanding
Story Summary: At the Qualcomm Stadium Billy Graham morning session, Pastor Miles met a performer, comedian, and ventriloquist who went by the name "Nana Pudding." Miles spent time trying to figure out what the name meant, assuming his grandmother called him "pudding." However, the performer corrected him: his grandmother used to make "banana pudding," which they couldn't pronounce as children, so it became "Nana Pudding."
Spiritual Metaphor: We often carry preconceived, incorrect ideas about who we are and what our names and purposes mean. But just as the comedian's grandma defined "Nana Pudding," God defines our true identity, name, and calling.
Full Story Text: At the Qualcomm Stadium Billy Graham morning session, Pastor Miles met a performer, comedian, and ventriloquist who went by the name "Nana Pudding." Miles spent time trying to figure out what the name meant, assuming his grandmother called him "pudding." However, the performer corrected him: his grandmother used to make "banana pudding," which they couldn't pronounce as children, so it became "Nana Pudding."

Illustration Name: The Closet Escape and the Realities of Motherhood
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Family, Children, Sacrifice
Story Summary: Pastor Miles reads a suggestion from a marriage retreat packet meant to spark romance: unannounced, pull your wife into a closet, hold her tight, and whisper, "Pack a light bag with some nice outfits. In the next 24 hours, you're going to be swept away to an undisclosed romantic location." Miles jokes that whoever wrote this has no concept of a mother's mind. For a mother to enjoy that, you must append: "And by the way, I have already arranged the food, packed the kids' vitamins, hired the babysitter, and left our contact number!"
Spiritual Metaphor: Husbands and families must recognize and honor the mental load, deep sacrifices, and constant responsibilities that mothers carry daily. True love and intimacy require sharing those burdens rather than adding to them.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles reads a suggestion from a marriage retreat packet meant to spark romance: unannounced, pull your wife into a closet, hold her tight, and whisper, "Pack a light bag with some nice outfits. In the next 24 hours, you're going to be swept away to an undisclosed romantic location." Miles jokes that whoever wrote this has no concept of a mother's mind. For a mother to enjoy that, you must append: "And by the way, I have already arranged the food, packed the kids' vitamins, hired the babysitter, and left our contact number!"

Illustration Name: The Duck and the 10 Chicks on a 50 mph Road
Tags: Protection, Guidance, Dependency
Story Summary: Pastor Miles was driving down a busy street where cars travel at 45 to 55 mph. He saw a mother duck crossing the road with at least ten baby chicks following behind her in a straight, single-file line. The duck line occupied almost three-quarters of a lane. A car sped by and nearly hit the mother duck, forcing her to flare her wings to protect her babies. Amazingly, they all made it safely across the busy street.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is a beautiful picture of Jesus' longing to gather us under His wings to protect us from the dangerous, high-speed elements of this world. Just as chicks survive by staying close in line behind their mother, we must stay dependent on and close behind Christ to remain safe under His care.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles was driving down a busy street where cars travel at 45 to 55 mph. He saw a mother duck crossing the road with at least ten baby chicks following behind her in a straight, single-file line. The duck line occupied almost three-quarters of a lane. A car sped by and nearly hit the mother duck, forcing her to flare her wings to protect her babies. Amazingly, they all made it safely across the busy street.

Illustration Name: The 21-Week-Old Fetus Grabbing the Surgeon's Finger
Tags: Creation, Love, Value, Sanctity of Life
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares an email he received about a surgeon who performs delicate in-utero operations. A fetus had a severe spinal condition (spina bifida). The surgeon opened the mother, lifted her uterus, opened it, and successfully operated on the 21-week-old baby inside. Right before closing the uterus, the tiny baby reached its hand out of the surgical opening and firmly grabbed the doctor's finger. A famous photograph captured this touching moment of the tiny hand gripping the adult finger.
Spiritual Metaphor: This illustrates that our lives are supernaturally formed and known by God from the womb. We are not accidents; we are fearfully and wonderfully made by a personal Creator who knows us intimately and feels our pain.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares an email he received about a surgeon who performs delicate in-utero operations. A fetus had a severe spinal condition (spina bifida). The surgeon opened the mother, lifted her uterus, opened it, and successfully operated on the 21-week-old baby inside. Right before closing the uterus, the tiny baby reached its hand out of the surgical opening and firmly grabbed the doctor's finger. A famous photograph captured this touching moment of the tiny hand gripping the adult finger.

Tags: Faith, Risk, Supernatural, Slang
Story Summary: Pastor Miles describes watching Stuart Scott on ESPN SportsCenter, who would call a spectacular home run or slam dunk a "baducka" slam (e.g. "show me the funk, baducka faith"). Miles uses this slang to define three tiers of faith: Baby Faith (taking very little risk because you have all the evidence), Medium / Risky Faith (taking some risk, knowing some answers but not all), and Baducka Faith (taking a massive leap of faith where, unless God performs a supernatural miracle, you will fail completely).
Spiritual Metaphor: God constantly challenges us to step out of our safe comfort zones and exercise "Baducka Faith" so that He can demonstrate His supernatural power.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles describes watching Stuart Scott on ESPN SportsCenter, who would call a spectacular home run or slam dunk a "baducka" slam (e.g. "show me the funk, baducka faith"). Miles uses this slang to define three tiers of faith:

Baby Faith: Taking very little risk because you have all the evidence.

Medium / Risky Faith: Taking some risk, knowing some answers but not all.

Baducka Faith: Taking a massive leap of faith where, unless God performs a supernatural miracle, you will fail completely.

Tags: Faithfulness, Running, Buffalo, Provision, Doubt
Story Summary: Miles was preparing for a massive youth crusade in Buffalo, New York, which cost $375,000 (the arena alone was $104,000). On the day of the crusade, they were short by $250,000. Miles went out for his customary run through the streets of Buffalo, crying out to God: "God, we are in serious trouble! We need a quarter of a million dollars!" God spoke to his heart and said: "Do you remember your first crusade? You didn't have the money. The second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth? You have never had the money! Why are you wasting your run complaining? I've always come through." Miles realized he had forgotten God's past faithfulness and focused entirely on the giant obstacle in front of him.
Spiritual Metaphor: When facing massive, seemingly impossible giants, we must remember and honor God's past victories in our lives. If He delivered us from the "lion and the bear" in the past, He will deliver us from the current giant.
Full Story Text: Miles was preparing for a massive youth crusade in Buffalo, New York, which cost $375,000 (the arena alone was $104,000). On the day of the crusade, they were short by $250,000. Miles went out for his customary run through the streets of Buffalo, crying out to God: "God, we are in serious trouble! We need a quarter of a million dollars!" God spoke to his heart and said: "Do you remember your first crusade? You didn't have the money. The second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth? You have never had the money! Why are you wasting your run complaining? I've always come through." Miles realized he had forgotten God's past faithfulness and focused entirely on the giant obstacle in front of him.

Illustration Name: John Kitna and the Cincinnati Bengals Turnaround
Tags: Football, Bengals, NFL, Calling, Platform
Story Summary: Miles visited and did chapel for the Cincinnati Bengals, whose quarterback was a dedicated Christian, Jon Kitna. The Bengals had been a laughingstock in last place for years. The team management told Kitna that if they didn't start winning, he would be benched regardless of how well he personally played. Instead of panicking, Kitna focused entirely on his calling to represent God on the team. Within a month, the Bengals went from last place to first place. Kitna told Miles: "I just want to continue to clarify why I'm on this team—it's not for football; it's for God." He also brought a skeptical teammate to chapel who ended up getting saved.
Spiritual Metaphor: When we focus entirely on our God-given calling and purpose, God can take our circumstances from "last place" to "first place" in an instant. Our platform (our job, sport, or business) is not our ultimate purpose; it is merely the vehicle God uses to let us represent Him.
Full Story Text: Miles visited and did chapel for the Cincinnati Bengals, whose quarterback was a dedicated Christian, Jon Kitna. The Bengals had been a laughingstock in last place for years. The team management told Kitna that if they didn't start winning, he would be benched regardless of how well he personally played. Instead of panicking, Kitna focused entirely on his calling to represent God on the team. Within a month, the Bengals went from last place to first place. Kitna told Miles: "I just want to continue to clarify why I'm on this team—it's not for football; it's for God." He also brought a skeptical teammate to chapel who ended up getting saved.

Tags: Compromise, Obedience, Relationships, Confrontation
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares about running into a couple at a local car wash. The man claimed to be walking closely with God, but was living unmarried with his girlfriend. Miles directly but lovingly confronted him, telling him he wasn't truly walking with God. The man admitted he was wrong, saying, "I know, I need to get back and do the right thing."
Spiritual Metaphor: Walking with God requires active obedience and alignment with His commands, not just verbal professions. True repentance means turning away from compromise and returning to God's standard.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares about running into a couple at a local car wash. The man claimed to be walking closely with God, but was living unmarried with his girlfriend. Miles directly but lovingly confronted him, telling him he wasn't truly walking with God. The man admitted he was wrong, saying, "I know, I need to get back and do the right thing."

Illustration Name: The Deaf Girl in Mexico and Pastor Mike McIntosh
Tags: Chapel, Mexico, Deaf, Healing, Miracles, Calling
Story Summary: In 1984, as a 24-year-old rookie defensive back with the Chargers, Miles McPherson attended team chapel. The guest speaker was Pastor Mike McIntosh, who arrived casually dressed in blue jeans and a Hawaiian shirt. McIntosh shared a story about a little deaf girl in Mexico who had her hearing miraculously restored after he prayed for her. This story moved Miles to tears and eventually led him to join Horizon Christian Fellowship, where he served on staff for 15 years.
Spiritual Metaphor: God uses casual, authentic, and simple presentations of the gospel to pierce through the outer defenses of even the toughest hearts. A genuine, Spirit-led message has the power to not only touch emotions but completely redirect a person's life's calling.
Full Story Text:
In 1984, when Miles McPherson was a 24-year-old rookie defensive back with the San Diego Chargers, he attended the team's weekly chapel services. The chapel speakers varied widely, bringing everything from animals and guitars to cowboys hats. One week, the guest speaker was Pastor Mike McIntosh (founder of Horizon Christian Fellowship). McIntosh arrived dressed very casually in blue jeans, sneakers, and a Hawaiian shirt. Miles sat in the back of the chapel, initially wondering why the speaker was not dressed more formally for a church event, though he found himself comforted by how relaxed and approachable McIntosh was.

During his sermon, McIntosh shared a story about ministering in Mexico. He met a little deaf girl who wore pigtails and a red dress. McIntosh described putting his fingers into the girl's ears and praying for her, after which her hearing was miraculously restored. Miles sat in the back of the room with his hands covering his face, weeping as the Holy Spirit touched him. This encounter ultimately led Miles to join McIntosh's church, eventually serving on the pastoral staff there for 15 years.

Tags: La Mesa, Hitchhiker, Angels, Verification, Message
Story Summary: A close friend of Miles felt prompted by the Lord to pick up a homeless man walking slowly in pouring rain in La Mesa. Initially resisting, the friend drove home but returned after the prompt persisted. The passenger, who was missing one eye, immediately praised the Lord and spent the entire 5-mile drive sharing deep spiritual insights. After being dropped off at a busy intersection, the man instantly vanished.
Spiritual Metaphor: This encounter demonstrates Hebrews 13:2—that we may entertain angels without knowing it. God places specific, supernatural messengers in our lives to humble us, redirect us, and restore our failing faith, often hiding them in packages our flesh would naturally avoid or fear.
Full Story Text:
A close friend of Pastor Miles was driving up a street in La Mesa, California, during a pouring rainstorm. As he drove, he saw a homeless-looking man walking very slowly down the street in the drenching rain. He felt a strong prompt from the Lord to pick the man up and give him a ride, but he resisted, thinking about how his car would get soaked and how dangerous it might be. He kept driving all the way home. However, as soon as he pulled into his driveway, the prompt from the Lord came again, even stronger: "Go back and pick that dude up."

Obeying the second prompt, the friend drove back. Five minutes had passed, yet the man was only five feet further from where he had first seen him, still walking at an incredibly slow pace. The friend rolled down his window and told the man to get in. When the man sat in the car, the friend turned and noticed that the passenger was missing one eye. Immediately, the passenger said, "Praise the Lord, thanks for the ride." When asked where he was heading, the man replied, "San Diego State." Given how slowly he was walking, it would have taken him years to cover that distance.

During the entire five-mile drive to SDSU, the one-eyed man spoke deeply about Jesus, God, heaven, and spiritual realities. Miles' friend, who was severely struggling in his faith at the time, was deeply humbled and restored by the man's words. The friend pulled into a u-turn lane at the intersection of Montezuma Road and College Avenue to drop him off. He let the man out in the middle of the intersection and completed his u-turn. When he turned his head a split second later to look for the slow-walking man, the man had completely vanished into thin air. There was nowhere he could have walked in that short time.

Illustration Name: The Nutcracker Ballet and the Girls' Angels
Tags: Spreckels Theater, Children, Angels, Warning, Protection
Story Summary: While attending his daughter's Nutcracker performance at the Spreckels Theater, Miles sat behind a group of talkative eight-year-old girls. An observer (a schoolteacher) looked at him with extreme suspicion, making Miles feel self-conscious. Once Miles explained he was a choir member at the Rock Church, the teacher warmly recognized him. Miles noted that every child has a ministering angel that reports to the Father.
Spiritual Metaphor: Children are precious to God, and their angels always behold the Father's face (Matthew 18:10). This reminds us of our own responsibility to serve as "ministering spirits" in the lives of others, delivering kind words, prayers, and encouragement whenever and wherever God sends us on an assignment.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles went to see his daughter perform in the Nutcracker ballet at the Spreckels Theater in San Diego. The theater was completely packed with children, mostly seven- to ten-year-olds. Miles sat in a section right behind a group of eight-year-old girls. The girls kept giggling, whispering, and looking back at Miles, so he started talking to them. One of the girls spoke a single sentence that took a full seven minutes to say because she spoke so fast, and another girl playfully stuck her tongue out at him.

As Miles sat talking to the girls, he noticed a schoolteacher sitting two rows in front of them, looking back at him with extreme suspicion. In his mind, Miles grew self-conscious, worrying that the teacher might think he was a suspicious stranger and call the police. Finally, after getting the teacher's attention, Miles mentioned, "I go to the Rock Church and I'm in the choir." The teacher's face instantly lit up, and she turned to the girls, saying, "Oh! That's the man I told you about who likes the Discovery Channel!" Miles laughed, but noted that every single one of those little girls has an angel that directly reports to the Father in heaven.

Tags: Wrong Number, Calling, Phone, Miracle, Salvation
Story Summary: Miles called a friend but dialed the wrong number. The man who answered asked who it was, and when Miles identified himself, the man gasped, explaining he had attended Miles' Christmas Eve service four years ago and hadn't been back since. Miles prayed with him over his current struggles and invited him back for the upcoming Christmas Eve service, which fell on the exact four-year anniversary.
Spiritual Metaphor: God frequently orchestrates seemingly random "wrong numbers" and accidental situations to pursue those who are running or drifting from Him. We must remain spiritually sensitive and available to be used as God's instruments, recognizing that there are no coincidences in the Kingdom of God.
Full Story Text:
Pastor Miles called a friend but accidentally dialed the wrong number. When a man answered, Miles asked, "Is John there?" The man said no, but instead of hanging up, the man asked, "Who is this?" Miles replied, "This is Miles." To his surprise, the man gasped and said, "I know you! I came to a church service you spoke at on Christmas Eve four years ago, and I've been telling everyone about it ever since—but I haven't been back."

Miles laughed and replied, "You've been talking about it for four years and you haven't been back?" He realized their phone numbers were almost identical. Sensing a divine appointment, Miles told him, "You know God is after you, right? This is not a coincidence." Miles then prayed with the man over the phone for the struggles he was going through, and invited him to return to the Rock Church for the upcoming Christmas Eve service, which happened to be the exact four-year anniversary of his last visit.

Illustration Name: The Phabulous Pharisees and Leather Boxes
Tags: Pharisees, Hypocrisy, Appearance, Head Box, Heart
Story Summary: Miles explains the historical character of the Pharisees, who wore special robes and leather straps with small boxes (phylacteries) containing Scripture on their foreheads and wrists. This was an outward, showy sign of holiness. Miles jokes that this is the equivalent of a modern pastor strapping a giant physical Bible to his head with duct tape.
Spiritual Metaphor: This illustrates the danger of hollow, outward religious performance. Pharisees focused on physical, mechanical representations of holiness while their hearts were far from God. God doesn't want showy, superficial displays of piety; He desires a "circumcised heart"—an inward reality where our love for Him is authentic.
Full Story Text:
In his message, Pastor Miles describes the historical character of the Pharisees, whom he calls the "Phabulous Pharisees." The word Pharisee literally means "separated one." They were highly educated, strictly trained rabbis who wore special, elaborate robes to signal their holiness to everyone who saw them. As part of their outward display of piety, they wore a leather strap wrapped around their heads with a small, domed leather box (called a phylactery) positioned directly on their foreheads. Inside this box was a small scroll containing the written word of God. They wore an identical leather strap and box wrapped around their wrists.

These boxes were meant to represent that the word of God was constantly on their minds and guiding their physical actions. Miles jokes that this would be the modern equivalent of a pastor walking around with a giant physical Bible strapped directly to his head with duct tape, telling everyone, "Look how holy I am!"

Tags: Coaching, Youth Football, Sanctuary, Vulnerability, Rest
Story Summary: Miles admits that being "Pastor Miles" 24/7 is a heavy and exhausting burden, and sometimes he just wants to be "Miles." He notes that coaching youth football is a major blessing because the players and coaches treat him simply as "Coach," allowing him to drop his public guard and find a temporary sanctuary.
Spiritual Metaphor: Even the most committed spiritual leaders experience weariness and the desire to hide from public expectations. Pressing on doesn't require us to be superhuman or perfect; it requires us to acknowledge our limits, find healthy outlets, and force our flesh to remain obedient even when we feel spiritually exhausted.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares a vulnerable moment, admitting that being "Pastor Miles" 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, is an incredibly heavy and exhausting burden. There are times when he does not want to carry the public expectations of being a senior pastor; he just wants to be "Miles." He explains that coaching youth football is a profound blessing for him because the kids and other coaches on the field do not treat him like a spiritual figurehead—they simply call him "Coach." This role allows him to step outside of his pastoral identity, drop his guard, and find a temporary sanctuary where he can just be himself.

Tags: Pizza Hut, Cheerleaders, Curls, Appearance, Authenticity, Character
Story Summary: During a pizza party at Pizza Hut after a game, Miles watched the youth cheerleaders. Throughout the season, he had admired their flawless, perfectly bouncy curls. He was shocked when several cheerleaders walked past his table holding their "perfect curls" in their hands, revealing they were fake clip-on hairpieces.
Spiritual Metaphor: It is easy to maintain a perfect, styled "religious appearance" when life is going well and everyone is watching (clip-on curls). The true test of our faith and character is what happens when life gets messy, painful, and difficult—when our appearances are stripped away, and our "curls are gone."
Full Story Text:
After a youth football game on a Saturday, one of the team dads treated the entire group of 70 cheerleaders and football players to a pizza party at Pizza Hut. Pastor Miles sat in the restaurant watching the cheerleaders. Throughout the entire football season, he had quietly admired how these young girls managed to have absolutely flawless, bounce-free, perfect curls in their hair for every single game. He assumed they must spend hours getting their hair styled.

However, as he sat eating his pizza, he was shocked to see several cheerleaders walk past his table holding their perfect curls in their hands! It was only then that he realized the flawless hair curls were actually fake hairpieces clipped onto their heads.

Tags: New Haven, Yale, Running, Pride, Asthma, Perseverance, Discipline
Story Summary: During his college years, Miles challenged himself to run a grueling, hilly 2-3 mile course in New Haven with the varsity baseball team. Suffering from exercise-induced asthma, Miles faced intense physical agony when the team's leader took off at a full sprint. Refusing to lose to a baseball player, Miles pressed through the burning pain to finish tied for first.
Spiritual Metaphor: Running the Christian life is a grueling, painful, and disciplined race that requires us to push past physical and emotional agony. To finish well, we must run in a way to win, disciplining our bodies, bringing our fleshly desires into subjection, and refusing to allow pain to stop us.
Full Story Text:
During his college years, the university's varsity baseball team was known for being in spectacular athletic conditioning. Their standard training regimen included running a grueling, hilly two-to-three-mile course through the steep neighborhood streets of New Haven, Connecticut. Priding himself on his own elite physical fitness, Miles asked the baseball players if he could run the course with them. They agreed, and Miles set out with the team.

Miles suffered from exercise-induced asthma, which he knew would make the hilly run extremely painful. As soon as they stepped outside the gym doors, the baseball team's leader took off at a full sprint. They soon reached a hill so steep it felt completely vertical. As they ran, Miles' lungs began to burn intensely, his asthma flared up, and his body screamed in agony. However, his athletic pride kicked in, and he repeated to himself: "There is no way on earth I am losing to a baseball player. That is completely unacceptable." Refusing to quit or even slow down, Miles pressed through the excruciating pain, matching the leader step-for-step, and finished the run tied for first place.

Sermon Title: Why Bad Things Happen To Good People - Part 1
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Children, Parenting, Discipline
Story Summary: Pastor Miles shares his parental strategy for when his young son falls down, cries, or gets hurt. Miles does not rush to pick him up right away (unlike his wife, Debbie, who immediately runs over crying, "Oh, booy baby!"). Miles has a "30-second to one-minute rule" to let him cry. He explains that the first 30 seconds are just fear, initial pain, and checking to see who is coming to help. If the child is still crying after a full minute, then Miles knows it's serious and needs attention.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is how God often deals with our trials. When we experience initial pain or a setback, our first reaction is fear, panic, and seeking attention. God doesn't always rush to instantly rescue us; He lets us process our fear, teaching us to grow strong and resilient through minor injuries.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles shares his parental strategy for when his young son falls down, cries, or gets hurt. Miles does not rush to pick him up right away (unlike his wife, Debbie, who immediately runs over crying, "Oh, booy baby!"). Miles has a "30-second to one-minute rule" to let him cry. He explains that the first 30 seconds are just fear, initial pain, and checking to see who is coming to help. If the child is still crying after a full minute, then Miles knows it's serious and needs attention.

Sermon Title: Why Bad Things Happen To Good People - Part 1
Tags: Sickness, Pain, Perseverance
Story Summary: During his son's baseball practice, a 12-year-old boy fell in center field and refused to get up. The coach asked Miles to go check on him. Miles walked over and found the boy crying and blowing on his knee. Looking closely, Miles saw there was no torn ligament; the boy had simply re-injured a dry, old scab on his leg. He had just "cracked the seal" on the old scab, causing it to sting slightly. Miles stood over him and told him simply: "Get up. That's part of being a boy. You're twelve, this is normal."
Spiritual Metaphor: Many of the "monstrous" problems we complain about are just re-opened old emotional scabs from our past (a broken relationship, a past insult, a minor failure). We blow on them and act like our life is over, but God looks at us and says, "Get up, shake it off, this is a normal part of life. You have the strength to handle it."
Full Story Text: During his son's baseball practice, a 12-year-old boy fell in center field and refused to get up. The coach asked Miles to go check on him. Miles walked over and found the boy crying and blowing on his knee. Looking closely, Miles saw there was no torn ligament; the boy had simply re-injured a dry, old scab on his leg. He had just "cracked the seal" on the old scab, causing it to sting slightly. Miles stood over him and told him simply: "Get up. That's part of being a boy. You're twelve, this is normal."

Sermon Title: Why Bad Things Happen To Good People - Part 1
Illustration Name: The "Curse God to His Face" Agenda
Tags: Satan, Temptation, Battle
Story Summary: Miles explains that the devil (referred to by his many names: abaddon, liar, Beelzebub - "Lord of the Flies") has a very specific agenda behind every trial. Satan didn't want Job's animals or family destroyed just for the sake of destruction; he did it so that Job would "curse God to His face."
Spiritual Metaphor: Every major problem in our lives—losing a job, getting a disease, going through a breakup—is weaponized by the enemy for one purpose: to get us to curse God, doubt His love, and walk away from Him. The devil wants us to believe that God is a liar or doesn't care. Our battle is not against the physical problem (the job, the boss, the sickness); the battle is to remain firmly under God's protective wings and refuse to doubt His goodness.
Full Story Text: Miles explains that the devil (referred to by his many names: abaddon, liar, Beelzebub - "Lord of the Flies") has a very specific agenda behind every trial. Satan didn't want Job's animals or family destroyed just for the sake of destruction; he did it so that Job would "curse God to His face."

Sermon Title: Why Bad Things Happen To Good People - Part 1
Tags: Sickness, Hardship, Trial, Perspective
Story Summary: Miles describes the incredible, dramatic timing of the bad news delivered to Job. Job had a massive estate, and the devil orchestrated four distinct tragedies to strike almost simultaneously. One servant ran to Job to say the Sabeans took the oxen and killed the servants. While he was still speaking, a second ran to say fire from heaven consumed the sheep. While he was still speaking, a third ran to say the Chaldeans raided the camels. While he was still speaking, a fourth ran to say a wind collapsed the house on his children. Job had four guys running to him in a timed relay of absolute devastation, waiting to see if he would crack.
Spiritual Metaphor: Life's trials often hit us in overwhelming, rapid-fire succession—one bad news report after another, leaving us dazed. The enemy times these blows perfectly to crush our faith. But like Job, we must respond to the rapid-fire bad news by falling to our knees and saying, "The Lord gave, and the Lord has taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord."
Full Story Text: Miles describes the incredible, dramatic timing of the bad news delivered to Job. Job had a massive estate, and the devil orchestrated four distinct tragedies to strike almost simultaneously. One servant ran to Job to say the Sabeans took the oxen and killed the servants. While he was still speaking, a second ran to say fire from heaven consumed the sheep. While he was still speaking, a third ran to say the Chaldeans raided the camels. While he was still speaking, a fourth ran to say a wind collapsed the house on his children. Job had four guys running to him in a timed relay of absolute devastation, waiting to see if he would crack.

Sermon Title: Why Bad Things Happen to Good People - Part 4
Illustration Name: The Pleiades and Orion Light Year Scale
Tags: Creation, Stars, Sovereignty, Perspective
Story Summary: In Job 38, God asks Job if he can "bind the cluster of Pleiades or loose the belt of Orion." Miles explains that Pleiades is a cluster of stars located 400 light-years away (light traveling at 186,000 miles per second for 400 years!). Orion contains three massive stars forming its belt and a giant nebula where new stars are born.
Spiritual Metaphor: We get consumed with our minor, local problems (unpaid bills, minor insults, personal worries) as if they are the center of the universe. God points us to the cosmos He constructed with his fingers to remind us of His infinite, mind-boggling scale. If He can manage stars light-years away, He can easily handle our small local problems.
Full Story Text:
The Story:In Job 38, God asks Job if he can "bind the cluster of Pleiades or loose the belt of Orion." Miles explains that Pleiades is a cluster of stars located 400 light-years away (light traveling at 186,000 miles per second for 400 years!). Orion contains three massive stars forming its belt and a giant nebula where new stars are born.

Sermon Title: Why Bad Things Happen to Good People - Part 4
Illustration Name: The 10-Foot Bald Eagle and the Broken Arm
Tags: Creation, Nature, Design, Strength
Story Summary: Miles details the awesome biology of the eagle. There are 150 kinds of eagles, with wingspans up to ten feet. Their nests can be eight feet wide and weigh over 12 tons (24,000 lbs). The eagle flies at 100 mph, slamming into prey with claws that act like sharp fish hooks. A man holding an eagle had his arm broken in two places simply because the eagle clamped down with its powerful claws.
Spiritual Metaphor: This is a picture of God's incredible, majestic design in creation. God designed the eagle's claws, its eyes to see a fish from a mile away, and its wings to glide on wind currents. When we look at the power and detail of His creation, we realize we can trust Him with the intricate details of our lives.
Full Story Text: Miles details the awesome biology of the eagle. There are 150 kinds of eagles, with wingspans up to ten feet. Their nests can be eight feet wide and weigh over 12 tons (24,000 lbs). The eagle flies at 100 mph, slamming into prey with claws that act like sharp fish hooks. A man holding an eagle had his arm broken in two places simply because the eagle clamped down with its powerful claws.

Sermon Title: Why Bad Things Happen to Good People - Part 4
Illustration Name: The Woodpecker's Head-Shock Absorber and Wrap-Around Tongue
Tags: Creation, Design, Sickness, Protection
Story Summary: Miles describes the woodpecker. When a woodpecker pecks a tree, it hits with 1,000 times the force of gravity. If its head weren't engineered with a special skull structure to distribute the shock around its brain, its head would explode on the first peck. Furthermore, its extremely long tongue wraps completely around the inside of its skull, anchoring in its nostril, so it can extend deep into the tree to catch bugs.
Spiritual Metaphor: Every single creature, no matter how small, is meticulously engineered with perfect, supernatural mechanisms to survive and thrive. This is not the result of random evolution. If God spent so much detail designing a woodpecker's tongue and shock-absorbing head, how much more care has He put into designing, protecting, and preserving your life through hard times.
Full Story Text: Miles describes the woodpecker. When a woodpecker pecks a tree, it hits with 1,000 times the force of gravity. If its head weren't engineered with a special skull structure to distribute the shock around its brain, its head would explode on the first peck. Furthermore, its extremely long tongue wraps completely around the inside of its skull, anchoring in its nostril, so it can extend deep into the tree to catch bugs.

Tags: Faith, Healing, Miracles, Trust
Story Summary: During a mission trip to Mexico, Pastor Miles McPherson prayed for a little girl suffering from severe ear infections whose ears were biologically malformed with two distinct ear-hole openings on each side. Inspired by Jesus, Miles put his fingers into her ears and prayed. Later, the medical doctor discovered she was completely healed, possessing only one perfect, normal ear-hole on each side.
Spiritual Metaphor: This physical, biological healing was an undeniable miracle of flesh and bone. Miles uses it to show that if God has the supernatural power to physically remodel a little girl's ears, we can completely trust Him to repair and resolve any complex, seemingly impossible life problems we face.
Full Story Text: During a mission trip to Mexico, Pastor Miles McPherson was asked by a medical doctor to pray for a little girl suffering from a severe, chronic ear infection. The girl's ears were biologically malformed; she possessed two separate ear-hole/eardrum openings on each side of her head instead of one. Inspired by Jesus' biblical method of healing—putting His fingers into a deaf man's ears—Miles put his fingers into the little girl's ears and prayed for her. Later, the clinical doctor called Miles back in absolute amazement. Upon re-examination, the doctor discovered that her ears had been completely healed, and she now possessed only one perfect, normal ear-hole on each side.

Tags: Apologetics, Creation, Evolution, Design
Story Summary: Pastor Miles holds up his digital wristwatch (which has various alarm and stopwatch functions) and asks if anyone walking along the beach would ever believe that the sand, ocean waves, and seaweed pounding on the surf for 40 trillion years naturally put the watch together. No one would believe that random ocean activity could assemble a watch.
Spiritual Metaphor: Yet, our culture readily accepts that human beings—who are infinitely more complex than a watch, with eyes, brains, lungs, and the capacity to think, feel, and love—are the result of random evolutionary chance. We are meticulously designed by a Master Creator, and denying His design is intellectually dishonest.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles holds up his digital wristwatch (which he jokingly complains about because the date is wrong and it has various alarm/stopwatch functions). He asks the congregation a logical question: if they were walking along the beach and found a highly complex digital wristwatch, would they ever believe that the sand, ocean waves, and seaweed pounding on the surf for 40 trillion years naturally put the watch together? No one in their right mind would believe that random ocean activity could assemble a watch.

Illustration Name: The "Shut Up to Your Father" Lesson
Tags: Family, Parenting, Consequence, Judgment
Story Summary: Pastor Miles recounts a conversation he had with his son about a neighborhood kid who was a "brat" and told his own father to "shut up." Miles asked his son what would be the "therefore" if he hypothetically said that to him. His son instantly rattled off severe consequences (getting grounded, a spanking, tied up outside, held underwater).
Spiritual Metaphor: Every action in life has an immediate "therefore"—an inescapable consequence or result. If we choose to live in disobedience to God, we cannot escape the "therefore" of His spiritual laws and eventual judgment. We must recognize the severity of our rebellion and repent.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles recounts a conversation he had with his son about a neighborhood kid who was a "brat" and had told his own father to "shut up." Miles recognized this as a vital teaching moment. He asked his son: "If you hypothetically said 'shut up' to your father, what would be the 'therefore'?" His son immediately rattled off the consequences: "Oh man, I'd get grounded, a spanking, tied up outside for a week, and held underwater for five minutes!" The child instantly understood the severe, inevitable consequences of disobedience.

Illustration Name: Chuck Norris and the POW Rat
Tags: Hell, Judgment, Severity
Story Summary: Miles describes a scene from the movie Missing in Action, where Chuck Norris' character is captured in a POW camp and tortured. The captors place an angry, giant rat in a sack and tie it onto his head as he hangs upside down. Blood seeps through the sack, but when they remove it, Chuck Norris is holding the dead rat in his mouth, having killed it with his teeth.
Spiritual Metaphor: As intense, brutal, and terrifying as earthly warfare and torture can be, it is absolutely nothing compared to the severity of the eternal judgment of God. In hell, there is weeping and gnashing of teeth with no rest, which is why we must urgently share the gospel and lead people to repentance.
Full Story Text: Miles describes a scene from the movie Missing in Action, where Chuck Norris' character is captured in a POW camp and tortured. The captors place an angry, giant rat in a sack and tie it onto his head as he hangs upside down. Blood seeps through the sack, but when they remove it, Chuck Norris is holding the dead rat in his mouth, having killed it with his teeth.

Illustration Name: The Malvern Mules Blow-Pop Pep Rally
Tags: Youth, Humility, Identity, Purpose
Story Summary: When Miles was in high school, he won a ticket-selling competition for a pep rally. When he got to the gym, they placed Miles—who was 5'11", 155 lbs, with a massive afro looking like a blow-pop—on top of a live mule (the school mascot) and pulled him around the gym. He felt incredibly foolish waving to the laughing crowd.
Spiritual Metaphor: Miles entered the gym feeling proud but rode out looking like a fool. In contrast, the donkey that carried Jesus into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday entered as an obscure, unused animal but rode out as a hero because it was set apart to carry the King of Kings. God wants to take us out of our obscurity and old lifestyles, set us apart, and use us for the greatest purposes in His Kingdom.
Full Story Text: When Pastor Miles McPherson was in high school, he won a ticket-selling competition for a pep rally, selling the most tickets at a dollar each. He eagerly anticipated being honored. However, when he arrived at the gym, he discovered a live mule (the school mascot was the Malvern Mules). The organizers placed Miles—who was 5'11", 155 lbs, and had a massive afro looking like a "blow-pop"—on top of this mule and paraded him around the gym. Within three seconds, Miles realized how incredibly foolish he looked waving to the laughing crowd.

Illustration Name: Mission to Mars and the Hometown Buffet
Tags: Wife, Marriage, Family
Story Summary: Pastor Miles took his son to see the movie Mission to Mars while his daughters were at camp, leaving only one child at home. The movie portrayed humans evolving from alien-seeded DNA. Afterwards, they went to Hometown Buffet, where Miles observed people gorging themselves on the all-you-can-eat food and walking out stuffed, bloated, and in physical pain.
Spiritual Metaphor: The secular world invents wild evolutionary theories to avoid admitting accountability to a Creator. Similarly, people try to satisfy their inner emptiness by stuffing themselves with worldly pleasures at the "buffet of life," leaving them bloated and spiritually starving. Only a personal relationship with Jesus satisfies the soul.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles took his son to see the movie Mission to Mars. The film depicted human origins as starting when an alien on Mars released DNA that flew through space, hit Earth, and evolved from an amoeba into a frog, then a cow, a buffalo, and finally humans. After the movie, they went to Hometown Buffet, where Miles observed people gorging themselves on the all-you-can-eat spread, walking out stuffed, bloated, and physically hurting.

Tags: Youth, Catholicism, Holiness
Story Summary: Miles grew up attending Catholic school for eight years and recalls dipping his hand in the holy water font to cross himself without ever understanding what made the water "holy" or why he was doing it. Years later, he learned that the water was holy simply because it had been formally set aside for baptisms to symbolize ceremonial washing.
Spiritual Metaphor: In Greek, "holy" means to be set apart for one specific purpose. Just as ordinary water is made "holy" by being set aside strictly for baptism, God wants to cleanse us of our sinful pasts, set us apart from the world, and make us holy vessels dedicated exclusively to His divine service.
Full Story Text: Miles grew up attending a Catholic school for eight years, wearing a green suit, plaid tie, and penny loafers. He vividly remembers entering the church and dipping his hand into the holy water font to cross himself, without ever understanding what made the water "holy" or why he was doing it. Years later, he learned that the water was holy simply because it had been formally set aside for baptisms to symbolize ceremonial washing.

Tags: Surrender, Stewardship, Obedience
Story Summary: Pastor Miles points out that throughout His earthly life, Jesus continually relied on borrowed things: He preached from a borrowed boat, rested in Lazarus' borrowed house, ate the Passover in a borrowed room, rode into Jerusalem on a borrowed colt, and was eventually buried in a borrowed tomb.
Spiritual Metaphor: Jesus does not need to own worldly goods because He owns everything. Today, He wants to "borrow" your life, your home, your car, your talents, and your heart for His work. When we reluctantly withhold our resources, time, or finances from Him, we are fighting against the very purpose for which He set us apart.
Full Story Text: Pastor Miles points out that throughout His earthly life, Jesus continually relied on borrowed things: He preached from a borrowed boat, rested in Lazarus' borrowed house, ate the Passover in a borrowed room, rode into Jerusalem on a borrowed colt, and was eventually buried in a borrowed tomb.
